“I’ll grab new blankets from the basement, mi amor, ” her voice was quiet, almost nervous to disrupt the piece. 

Agatha felt the smile back on her face as she reached forward, unbuckling the strap from her wife’s hips, “Your phone’s on the counter. Don’t go in the dark.” 

The strap fell from Rio’s thighs and she turned, grabbing a candle holder to make her way towards their bedroom. She set the strap aside on the bathroom counter, figuring they would wash it later and using the toilet before walking back out to find her wife just coming back up their stairs with a pile of fresh blankets, her phone in hand as Agatha set down the candle holder on the coffee table. 

Rio spread the blankets out, piling them together until they created a makeshift bed before the fire. Agatha plopped back down, her hands already reaching for the unfinished blanket on the couch cushions. She smiled softly as hands pulled her to lay her back against a warm chest facing the fire, Rio using the front of the couch to hold them up. A comforting silence stretched between them, hands on her stomach as she started to work with only the light from the fire and the candles around them.  

Agatha counted the stitches as she went, spinning the fresh wool into an azalea pattern onto the green crocheted blanket she had already begun. Rio had bought her more wool from the market, in fact, she actively had to stop Rio from buying anything Agatha had commented on. It was sweet and so utterly her wife. Her wife bought gifts for their friends, a couple things that she wasn’t allowed to see including the green tote bag she had found on her wife when she was talking to the physics professor– Vincent or Victor– whatever. 

 

Vernon? Vhagar? was kind to her wife and to her surprise her wife, for once, was nice in return. She didn’t bite his head off or scare him away, and when Rio introduced her to him as her wife, Agatha’s heart had swelled with pride. She would never admit it to her wife, but something in her always swam when she was called hers , when she was called Rio’s. Vito seemed like a kind of man who wouldn’t gossip, so maybe that’s why her wife finally decided to let their secret out, but then her gaze had shifted– bright red hair and wide fearful green eyes staring at them. She had almost laughed– her darling possessive wife always knew how to make a scene. In truth, it was relieving, finally being able to let the redhead know that she was taken, instead of her getting longing looks from the woman like she was a kicked puppy. To the gods it was annoying, and there were multiple times Agatha had sat at their altar, wondering whether or not she should hex the bitch out of spite. She didn’t, knowing their proximity was too close, and she couldn’t afford that negative energy while with child. 

Agatha lifted her head, surveying their living room. In the corner sat the proud tall Yule tree. Rio had pranced around the tree selection like a love-struck reindeer before she changed, studying the trees like she was sizing them up whether they should be hired to work for her. It took an hour until Rio finally picked their tree, and by then, Agatha wanted to kill her– well, until the little shit opened her mouth and said the tree had to be perfect for the baby. And Agatha had simply melted. 

She got their bags in the car as Rio and a worker hauled the tree into her truck bed and they were off. When they came home, her wife had yelled at her to sit down while she took care of the tree. She had rolled her eyes, knowing very well she could help her carry it in, but Rio wasn’t having it. So, Agatha for once in her life, didn’t fight her– but not because she wanted to give in to her wife, but because she was hungry and her cravings wanted the fresh roasted chestnuts her wife had bought her. 

By the time Rio had managed to set up the tree, pine needles littered the floor. Her wife had tracked in mud and snow, marking up her hardwood floors and staining the carpet. Any other day, Agatha would have been pissed, but she had simply sat back on the couch, finishing the last of the roasted nuts in retaliation. Rio had scowled, but effectively said nothing and began cleaning up the mess she made. 

She had made cider while Rio went to the basement again, grabbing their ornaments for the tree when the lights above flickered and fully went out, plunging the house in darkness. She had groaned and heard Rio yell from the stairs as she probably tripped on something. Her wife came up the stairs, marching outside to her greenhouse. Agatha wasn’t in the least bit of surprise, as her wife fussed over her plants in that greenhouse, and once again, Agatha had patted herself on the back for gifting Rio it for their ten year anniversary. As much as Rio was a lovergirl behind closed doors, Agatha could easily match her. 

While her wife checked the power in the greenhouse, Agatha had gathered their blankets and placed them in the living room. Soon, Rio came back with an armful of firewood and built them a fire. She had shrugged off her coat and shirt, leaving her in a sports bra, the tattoo moving with her muscles– well, one thing led to another and as much as she wanted to trace her hands over the design while her wife took her, something else wanted Rio inside her. She didn’t know exactly why, but everything in her craved the feeling and she did promise her wife she could sit back and relax– who was Agatha Harkness-Vidal if she didn’t live up to her promises to her wife. 

 

She lost count when lips pressed against her head and she made a slight noise of protest, grumbling slightly as her fingers went back, trying to recount but her wife shifted behind her, reaching for the green tote bag on the couch. 

“I got you something, mi amor, ” Rio’s voice was impossibly soft, and hesitant. 

That hesitancy made Agatha freeze, setting the yarn and needle aside. For a moment, she wondered if Rio went against their rules, the number one, most important unspoken rule: don’t acknowledge or celebrate Agatha’s birthday. She hated the way they loomed like an unavoidable storm, the way they curled at the edges of her year, tainting everything in their wake. She hated the way people said “Happy Birthday” with careless ease, as if the words weren’t bitter, as if they didn’t choke her. 

Evanora had never let her be happy. So, Agatha had stopped counting after a while. What was the point? Every birthday was the same– a quiet reminder that she was an obligation, a mistake, a burden to be shaped into something useful. If she was lucky, Evanora ignored the day entirely, barely sparing her a glance. If she was unlucky, it became another lesson in power, in control, in breaking apart only to be built again in her mother’s image. She remembered the first time she had dared to wish for something– a cake, nothing extravagant, just something sweet, something hers. She had been small, young enough to still believe that birthdays were meant to be celebrated. 

Evanora had sneered, “You think you deserve that?”

And just like that, the idea of birthdays soured, curdled into something ugly, something she wanted nothing to do with. Every year after that was another mark, another tally of survival. That’s all birthdays were– proof that she was still here, despite everything. So no, she didn’t celebrate. She didn’t count down the days. She didn’t let anyone acknowledge it, not even Rio.

Rio seemed to have sensed that when she was around for Agatha’s birthday. Somehow, Rio knew to treat it like any other day, except Agatha noticed the extra cuddling, the lingering kisses, and if they had made love on that day, how Rio would give and give and give. She quickly realized it wasn’t fully because Rio wanted to celebrate her birthday, it was because Rio wanted to show her how loved she was by her. So, she would let her, just no gifts, no cakes or candles, or even an acknowledgement. 

Rio was a different story, and for once, Agatha was happy to plan gifts and surprises for her wife. Her wife was simple, to her luck. A single cupcake to share– or rather a couple of beignets with a few candles and a few gifts she knew her wife would love. Dinner was always to Rio’s favorite Puerto Rican restaurant about two hours away. And sometimes, when they didn’t want to make the drive back, they would book a hotel or motel, and fuck, it was rough, hard, and wild. 

Her wife’s birthdays were simple, and something that she wanted to celebrate too. She could just imagine how they would celebrate their little one’s. They would be thoroughly spoiled, Agatha giving them everything she never had as a kid. She would make sure that their kid would always be loved, and she had no doubt that Rio would pull through.

However, Agatha’s own birthday was in twenty one days, December 21st, and seven days before they would find out if they were having a son or daughter. In truth she didn’t care, but it would be a nice late Yuletide gift for her to win the first bet. There was a secret part of her that was all too ready for this kid to come out as a carbon copy of her wife. 

But in this moment, as Rio shifted them, guiding their bodies until they sat face to face– knees bent, hips pressed close, the firelight flickering between them– Agatha felt irritation begin to rise, slow and simmering. She allowed it, let Rio settle them as she pleased, but her silence spoke volumes. Either her wife sensed exactly where her thoughts were headed, or Agatha simply wasn’t hiding her frustration well enough. 

A kiss landed against her cheek– soft, a quiet offering– before brown eyes met blue. With a language only they knew, their souls spoke to each other, their love unearthing again as her wife simply reassured her that this was just a gift, not for any particular reason other than she loved her.  

“Just… don’t laugh at me,” Rio murmured, pressing a bag into Agatha’s hands. 

She exhaled sharply, but she took it, reaching inside with practiced patience. Her fingers brushed against paper first, the crinkle of it crisp in the warm air. She pulled it free, unwrapping the brown packaging with steady hands. The moment the fabric unfurled, soft green wool pooled in her lap, thick and warm between her fingers. Unbelievably fluffy. She smoothed a hand over it, feeling the intricate stitching, the way the fibers gave way beneath her touch. Her eyes traced the details– dark embroidered leaves curling across the surface, tiny bursts of red holly berries tucked between them like scattered jewels. And then, as she unfolded it further, her gaze caught on the unmistakable shape of two tiny sleeves and the curve of pant legs meant to keep impossibly small feet warm. 

Agatha’s fingers trembled slightly as she ran them over the embroidered leaves, the soft wool warm against her skin. It was too big. Too early. Too much. Her throat tightened, the weight of it pressing against her chest like a hand curling over her ribs. Rio had seen this onesie and thought of their child. Had pictured him– or her – growing into it, wearing it when they were old enough to sit up on their own, to reach out with tiny hands, to look up at them with wide, trusting eyes. It was such a simple thing. Just fabric. Just thread. But it wasn’t. It was proof that Rio already knew their child, already loved them in the quiet, certain way that she did everything. And Agatha– gods, she wanted that certainty. But all she could feel was the overwhelming, aching weight of it, pressing down on her with more force than she was ready for. Her vision blurred. 

No. 

No . 

She would not cry over a stupid onesie. But her hormones had other plans. Her breath hitched, her grip tightening on the soft wool, and she cursed under her breath as she squeezed her eyes shut. 

“ Mi amor ?” Rio’s voice was soft, careful. 

Like she already knew what was happening. Of course she did. Because Rio knew her. Because Rio saw through every wall, every mask, every carefully held breath. A thumb brushed against her cheek, and only then did Agatha realize a single tear had slipped free. Shit. 

“Shut up,” she muttered, voice thick, pressing the heel of her palm against her eyes. 

Rio huffed a quiet laugh, “I didn’t say anything.”

“You were going to.” 

“Well, now I’m definitely going to,” Rio teased, though her touch stayed gentle. “It’s okay, you know. You’re allowed to cry.” 

Agatha let out a watery scoff, shaking her head, “No, I am not. Not over a damn onesie.” 

Rio leaned in, pressing a kiss to her temple, “Hormones are a bitch, my love.” 

Agatha sniffed, trying to will the tears back, but her body had already betrayed her. 

She pressed the onesie against her chest, as if that would somehow ground her, and whispered, “You saw this and thought of them.”

 “I did.” 

“You already love them.”

“I do,” Rio murmured, no hesitation in her voice. “And so do you.” 

Agatha let out a shaky breath, tilting her head until their foreheads rested together, “I do.”

Rio’s arms wrapped around her then, warm and steady, and Agatha clung to her, onesie crushed between them. She was still overwhelmed. Still hormonal. Still utterly undone by the weight of love pressing against her ribs. Her wife’s lips kept pressing to her head before slowly pulling away, reaching for the bag again. The sound of glass clinking together caught her attention as Rio pulled out a little vial of oil and small bundles of herbs in jars. 

“The lady I bought the onesie off of gave me these for free. The herbs I was going to lace some to the wreath while others would be used for your baths. She told me this is anointing oil made of rosemary, lavender, and myrrh. She told me to trace protection runes on your stomach and it will also strengthen your connection with them.”

Agatha stared at the little vial in Rio’s hand, her chest tightening. The warmth of the fire flickered against her skin, but it was nothing compared to the heat curling low in her throat, the kind that came from feeling too much all at once. More tears raced down her cheek but they were simply kissed away. 

“May I?” her wife asked tenderly. 

Agatha swallowed hard, her fingers tightening around the onesie again. It was too much, too soon– wasn’t it? She had barely let herself believe this was real, barely allowed herself to acknowledge the swell of her stomach, the life thrumming beneath her skin. But Rio– Rio had never hesitated. The moment she had shown the positive pregnancy test to her wife, she was all in. She was there holding Agatha through her morning sickness, feeding her food to settle her stomach, and catching her when she felt dizzy. Rio had truly been ready– truly accepted this the moment they started the process. 

She exhaled slowly and nodded. Rio’s lips curved into a small, understanding smile. Her wife led her carefully to lay back on the soft pile of blankets, the warmth of the fire kissing her skin, and despite herself, she could feel her body settle into the comfort, her muscles loosening bit by bit. Her wife kneeled over her, uncorking the vial and having the smell of lavender consume her senses. With deliberate, but cautious movements, Rio dipped her fingers into the oil before flingers were sliding against her skin, warming her belly. For a moment she felt exposed, more than just naked in the middle of their living room, but her wife’s fingers brushed over her stomach again, sending a shiver up Agatha’s spine, and she gasped quietly, eyes fluttering shut. Rio’s touch was deifying, the oil now cool against the warmth of her skin, but the soft hum of the fire beside them was all-encompassing. 

“ Mi amor ,” Rio whispered, her voice low and filled with reverence, “ eres mi vida, mi todo .” (You are my life, my everything.) 

Agatha exhaled, a light breath escaping her lips as she reached for Rio’s hand, their fingers brushing together before she placed Rio’s palm over her stomach, the warmth of her touch grounding her in the moment. Rio’s thumb traced over her belly, slow and deliberate, and Agatha’s breath caught. She could feel the slight pressure of Rio’s touch, the weight of the love behind it. The oil was still there, cool and comforting, and Rio’s hand stayed steady as she continued to trace the runes, her voice like a sacred chant in the quiet room. 

“ Estás protegida, ” her wife murmured, her lips grazing Agatha’s ear as she continued to trace the first rune. “ Nada nos hará daño. ” (You are protected. Nothing will harm us.) 

Agatha’s chest rose with a deep, shaky breath. The words felt like a spell all on their own, winding around her heart, grounding her in ways she didn’t know she needed. 

“Te amo, ” Rio continued, kissing the sensitive skin just below Agatha’s ribs before moving her hand lower, continuing to draw the next rune. “ Siempre, mi amor. ” (I love you. Always, my love.) 

The sensation of Rio’s hand on her skin, her kisses, and the soft whisper of words in Spanish– their sacredness, the trust Agatha felt in this moment– it all collided into something overwhelming, tender, and raw. Her hands reached for Rio then, her fingers curling into the softness of her wife’s hair as she pulled her closer, desperate for more of this, more of Rio’s presence, more of this love that seemed to be unearthed from her wife’s very soul.

“ Eres mía, ” Rio’s voice was barely a whisper, a low sound vibrating their foreheads. “ y yo soy tuya. ” (You are mine, and I am yours.) 

She shivered, her heart pounding in her chest as she whispered in return, “ Siempre contigo .” (Always with you.) 

Rio’s fingers continued their delicate work, tracing the last of the runes over her stomach as she pressed another kiss to the sensitive skin there. It wasn’t long before Agatha’s eyelids grew heavy, the gentle rhythm of Rio’s touch, her soft words, and the warmth of their bodies entwined together lulling her into the safety and peace she hadn’t realized she craved so deeply. She didn’t fight the closing of her eyes as her wife kept whispering to her and to their unborn child, kisses placed against her stomach as all of her worries faded from her mind. 

Lips were placed on hers before she fully drifted off, listening to Rio capping the vial and rearranging their blankets before stronger arms were pulling her into a place she was sure was safe, and with that– with the comfort of her wife’s love, the warmth and protection she felt from the oil, and the unborn child once more nestled between them, Agatha fell asleep dreaming of their future. 

 

***

 

Agatha groaned, stretching slightly, ready to feel the floor beneath her and Rio’s front pressed against her back. She felt neither. She quickly recognized the memory-foam mattress beneath her and her eyes flew open. She rolled on her back, her hand lazily searching for her wife beside realizing she was in bed alone. She tilted her head, confusion settling in as she sat up, her gaze sweeping the room as if that alone could answer her question. The closet light was on, that meant the power was back, but she heard no movement and her wife had a bad habit of leaving it on. Slowly, she swung her legs over the side, her hand going to her stomach as it rumbled, craving her wife’s arroz con gandules. 

Before she could move, a series of buzzing caught her attention on Rio’s nightstand. She reached over, finding it was Rio’s family group text going off. The phone unlocked the moment she looked at it, opening up to her wife’s texts. 

 

La familia Vidal

 

La pendeja: Sí, la abuela está aquí con nosotros. 

(Yes, Grandma is here with us)

 

Oriana: Riocita, traeré postre el lunes si tu esposa puede cocinar el cordero que hizo el año pasado.

(Little Rio, I’ll bring dessert on Monday if your wife can cook the lamb she made last year.)

 

Elio: Asegúrate de que la abuelita no esté de pie por mucho tiempo y también de que tome su medicación. ¿Está durmiendo ahora?

(Make sure Abuelita isn’t on her feet for too long and also that she takes her medication. Is she sleeping now?) 

 

Agatha scrolled up on the texts, knowing her wife didn’t care. She was in the group chat anyway, just rarely answered. Writing wasn’t her strong suit in Spanish. She could read just fine but she would sit there overthinking whether to use compound past tense or imperfect. But when speaking, she would just know. Her wife would always question it but of course, Agatha would just say it was because she was a hands-on learner and Rio’s tongue was an excellent teacher. 

 

La familia Vidal

 

Agatha y yo queremos invitarlos a una cena antes de Yule. El lunes. Díganle a la abuelita. :Me

(Agatha and I want to invite you to a dinner before Yule. On Monday. Tell Abuelita ).

 

La pendeja: ¿Por qué? Nos vemos el día veinticinco. Ella también es toda tuya durante una semana.

(Why? We will see you on the twenty-fifth. She’s all yours for a week, too.) 

 

Oriana: Oh, qué lindo, Riocita. Se lo diremos. ¿A qué hora?

(Oh, how nice, little Rio. We’ll tell her. What time?) 

 

17h30. Háganos saber si está trayendo otros invitados. :Me

(Let us know if you’re bringing any other guests.)

 

Agatha grimaced, standing up quickly and throwing on a pair of sweatpants and a sweatshirt before rushing into their living room. The blankets were cleaned up, boxes of ornaments sitting to the side, and the coffee table was pushed back into place with the onesie and unfinished blanket sitting neatly. She smiled fondly, looking around before moving towards the back porch. Everything was white, the pine trees far ahead holding blankets of snow to keep them warm. It was still steadily snowing, but it was light with a little sun peaking through, but her attention was on the fresh set of footprints leading to the greenhouse. 

She wasn’t surprised, especially now that Rio took the plunge and invited her siblings over for dinner, and Ximena Vidal: the matriarch of the Vidal family. Ximena raised Rio after her parents died and well, Ximena was a hard, cold woman, who still somehow radiated more warmth than Agatha had ever encountered. Agatha would never forget the woman, with eyes more intense than Rio’s, leading her away from her wife at their wedding reception. The woman merely took Agatha’s face in her hands, the way she would hold Rio (not wonder why she melted with this gesture) and smiled at her. 

“You are making my Riocita so happy,” she had spoken softly despite how thick her accent was. “Give her the world, por favor , cariño .” 

She understood Rio’s nerves about telling her family. Abuelita will be all over her, but it was more than that, because “ La Pendeja ”? That was Maria, and Maria already had something against her youngest sister that Agatha still had yet to figure out. She knew it was some type of competition that Maria made up between them, but they were Vidal women. Vidal women were stubborn, proud, and had an easy self assured confidence that made anyone else feel inferior. So when two Vidals collided, it was as if the very threads of the universe started to unravel. 

She placed her boots on and threw on a jacket before making her way out. She ignored how the cold bit at her ears and nose as she hurried through and into the greenhouse. It was warmer inside, so warm that Agatha was throwing off her coat, sweatshirt, and shoes. The greenhouse was simple, but organized in a strict fashion in which Rio had down. In the center were a row of azaleas and to the right in the corner were calla lilies and roses. To the left were lilies of the valley and red spider lilies. In the back was Rio’s more expensive plants she still had no idea what they were named, but she knew that was where her wife was. She followed the small dirt path to the back, the greens hanging over into the trail, but bright and flourishing. Her wife sat in a black wicker chair, her legs curled underneath her. her tank top was loose, the green joggers blending in with her plants. Her entire focus was on the wreath in her hands, weaving the pine and herbs into it with care, but her eyebrows were knitting themselves together and Rio was biting the inside of her cheek. 

She approached slowly, but Rio didn’t look up at her, and it was then she knew what her wife needed. For the first good year of their relationship, Agatha had trouble understanding sometimes what her wife needed. She never completely understood it, even till this day, but there were times she did. Either her wife needed touch to ground her or to avoid it like the plague because it burned her. She slowly learned the signs over the years, and sure, there were moments where she either ignored them or missed them, but when she saw them, they were as clear as glass: Rio’s shoulders were tense, pulled up towards her ears, her jaw was going to work at her cheeks, and her eyebrows seemed to be debating if they should become a unibrow. She knew those signs anywhere. Her wife needed a grounding touch whether it was being held, a simple touch to her cheek, or as of lately, Rio’s hand on her stomach. 

“My love,” she said softly, not wanting to startle her. 

Rio didn’t look up, but her shoulders dropped ever so slightly at the sound of Agatha’s voice. The tension in her body remained, but it had loosened just enough to tell Agatha she was listening. Agatha watched as her wife set the wreath down with more force than necessary, jaw tight as she pushed herself up from the chair. She started pacing, bare feet silent against the warm floor of the greenhouse, her fingers threading through her curls before dragging down her face. Agatha didn’t move right away. She knew better. Instead, she slid into the chair Rio had just abandoned, crossing one leg over the other as she watched her wife work herself up. 

“They’re going to have something to say, I know it,” her wife muttered, mostly to herself, hands flying in exasperated gestures. “Maria’s going to be insufferable, and Abuelita’s going to ask a million questions, and Oriana–” she huffed, shaking her head. “Oriana will be fine, but she’s not the problem. The problem is Maria.” 

Agatha hummed, neither agreeing nor disagreeing, just giving her wife space to spill. 

“She’ll make some comment, she always does. About how I didn’t tell her sooner. About how I’m fucking doing things wrong. Like she’s the fucking expert on my life,” Rio scoffed, still pacing, hands still moving in sharp, clipped gestures. “And then she’ll say something about you. And about us. And I will not be fucking responsible for what I say back.” 

She turned then, eyes flashing as if daring Agatha to tell her she was overreacting. But Agatha only arched a brow, the corner of her mouth twitching with the barest hint of amusement. 

Rio’s scowl deepened, “Don’t look at me like that.” 

Agatha tilted her head, silent. Her wife exhaled sharply through her nose. 

“Agatha.” 

Still, she said nothing, just watching her with that same quiet, knowing gaze. Rio’s pacing slowed. Her hands curled into fists, then uncurled. Her shoulders, still tense, pulled up once, twice, then slumped forward in surrender. With a groan, Rio crossed the short distance between them and sank to her knees in front of Agatha. Her forehead pressed against Agatha’s thigh, her breath warm against the fabric of her sweatpants. Agatha let her settle, smoothing a hand over Rio’s dark mane, threading her fingers through the soft strands, nails scratching lightly against her scalp. She felt more than heard her wife’s exhale, a slow release of breath against her leg. Rio’s hand, without thought, slid over Agatha’s knee, then up, palm finding its familiar place on the curve of her stomach. Her fingers spread, her thumb brushing absently against the fabric of Agatha’s shirt, as if reminding herself– reminding both of them– that some things mattered more than Maria’s words. More than old wounds and past grievances. Agatha kept combing through her wife’s hair, soothing, steady. 

“You done?” she murmured.

Rio huffed another breath against her thigh, “Maybe.” 

Agatha smirked, shifting slightly so her wife would look up. “Maria doesn’t matter.” 

Rio exhaled sharply, “I know.” 

Agatha traced the line of Rio’s jaw, guiding her up until their eyes met, “Then breathe, my love. We’re okay.” 

Her wife held her gaze for a long moment before finally– finally– her shoulders sagged, the fight ebbing from her muscles like the tide retreating from the shore. Her forehead pressed back against Agatha’s leg, her fingers still resting over the life growing beneath her palm. 

“ Cariño… ” 

Agatha smiled, leaning down to press a kiss to the crown of Rio’s head, “I know, my love.”

She let her wife stay there for a moment longer, fingers still stroking through locks of raven hair. Her wife nuzzled against her thigh, her hand running soothing circles on her stomach. Slowly, she leaned down, pressing another kiss to her head before giving a gentle tug, her touch coaxing rather than commanding. 

“Come inside, my love,” she said. “You’re barefoot, and I know you’re going to start complaining soon. Plus, your kid wants their mami ’s arroz con gandules.”

Her wife huffed but didn’t move, “Is this your cute attempt to cover up a craving, mi amor ?”

“I don’t know what you are talking about,” she decided, making her wife chuckle as she stood. 

Rio placed a kiss on her cheek, “Okay, okay, I won’t tease you anymore since you’re carrying our child. I’ll make arroz con gandules for you and then we can decorate the tree.” 

Her wife and her put their shoes back on before heading back into the house. Rio went straight to the kitchen, pulling out the ingredients as Agatha found herself back on the couch, grabbing the blanket and starting up again, losing herself in the rhythm of tying their child’s blanket together. She hardly noticed time passed until Rio held a plate out to her before sitting beside her and eating together. 

Rio finished first, quickly grabbing Agatha seconds…and thirds…and fourths before she decided she had enough and stood to help with the tree. As they moved toward the tree, the afternoon air felt heavier with the quiet hum of their shared space. Agatha’s fingers brushed against the smooth glass of an ornament, but before she could lift it, Rio’s hand was there– always close, always sure. There was a gentle press against her stomach, a touch that had become familiar and comforting. Rio’s hand lingered for just a second longer than necessary, her fingers splayed as if she could feel the life inside Agatha even before it made its presence known to the world. Agatha glanced over at her wife, a small, playful smile tugging at her lips. 

“You don’t have to keep doing that,” she said softly, though there was no real protest in her voice. “The baby’s not going anywhere.” 

Rio simply smiled, brushing her thumb over Agatha’s skin, “I like knowing he’s there.” 

Her voice was low, almost tender, as if the simple act of touching her wife grounded her to something far greater than herself. Her tensions were fully melting away as their rhythm set in between actually decorating and her wife sneaking touches to her stomach. She rolled her eyes, but the laughter was instantly spilling between them. Agatha teased Rio for being too precise with the ornaments, muttering about “the right balance,” while her wife rolled her eyes at Agatha’s habit of placing things at random, claiming it gave the tree “character.” At one point, she smirked and deliberately put a bauble in the most unbalanced, awkward spot possible, just to watch her wife twitch before sighing and fixing it. 

They were almost done. All that was left was the star Agatha had made for them from their very first Yule together as a couple. She had gathered a few twigs from when Rio would take her out to picnics, cleansing them and tying them together into a star before adding fresh herbs to it every year. This year her and Rio tied motherwort and coriander to it. Agatha always put the star on the stop of their tree. It was a tradition just like Rio picking the Yule tree. She found the step stool beside the boxes, bringing it over. Before she could even place her foot on the first step of the stool, hands were on her hips, pulling her back. 

“No.” 

It wasn’t harsh, but it carried the weight of a thousand gentle commands. 

“Rio!” she gasped, half-laughing, half-incredulous as she found herself being bodily maneuvered away from the stool. “Are you serious?” 

Her wife only gave her a firm look, the kind that brooked no argument, before taking the star from her hands as if she hadn’t just manhandled Agatha across the room, “I said no.” 

Agatha folded her arms, her lips curving in an amused smirk, “You do realize I’ve been putting the star up every year, right?” 

“Not this year,” her wife shrugged, turning toward the tree with an air of finality. “This year, I put it up.” 

Agatha huffed, trying to look put out, but Rio’s overprotectiveness was so absurdly endearing she couldn’t even bring herself to be truly annoyed. 

“You’re acting like I was about to scale a mountain instead of stepping on a stool,” she muttered, rolling her eyes. 

Rio smirked, never looking away from the tree as she carefully adjusted the star.

“You’re pregnant,” she said, as if that explained everything. 

Agatha let out an exaggerated sigh, “So what? Is breathing next on your list of things I’m not allowed to do?”

“If I thought you’d find a way to make that dangerous, yes,” her wife didn’t lose the smirk. 

She stepped down, facing off with her. Agatha narrowed her eyes, real annoyance finally filling her. Blame it on hormones or blame it on her wife’s stupid smug smile. Before she could bite out something mean, lips pressed to forehead and she froze, all the irritation completely draining as if it had never existed.

“I hate you.”

“I love you more,” her wife said simply returning the boxes.

Before Agatha could move, to help her wife, they heard Rio’s phone ring from their room. Her wife sighed, kissing her cheek as Agatha playfully swatted at her ass, but something made her freeze, something like dread filling her system. 

She shook her head the moment she heard her wife’s irritated voice, “What do you want?” 

Maria. 

Maria’s voice came through, echoing down the hall, sharp and unimpressed, “Nice to hear from you, too, hermanita .” 

Her wife appeared in the doorway, but she wasn’t looking at her. Her gaze was distant and her eyebrows creased together. Agatha sat down on the couch, patting the seat next to her, but her wife only began to pace in front of her. 

Rio rolled her eyes, already regretting picking up, “Maria.” 

“I’m bringing someone on Monday,” Maria said, as if she hadn’t just ignored Rio’s irritation. “My fiancé.” 

Rio blinked, “Your what?” 

“My fiancé,” Maria repeated, slow and deliberate, like Rio was an idiot. 

Her wife stared down at the phone in her hand as if the device itself just offended her before speaking, “You’ve had a boyfriend, and now you’re engaged? Since when?” 

Maria scoffed,“Like you can say anything after hiding Agatha for three years.” 

Agatha raised a brow, but pursed her lips in an attempt to hide her amusement. It was a weak jab at Rio, a jab at closed and perfectly well healed wounds that Rio and her often found themselves laughing at when they remembered. She didn’t say anything, just watching as Rio grit her teeth. 

“I didn’t hide her.”

“No?” Maria’s voice dripped with mock surprise. “Oh, that’s right. I had to knock on your apartment just for her to open the door, having no idea who I was.” 

“That was twelve years ago.” 

“And yet, it’s still embarrassing.” 

Rio pinched the bridge of her nose, “Maria–” 

Maria cut her off with a sharp laugh, “You know, it’s funny. I never thought you’d settle down. But I guess even you had to grow up eventually.” 

Agatha’s fingers twitched. There was something off about Maria’s tone, something more biting than usual. She tilted her head, keeping her gaze on her wife, knowing not to intervene yet. 

Rio’s lips parted to respond, but Maria continued, “And now you’ve got this–” she made a noise, something dismissive “–little life you’ve built. But I have to wonder… are you really happy, hermanita ?” 

Rio stilled, her jaw clenched as her grip tightened every so slightly on the phone.  

“Or is it just easy?” Maria pressed, her voice lilting in faux curiosity. “Hiding away, playing pretend, shutting out the rest of us– ” 

Agatha saw the shift in her wife before she heard it– the way Rio’s grip on her phone turned vice-like, the way her shoulders pulled tight with unspoken fury. Then– 

“ No estoy jugando a nada, maldita sea. ” (I’m not playing at anything, damn it.) 

Maria hummed, like she wasn’t impressed, “ Por favor, Rio. No es lo mismo. No es– ” 

“ No es qué ?” Rio snapped. “ ¿No es lo que tú crees que es una familia? Porque nos casamos, María. Y es real. Más real que cualquier– ” (Not what? Not what you think a family is? Because we got married, Maria. And it’s real. More real than any–) 

Maria scoffed, cutting her off, “ Sí, sí, claro. Pero dime algo– ” (Yeah, yeah, sure. But tell me something–) 

And then, Agatha felt it. The shift. The moment before Maria struck deep, before she said something cruel enough to wound, something she couldn’t take back. Maria’s voice turned saccharine. 

“ Dime, Rio. ¿De verdad crees que Abuelita quiere esto? Que ella de verdad quiere que dos mujeres– ” (Tell me, Rio. Do you really think Abuelita wants this? That she really wants two women–) 

“Enough,” Agatha’s voice cut through the conversation like a blade. 

Her wife turned, startled. Maria went silent. Agatha stood slowly, crossing the room with deliberate, measured steps. She plucked the phone from Rio’s hand with an ease that spoke of years of knowing exactly how to handle her wife’s fire. 

“Maria,” she said, calm, cold, and utterly unyielding. “You should think very carefully before you finish that sentence.” 

Maria said nothing. 

“I know what you’re doing,” Agatha continued, voice as smooth as glass. “And I’ll tell you now– you won’t get what you want.” 

Still, Maria didn’t speak. 

Agatha tilted her head, amusement curling at the edges of her mouth. 

“What’s wrong? No clever remarks? No cheap insults?” she clicked her tongue. “Shame.” 

A beat of silence before Maria exhaled, sharp and irritated, “ Nos vemos el lunes. ” (See you Monday.) 

The line went dead. 

Agatha lowered the phone to the coffee table before turning to her wife. She was standing still now, but her breaths were shallow, her fists clenched to the point she knew Rio’s blunt nails were about to draw blood. Tentatively, she reached for her hand, fingers easily breaking the strong grip and threading through. She guided her wife to the couch, who let out a slow, shaking breath, and rested her head against Agatha’s thigh as she laid on her side. 

She stroked her hair, her fingers moving around her scalp as Rio’s gaze remained on her round stomach in front of her face. A hand came up, rubbing in circles again as Agatha sat back, watching the tension slowly leave her wife. 

“She’s not worth your anger, mi amor .” 

Rio’s lip curled, “ La quiero muerta.”

 

 

Chapter End Notes

Here is where I get my symbology of the herbs : https://www.thewiccanlady.co.uk/herbal-grimoire

Also, I made a X account, so if you wanna chat there it's @krvidal2119
would love to hear from you on there, along with any requests there or to answer questions or just share some Agathrio posts.

YES FAMILY DRAMA IS COMING UP SOON (Vidal shit first, yes Evanora will eventually make her appearance-- one thing at a time)

gonna try to post Monday, I love you all my darlings 💚💜

Chapter 15: Creeper Distractions

Chapter Summary

Minecraft

Chapter Notes

Guys, I am so sorry. This chapter is shorter than the others but I am working on the next part tonight and hoping to have it out ASAP

Friday, the 21st was my birthday and I kinda forgot until I was on the slopes... and then travelled for the next few days... might have a concussion.... went too hard my 21st birthday, will explain in the comments if you are interested.

For now, enjoy this short sweet chapter and yes, I jumped on the Minecraft bandwagon.

Follow me on Twitter for updates https://x.com/krvidal2119

I post a lot of updates there to keep in the loop. please don't be afraid to message me. I love receiving your feedback and talking to you guys

ANYWAY -- Love, RioAllAlong_719 💚💜

See the end of the chapter for more notes

 

Rio

 

The basement was dimly lit between the television and the light from the heater spanning across the open space. All the lights were off as Rio slouched against the couch, blankets draped over her legs haphazardly revealing her in all of her glory : a Dean and professor, sitting back with a chunky steelseries arctis pro wireless pine green headset hugging her head and a black Playstation controller in her hands. She wore her green creeper hoodie, ignoring the way the zipper scratched her chest. Her boxers were a tighter fit, fitting her perfectly, even though they were a gag gift from Alice with Minecraft’s skulls littering them. Rio cracked her neck, her glasses slipping as she merely pushed them back up her attention locked on the screen in front of her.

She moved with precision, her thumbs moving over the throttles and buttons with practiced ease as she constructed yet another layer of fortifications around her virtual castle. She spent years working on the castle outside itself and now, it was decorated on the inside with a grand throne room, ballrooms, courts, and even gardens. She called it her Queendom of Death. The obsidian walls stretch high, torches lining every crevice, ensuring no gaps, no weak spots. She had already built a watchtower, a hidden tunnel, even an emergency escape route– just in case. It was easier to focus on the game than on the conversation that still echoed in her mind. 

Dime, Rio. ¿De verdad crees que Abuelita quiere esto? 

Her fingers tightened against the controller, her jaw locking. She could still hear her bitch of a sister’s voice, smooth and cutting, pressing into the scars of old wounds like she was testing how deep they went. 

Que ella de verdad quiere que dos mujeres…

 The words had stuck, sinking into her ribs like a blade. She had barely slept, had barely eaten. Instead, she had thrown herself into the one thing she could control– her world, her defenses, her walls. Nothing could hurt her here… well, except for that memory threatening to surface. She blurred it, trying to bury it back down. 

She was sixteen when she thought her world was about to end. She had snuck a girl home– well, the house was supposed to be empty. Abuelita was out with her friends, Oriana was working late, and Maria… well, all she knew at the time was that her sister wasn’t going to be home. The girl was in Rio’s class, one that she had no intention of actually dating, but who was she going to pass up on an opportunity like that. It wasn’t her first girl and for once, Rio wasn’t going to risk indecent exposure and would be lying on something comfortable instead of the sand at the beach or against a tree. Rio wasn’t sure how long they had been at it. All she knew was she was between the girl’s legs and then the door opened. There was a lot of screaming and two hours later she was on the porch before her Abuelita who only sighed and shook her head– but to her luck, ultimately did not care… but Maria did.

She shook out of it focusing back on collecting supplies. She had a new mission : go to the caves, defeat a dragon, and collect their eggs. This time she would be successful. Her dogs, Morticia and Gomez, followed her as they made their way through the caves. It was a long way down, avoiding more monsters and fighting what she needed too. She tongued at her cheek as the screen flashed with a creeper about the blow up. She dodged it continuing her way through the grey twists and turns. Then finally, the cave opened and in the center a great black crystalled dragon turned to her. She smirked, selecting her bow and just as she aimed, the screen went black, shrouding the room in darkness. 

“What the fuck?!” Rio tore her headphones off but was stunned as the lights came on, blinding her instantly. 

The sting of light burned as Rio squeezed her eyes shut, a hiss slipping through her teeth. She barely registered the sound of footsteps before a familiar warmth pressed against her, a steady hand cupping her cheek. 

“Rio,” Agatha murmured, voice softer than expected, but edged with something firm– unyielding. “I’ve been calling for you for the past ten minutes, my love.”

Rio forced her eyes open, blinking rapidly to adjust. Her wife stood in front of her, framed by the dim glow of the heater, her expression unreadable. Her gaze narrowed down to her stomach, not able to help it. Her wife was in Rio ’s Bad Omens t-shirt. It wasn’t oversized, in fact Rio liked this shirt more where she would tuck the end of the shirt into her bra in the summer months. Now on her wife, it didn’t hide her pregnant belly, the simple roundness that she knew an oversize shirt would struggle to hide anyway– especially at fifteen weeks pregnant. 

“What the hell, Agatha?” Rio’s voice came out hoarse, laced with exhaustion rather than anger. She scrubbed a hand down her face, fingers catching on the bridge of her glasses. “I was in the middle of– ”

“Running,” Agatha interrupted. 

Rio froze, throat tightening as she stared up into the bright blues of her wife. 

Her wife only sighed, caressing the side of her cheek, “You always do this. You build walls, burrow yourself in caves” she tilted her head toward the now-black screen “ –whatever helps you hide. 

Rio’s fingers curled into the blanket pooled around her lap, “I’m not hiding.” 

Agatha shook her head, her thumb pushing against her cheek softly, “Then what are you doing?” 

She pressed her lips together, trying to shake away Maria’s voice, the weight pressing into her chest. 

Que ella de verdad quiere que dos mujeres… 

She shut her eyes, “It doesn’t matter.” 

Agatha clicked her tongue, tilting Rio’s chin up with two fingers, “It does. You matter.” 

Rio exhaled shakily, forcing herself to meet Agatha’s gaze. Her wife– her brilliant, stubborn, unshakable wife– looked at her with something fierce, something unwavering. Then, the expression softened as her wife turned around, plugging the television back in and before nodding her towards the love seat part of the couch. Rio found her body moving before her mind could question what was happening. She settled back, her legs now propped on the couch as her wife took her controller from her hands and turned off her headset. 

“What are you doing?” she asked, brow furrowing. 

Her wife sat down between her legs, leaning back against her as she logged into the game and found her way to Rio’s world. She went back to Rio’s task of collecting food, armor, and bows and arrows before finding Morticia and Gomez in their own room 

As she walked the halls of her castle, Agatha made her annoyance clear now, “Remember promising me that you would drop anything to help with any pregnancy symptoms?” 

Rio tensed, her hand coming around her wife’s waist and rubbing the side of her stomach. She had read recently that her wife’s sides of her stomach might start to hurt with the stretching. She placed her lips on her wife’s head who just hummed for a moment. 

“Not there, love. Up and under, darling,” her wife said before chuckling. “Have at it.” 

Her body was once more faster than her mind, seeking out exactly what she loved. She couldn’t stop the smile as her hands reached unearth her wife’s shirt, moving up soft skin to two plush breasts. Rio’s hands moved instinctively, palms warm against Agatha’s skin, thumbs tracing slow, reverent circles over the soft swell of her breasts. The moment Agatha melted into her, a low hum slipping past her lips, Rio exhaled, her forehead pressing against the back of her wife’s shoulder.

“You know,” her wife mused as she maneuvered herself into the pixelated cave, “for someone who spends their time building defenses, you’re incredibly weak in one specific area.” 

She blinked, “What the hell are you talking about?” 

Agatha smirked, clicking her tongue as she gathered supplies in-game, “Oh, I think you know. I’m starting to think you love them more than me.” 

Rio’s grip faltered for half a second before she growled, nipping at Agatha’s shoulder in retaliation, “ No empieces, mi amor. ” (Don’t start, my love). 

Agatha laughed, clicking her tongue as she raced away from an exploding creeper on screen, “I mean, I get it, really. They are spectacular. Perky, full–” she gave a little dramatic sigh, “ –and they are just going to get bigger.” 

Rio groaned, dropping her forehead against Agatha’s back, her fingers twitching where they cupped soft, warm skin, “Agatha.”

Her wife ignored her suffering, tilting her head thoughtfully, “You know, I’ve caught you staring. A lot. Like, an inappropriate amount for a boss to look at their married employee.” 

She gritted her teeth, “I will end you.” 

Agatha smirked, finally turning to glance at her over her shoulder, eyes glinting with mischief, “You’d have to let go first.”

Rio only returned the smirk, her hands slipping out of her wife’s shirt causing her wife to hiss in pain. She froze, her eyes losing their teasing gleam as her hands went back immediately, massaging the warm skin there as her wife pushed her chest more firmly into her hands. Regret crept in hard, not realizing how sore they must be. Her wife paused the game allowing Rio to massage her breasts and place apologetic kisses to her head repeatedly. 

“Why didn’t you come to me sooner, mi amor ?” she asked softly. 

Her wife shrugged, “You needed space to think, my love–”

“You gave me it all yesterday after the call, plus this–” a hand rested briefly on her stomach before moving back up “ –is more important than anything. How long have they been bothering you?” 

“Since this morning,” she shrugged again, forcing nonchalance. 

“Agatha,” Rio sighed, but her wife just continued the game, heading deeper into the caves. 

They sat there in silence minus the chopping of blocks, the fling of arrows, and hiss of creepers. Rio kept her hands on her wife’s breasts as Agatha defeated a Minecraft dragon and was claiming her prize of an egg. She had rolled her eyes, knowing how good her wife was at this game. Rio had taught her years ago, after months of begging for her to play it with her. Agatha would act like she hated the game, alway muttering “you’re lucky I love you,” before sitting her ass down and playing like a professional. She had even helped Rio build part of the castle and one day Rio came back from a meeting with her advisor, she caught her playing without her. Her girlfriend at the time tried distracting her with sex, but she was wearing Rio’s creeper boxers– the ones she claimed that she would burn– and well, of course Rio hasn’t let her live it down since. 

As her wife went back down into the caves to find another dragon, Rio kissed the side of her head, “You think he will like Minecraft?”

“Who?” her wife asked absentmindedly, her gaze remaining on killing a zombie. 

Rio cupped her wife’s stomach, “Our son, mi amor. ” 

“Yeah, I am sure he will like it. Kids usually take interest in what their parents are interested in.”

She couldn’t stop the smirk as everything lit up with excitement. She was silent for a moment, wondering if her wife would catch what she just said, but instead she ran into another zombie and killed it with ease, watching it poof into smoke. Rio leaned forward, pressing a kiss to her neck as she chuckled darkly.

She watched as her wife’s fingers froze for just a heartbeat, her character pausing in the game for a split second as the realization dawned on her. Rio had been wondering when her wife would slip, to subconsciously agree they were going to have a son. Her wife had always been careful, always referring to their child as “they” but Rio couldn’t stop the way her heart was flipping as it finally came out. He . 

Her lip curled into a knowing smile, a triumphant glint flashing in her eyes. Her wife ignored her, continuing the game, and for once Rio thought to have pity on her but the moment was just too sweet to ignore. 

Placing another kiss below her ear, she started casually, “So, when our son is born…”

Her hand moved to her wife’s stomach, the warmth of her touch gentle but possessive. She knew exactly what she was doing. Agatha’s eyes flicked down for a moment, her thumbs moving on the controller, as if trying to ignore the slip. Her shoulders were tense as her posture shifted, feigning to make herself more comfortable, but Rio could tell— this was just enough proof her wife was starting to believe.

“You just called him ‘he,’” she teased, her voice humming a laugh, her hand moving in slow, soothing circles on Agatha’s belly. “You’re slipping, mi amor . You’re starting to realize that I’m right.”

Agatha sighed dramatically, a theatrical show of annoyance but a small smile betrayed her performance, “Maybe I just want to mess with you.”

There’s a beat silence as Agatha slashed another zombie, “Maybe I’m tired of you being so fucking smug about it.”

Rio pressed her lips to the side of neck again as her hands went back to massaging her wife’s breasts, her thumb pressing down gently as she felt her heartbeat pounding underneath her palm. It made Rio’s own heart skip a beat as she nuzzled her nose there, taking in her sweet scent. 

“Or maybe,” she whispered. “You’re just mad because you know I am right. Can’t you see it? May fourteenth he will come screaming into the world with a head of black hair, dark brown eyes, the same nose as mine, and I guess he can have your chin, mi amor .” 

That earned her a sharp elbow in her stomach, “Do you want me to get you more dragon eggs or not?” 

“I thought you hated Minecraft,” Rio bit back, chopping her teeth noisily by her ear.

Agatha nudged her again, her eyes gleaming, “I’ll blow up your castle.”

Rio gasped, her eyes widening comically,  “You wouldn’t.” 

Her wife smirked, turning her character around and pulling out TNT. Panic set into her chest as she sat up, releasing her wife’s breasts slowly before hand shot out to grab the controller off of her. Her wife had a firm grip on it as she tugged and pulled, trying to twist it away but her wife just laughed evilly, leading the character out of the cave as Rio sat up, her hands pulling at her wife’s arms. 

“Agatha, wait– no! Devuélvelo, por favor– ah! Bruja, no te atrevas! ” she laughed as she leaned over her wife, both of them falling into fits of laughter as they fought over the controller. (Give it back, please– Witch, don’t you dare). 

Her sides hurt as she managed to yank the controller out of her grip, standing up on the couch now as their laughter filled over the sounds of the game. She led her character away when suddenly a hand wrapped around her ankle, and the world tilted. She yelped as she crashed back into the couch before her body rolled onto the floor. Then, from the television a loud explosion went off and the screen went red. 

Rio had barely time to notice it as her attention turned back to her wife who was clutching her stomach as she laughed, little snorts rolling her shoulders as she tried covering it, tears coming from her eyes. She felt a soft smile on her face as her wife covered her mouth, continuing to laugh, her snorts making Rio’s heart flutter. Her wife hated her laugh, but it was one of the many reasons Rio fell in love with her. She would never forget the first time she made Agatha truly laugh, how red her cheeks were as she hid from Rio, suddenly all shy. Rio had only smiled brightly, pulling her hands away as Agatha had calmed down her laughter. 

 

“That was the cutest laugh I ever heard,” Rio smiled down at her, tucking chestnut strands behind her ear. 

Agatha shook her head, her cheeks pink as her eyes closed, “No, it’s not–” 

Rio cut her off with a sweet kiss, smiling into it as her girlfriend melted. 

 

And since then, it was Rio’s goal for the rest of her life to make that sound happen over and over again. She sat up from where she landed on the ground, laughing lightly as her wife reached out, caressing her head worriedly. Blue and brown locked with each other as Rio’s hands found her way to her wife’s stomach, caressing it softly as they fell into a comfortable silence. 

“Are you alright, my love?” her wife asked her sweetly, a hand still resting on the back of her head where she noticed a light throbbing setting in. 

She nodded anyway, “Yes, mi amor . And our son?”

Her wife shook her head, but smiled nevertheless, “Our child is fine. Now, come on. I let you mope all yesterday. I need your help cleaning for tomorrow.” 

 

 

Rio paced back and forth in front of the oven as her wife sat behind her at the counter, drinking her tea as eyes followed her. She just placed the lamb in the oven after following the directions from her wife all day. Agatha’s ankles and wrists had started to swell yesterday and Rio wasn’t going to let her do any more work. The last hour of the night was spent with her on the floor before the couch with lotion rubbing her ankles for her. Her wife didn’t even have to ask her. Rio knew immediately what she needed as Agatha worked on the baby’s blanket. 

Now, it was Monday. Abuelita and her sisters would be here any moment. She wasn’t sure what she was more nervous of– Abuelita or Maria. Abuelita would be so happy for them finally starting a family, but everything in her was tight every time she thought of how they would tell her. Agatha’s plan was simple: finish dinner, eat dinner, play some games that she knew Abuelita loved, and then give them all an envelope with the twelve week scan photos. It was practical, a careful delivery, but something in Rio told her that it wouldn’t be so simple. Something would happen, and that something she did not know made her want to cancel the whole thing last minute. 

“My love,” her wife’s calm voice reached her. “You’re going to wear a hole in my floors.” 

Rio raised an eyebrow, “ My floors, cariño. I was the one who put them down.” 

Her wife rolled her eyes, “Point still stands, sweetheart. You are not adding more work on top of us building the nursery. Now, did you hide my blanket for me? The stroller is hidden in the garage?” 

She nodded, “Yes and yes. I put the onesie away too.” 

“And the strap?” Agatha smirked. 

“¡Mierda! ” Rio swore, running back to their bathroom knowing her family had a tendency to snoop. 

Rio would never forget Oriana’s mortification when she visited them in their shitty apartment during their doctorate schooling. Rio had forgotten to put their strap away from the previous night, leaving it on the counter of the sink in their only bathroom. Oriana had walked in and both of them had heard her gasp before she yelled at Rio… meanwhile, the strap wasn’t even green… it was purple. The one now, standing up tall on their bathroom counter was green. 

All of the articles were right– Agatha’s libido was high, but Rio would never complain– it brought her back to when they first started having sex. It was an addiction– still is but more tame. They defiled plenty of supply closets, two private study rooms, and too many times Lilia had knocked on Agatha’s bedroom door telling them to “shut the fuck up.” Now, Rio was sure as hell Agatha would have them fuck in one of their offices if they were not on break. 

She was half tempted to leave it out, knowing Maria would snoop and let her be traumatized, but one thought of her Abuelita seeing it had Rio putting it away in their dresser drawer full of their other sex toys. It wasn’t their finest hiding spot, rather the most accessible, but it wasn’t like her sisters or Abuelita would go through her drawers… right? 

Rio padded back out to the kitchen, finding her wife still sitting at the kitchen island. A smile crept across her face as she took in her wife. Agatha let her braid her hair back in a half-up, half-down style. Two braids sat on either side of her head, coming together in the back where Rio had combined them and braided down to her waist. Her wife did the same for her and Rio had beamed when she finally convinced her wife to match hairstyles with her. A large oversized purple sweater wrapped around her, the bump only noticeable if Agatha was doing what she was doing now: a hand rubbing the side of her stomach, easing the discomfort she felt as black leggings wrapped around her legs comfortably. 

Wrapping her arms around her and kissing her head, she spoke softly, “If you do that in front of Abuelita she will know that you’re pregnant before the announcement.”

Her wife laughed, hands resting over top of hers, “And if you keep doting over me, you’re going to be the one to give it away. I am not the problem here.” 

Rio feigned her hurt expression, “Excuse me, I didn’t realize doting was now not allowed.” 

Agatha shook her head, “It is when you are touching my stomach twenty-four seven– ugh, how bad are you going to be once they start kicking?” 

“I’ll become your worst nightmare,” she cooed, pressing her lips repeatedly to her head and cheeks. 

Her wife turned, capturing her lips. Rio smiled into it, feeling a hand come up and cup her cheek as the kiss lasted it. Their lips moved evenly and sure, capturing each other over and over again in a shared rhythm, speaking a simple devotion to each other as if this was their first time. Something warm and wet prodded at her lips, and on instinct, she parted them, tasting her wife before pulling away abruptly, causing her wife to scowl at her. 

“Don’t start something you can’t finish, mi amor ,” she murmured against her lips before pecking her softly. Later, cariño.” 

Her wife only nodded, their lips meeting again before a loud insistent knock echoed from the hallway. Rio froze, her heart pounding faster than the knocks. She stared down the hallway, not able to will her feet to move just yet until lips pressed against her cheek. She startled for a moment, meeting bright understanding blues. Her wife smiled at her, squeezing her shoulder as the simple touch brought her back. 

“It’ll be alright, my love. Go, don’t keep Abuelita waiting . ”

 

 

Chapter End Notes
