Chapter 16: La Familia Vidal

Chapter Summary

Agatha smiled, warm and knowing. “Ella ha estado–” (She’s been–)

“–cuido mucho a mi esposa, muchas gracias–” ( –I take great care of my wife, thank you very much– )

“ –sobreprotector,” Agatha finished smoothly, earning a sharp look from Rio. (–overprotective.)

OR :

Spoiler : Maria does not get hit by a bus... yet

Chapter Notes

I will answer comments soon from last chapter- currently half asleep so sorry if there are mistakes

I do not have siblings, so I really hope the fighting is good

Anyway, I love you all deeply

Love,

RioAllAlong_719 💚💜

See the end of the chapter for more notes

Rio

 

Rio hesitated for a moment longer before breathing out a long breath. She approached the door cautiously, smoothing out her own green sweater before opening it to the cold. The cold hit her face first, the snow blowing onto her front porch, but then warmth cradled her. She gasped as a frail but strong body was wrapping around her, a face burrowing into the crook of her shoulder. She smiled, all of the nerves and doubts ceasing to exist as her shoulders sagged. She took a deep breath in, meeting the rich ancient aroma of sandalwood mingling with the warmth of vetiver. She nuzzled in closer, finding the sweetness of orange blossoms, reminding her of her childhood in the hot summer sun of Puerto Rico. Then, the scent pulled away just slightly and warm, gentle, brittle hands were cupping her face as she pulled back to stare into brown eyes so dark they were almost black. 

“ Dioses míos, Riocita, mírate. Mi nieta hermosa. ¿Estás comiendo lo suficiente? Te ves delgada, nieta, ” her Abuelita fussed over her. (My gods, little Rio, look at you. My beautiful granddaughter. Are you eating enough? You’re looking thin, granddaughter.) 

“ Sí, Abuelita, estoy muy bien. ¿Cómo estuvo el vuelo? ” she beamed down at her grandmother finding the woman looked as if she had barely aged. (Yes, Abuelita, I am just fine. How was the flight?)

Her hair was long and silver, almost as long as her wife’s. Her face was full of wrinkles and her neck hidden from a green wool scarf wrapped tightly around her. Rio was quick to take her coat, guiding her Abuelita further inside the home to get her warm. 

Her Abuelita huffed, looking around, “ Los vuelos son cada vez más largos, Riocita .” (The flights are getting longer and longer, little Rio.)

She gasped as she was pulled into another hug, the old woman almost breaking her spin as she nuzzled back into her Abuelita ’s warmth.

“ Dioses, te extrañé, querida niña. ” 

“Rio!” a cheery voice made her eyes flick up to Oriana walking in with tray in her hands. 

They smiled at each other softly as her older sister held up a perfect tembleque . She was smaller than Rio, but with the same dark curls but way shorter. It was a sweet pixie cut, longer than the last time she saw her sister. Her eyes were the same as hers, but somehow softer. Oriana was the perfect picture of the softness of the Vidal genes. Her face was more round, her cheek bones softer and her jawline dull. 

“Oriana,” she smiled softly over Abuelita ’s shoulder.

But then her sister stepped to the side, allowing two figures to emerge through the door. They both were taller, more imposing especially the first figure. Maria, she was the picture of the sharp commanding features of a Vidal. Her hair was longer than Rio’s, she was taller, thinner but not as fit as Rio. Her cheekbones were sharp, her eyes almost cat-like and the same darkness of Abuelita ’s. Their eyes met and instantly, something darkened in Rio as Abuelita pulled away, turning towards the three sisters as the door behind Maria closed. Rio’s gaze shifted to the man behind her eldest sister. He was buff, his hair combed neatly to the side as he wore khakis and a nice blue button up, but to her surprise his hazel eyes were kind, if not shy. He stood behind her sister unsure as they began slipping out their shoes and hanging up their coats on the rack beside him. 

Abuelita beamed, stepping between the two sisters as she nodded to the man, “ Río, querido, este es Jack, el prometido de tu hermana. ” (Rio, my dear, this Jack, your sister’s fiance )

Her abuelita paused, staring at Maria who only smirked at Rio darkly, “Jack, baby, this is my little sister, Rio.” 

The man stepped forward shyly, offering his hand. His grip was light, hesitant as they shook hands. 

“ Él no habla español, es una pena realmente, ” Abuelita scowled. (He doesn’t speak Spanish– it’s a shame really.)

Rio almost choked on her own breath. For a moment, she thought she misheard. That maybe exhaustion had finally caught up with her, that her mind had twisted Abuelita’s words into something they weren’t. But no– there was no mistake. Ximena Vidal, the immovable matriarch, the force that had carved their family’s legacy into stone, had just dismissed Maria. The golden child. The perfect granddaughter. The heiress. 

Maria’s face didn’t fall, not completely– she was too proud for that– but Rio caught the sharp flicker of tension in her jaw, the way her smirk tightened like a pulled thread about to snap. She hadn’t expected that. Neither had Rio. Her sister had spent her whole life at Abuelita’s side, soaking in the praise, basking in the approval that had never been Rio’s to claim. While Rio had been the river, wild and untamed, Maria had been the shining sun, the favored one, the one who could do no wrong. Until now. And all it had taken was a fiancé who couldn’t speak Spanish. 

Rio flicked her gaze to Jack, who still stood there, awkward and unaware, shifting slightly as if he could sense the battle lines being drawn but had no idea what war had just begun. Poor guy. He had no clue what it meant to be a Vidal. No clue that his inability to speak their language was a crack in Maria’s perfect façade, a fracture in the golden image she had crafted so meticulously. And Rio? Rio was relishing every second of it. She had to bite down on the inside of her cheek to keep from laughing, pressing her lips into a thin, neutral line. 

Don’t smirk. Don’t smirk. Don’t–  

But Maria’s dark eyes snapped to her, sharp as knives, and Rio knew that Maria knew. She knew Rio had caught it, had witnessed that fleeting moment of imperfection. Of failure. Maria straightened, rolling her shoulders back, her grip tightening on Jack’s arm as if to remind herself that he was still a prize, even if he was less than ideal in Abuelita’s eyes. She recovered fast– she always did. 

“I suppose not everyone can be perfect,” Maria hummed, her voice smooth as silk but sharpened like a dagger. She tilted her head just so, her smirk returning, trying to regain her footing. “ Pero no te preocupes, Abuelita. Yo le enseñaré. ” (But not to worry, Abuelita. I’ll teach him.)

Rio almost barked out a laugh, Oh, she’s scrambling. 

Abuelita barely acknowledged her words, cupping Rio’s cheek with one hand as they listened to the timer of the oven from the kitchen, “¿Supongo que tu esposa está en la cocina?” (I assume your wife is in the kitchen?) 

“Sí, déjame ir a cenar, ” Rio said, already rushing around her Abuelita and into the kitchen. (Yes, let me go grab our dinner though.)

Her wife was about to stand from her place, but Rio’s sharp eyes narrowed to her, daring her to move as she reached for the oven mitts and pulled out the lamb. Just as she set the tray on the glass top stove, her sisters and Abuelita came into the kitchen. Oriana was first, setting her dessert down and wrapping Agatha into a warm hug. 

“Oh, Agatha, it’s been too long. How are you?” her sister greeted. 

Her wife gave her soft smile, “I’m wonderful, Oriana. The tembleque looks amazing.” 

Rio was half listening to their conversation, but something made her turn as her abuelita stepped into the kitchen, dark eyes trained on her wife. She watched as they flicked up and down her form, studying her face and then to her wife’s stomach. Abuelita ’s eyebrow raised as Oriana stepped away, leaving a clear view for Abuelita to study her wife. Then, those sharp eyes softened her mouth hanging open in shock, stepping towards her wife with speed that surprised Rio for an old lady. Rio found herself moving too, realizing that nothing passed Ximena. Her wife turned to her with a soft smile, and fuck, Agatha really was glowing. 

“ Abuela, que bueno verte ,” her wife smiled at her, but Ximena wasn’t listening to her. (It’s so good to see you.)

Everyone in the kitchen froze as Ximena stopped before Agatha, her hands raised over her stomach. Everything in Rio tightened. Her wife never had someone else other than Rio touch her belly and now, her abuelita, who did whatever she pleased, was reaching for her stomach. 

She couldn’t stop herself as her mouth opened, trying to stop the reigning matriarch, “ Abuelita, esperar–” ( Abuelita, wait–) 

“ Cállate, niña, ” her abuelita snapped, stopping her in her tracks. 

Then, her abuelita’s hands landed on their target, smoothing over her wife’s sweater so slowly, so carefully as if she were caressing something made of glass. The room held its breath as her wife relaxed under Abuelita’s touch, a hand resting over Abuelita’s wrinkled one, smiling down at the older woman. Rio stood frozen beside them, her breath caught waiting for something, some type of small praise, or even getting hit for not telling her, but none of it came. Instead, she watched as the unshakeable force, the untouchable– the woman who raised her who didn’t take any type of bullshit– cried. 

Tears poured from Abuelita’s eyes as her voice grew softer than anything Rio had heard in her entire thirty-three years of existence, “ Mi niña… estás trayendo vida a nuestra familia. ” (My girl… you are bringing life to our family.) 

Her abuelita’s voice, normally a commanding force that could silence an entire room, now trembled with emotion that was raw, unfiltered, and real. It was not the voice of the indomitable matriarch but of a grandmother, a woman who had carried generations of weight upon her back and was now witnessing something she had longed for. Agatha’s soft smile deepened, her fingers brushing over the back of Abuelita’s hand in reassurance, in quiet understanding. 

They both expected scrutiny, questions, maybe even skepticism, anything but this. Never tears, never the way Abuelita was staring at her wife like she was life itself. Rio felt something deep inside her chest tighten in a different way, and then it cracked open. Her abuelita never cried. Not when Rio had come home without her parents that night. Not when they attended their funeral. Not even when Abuelo passed, though through it all, Rio knew Abuelita had wept in the dark, in private, away from prying eyes. She went where no one could witness the grief of a woman who always held herself tall and proud, never breaking. 

Yet… here she was… breaking for Rio’s wife…breaking for Agatha…for the life they created… for the great-grandchild she had likely been waiting for the moment they had been married. 

Abuelita’s hands trembled as she pressed her palm a little firmer against the swell of Agatha’s belly, as if committing the shape of it to memory. 

“ Dios mío,” she whispered, her voice hoarse.

Then, she sniffed, gathering herself, swiping the wetness from her eyes as if embarrassed by her own reaction. She turned her gaze up to Agatha, and there it was: the warmth, the approval, the fierce love that so few ever earned from her. 

“ Eres una bendición para esta familia, Agatha, ” she declared, voice still thick with emotion but stronger now. (You are a blessing to this family, Agatha.) 

Rio felt the air leave her lungs, because Rio had never heard those words spoken– not to herself, not Oriana, and not even to Maria. Her wife was swept into a tight hug before Abuelita took her face into her hands, peppering kisses to her forehead like she did when Rio and her sisters were young. Then, Abuelita pulled away, turning to Rio and for a split second, she felt small. Not in the way Maria always made her feel, not in the way professors had made her feel when she was young and too smart-mouthed for her own good. No, this was different. This was ancestral. This was a granddaughter standing before the head of the family, a powerful force, awaiting judgement from the matriarch. Her abuelita’s eyes were still wet as arms reached for her. 

Rio froze, her eyes widening as Abuelita caressed her cheeks again. It wasn’t like Abuelita never did this– but she only did this in greeting after not seeing each other for a while or when she was proud. 

“ Ven aquí, niña, ” she said softly, and Rio moved before she could think. (Come here, child.) 

She stepped closer, hands stiff at her sides as if she didn’t know what to do with herself, until Abuelita reached out and grabbed her wrist, pulling her into her arms. Her mouth fell open as the hug shrouded her in warmth, and once more Rio could hardly understand what was happening. Her hugs were always warm, always a safe space, but this felt different. This was more than her warmth, more than her safety, more than her love, and slowly Rio started to realize this was her pride– something Maria and her constantly fought for. 

And then, just to make the realization hit harder, she whispered, voice thick with emotion, “ Hiciste bien, mi Riocita. ” (You did well, my little Rio.) 

And just like that, it broke Rio out of her stupor as she audibly gasped, her eyes burning as they glanced towards her wife. Agatha was smiling softly as her, her hand rubbing the side of her stomach. Their eyes met and for a moment Rio read from her wife, I told you she would be proud. Rio wanted to roll her eyes, to make a snarky comment at her, but all she could do was fist the back of Abuelita ’s blouse as she exhaled, shakingly slow and shuddering. 

The side of her neck grew wet with her abuelita’s tears and she just held her back, holding Abuelita together as she shook and cried into her. She watched as Oriana smiled at her, her own eyes glossing over as she stepped back, pushing Maria and Jack out with her. She didn’t miss the way her eldest sister’s mouth hung open in complete shock, and she revelled in it. 

When Abuelita finally pulled away, her wife was beside her, holding the envelope that contained the picture of the scan. With shaking hands, Abuelita opened, staring at the photos with wide eyes. Agatha leaned into Rio, her lips pressing against her cheek softly making Rio beam. Her own hand came down to her wife’s stomach, a steady reassurance for both of them that this was really happening. 

“ ¿Hasta– hasta dónde, querida?” Abuelita’s voice was hesitant for once. (How- how far, my dear?)

Agatha smiled, softly, “ Ahora tengo quince semanas, pero esas son las ecografías de la semana doce. Sabremos el sexo el día veintiocho. ” (I’m fifteen weeks now, but those are the twelve week scans. We find out the gender on the twenty-eighth.)

Abuelita blinked up at them for a moment before looking over Agatha once, twice, thrice, and, “ Vas a tener un niño, mi niña. ” (You are having a boy, my girl.)

Rio barked out a laugh, not able to help it as her wife gawked at Abuelita. Everything in her became giddy, her heart swelling as she shook her head, knowing her abuelita was right. Ximena was hardly wrong on things, and her knowing right away that her wife was pregnant, even with the sweater completely hiding the swell of her stomach, just proved Rio’s point further. Agatha scowled at her before poking her hard in the side just under her ribs. She yelped before pulling her closer and kissing her wife’s head despite her protests. 

“ Déjame adivinar, ustedes dos tienen una apuesta y por una vez, Río tiene razón,” Abuelita smirked. (Let me guess, you two have a bet and for once, Rio is right.) 

They both nodded, making Abuelita smirk but before anything else could be said, Oriana peaked in again, her face shy but brimming in excitement as she stepped back into the kitchen. She knew her sister wouldn’t be able to hold back any longer and was actually surprised Oriana even stepped out of the room in the first place. Abuelita gave her a small nod to come and her older sister practically dived into Rio. Rio gasped, staggering back as she tried wrestling her sister off of her, but it was futile despite being stronger. 

“ Por los dioses, por los dioses, Riocita, ¡vas a ser mamá! ¡Estás a punto de ser mamá! ¡YO VOY A SER TÍA!” she screeched into Rio’s eardrum, making her brain rattle as her skin crawled with the unwanted touch. (By the gods, by the gods, little Rio, you’re going to be a mom! You’re about to be a mom! I’M GOING TO BE AN AUNT!)

Rio pulled out of her grip, hiding behind her wife who was next to get assaulted by Oriana’s affections. Her wife merely laughed, hugging her sister back. 

“Oh, Agatha, this is so wonderful,” Oriana beamed, pulling away and reaching down but stopped herself, looking up for permission. 

Her wife nodded, allowing their child’s Tia to feel the roundness of her stomach where they grew. Rio smiled, watching her family. Her abuelita was still gushing over the scan while Oriana couldn’t stop crying. Her wife was still glowing and their eyes met with a silent understanding at how happy they made their family… but it didn’t last… they knew it wouldn’t. 

The room seemed to drop temperatures as a shadow loomed over Rio. She turned slowly, meeting the forced smile of her eldest sister. She could see the muscles in her sister’s cheeks working, holding it all together as Maria slowly cracked underneath it all. Her eyes were dark, calculating, flicking between them as she seemed to hold her breath, not wanting to drink in the poisonous gases that threatened her pride. 

Rio straightened herself, keeping her hand protectively over her wife’s stomach as her wife further leaned into her on instinct. Maria’s eyes dropped to her hand, watching as Agatha placed her own hand over Rio’s where the emerald ring sparkled, mocking Maria. Then something unreadable flashed through eyes as she tilted her head, her tongue pushing at her cheek before stepping closer.

“Well,” she started, her voice too strained, too practiced. “This is quite a surprise, Riocita. ”

Rio almost scoffed– almost but was stopped by something squeezing her hand. She didn’t need to look at her wife to know she was telling her to behave. 

However, the middle sister was completely oblivious to the building tension or didn’t care. She was bouncing, vibrating with excitement as she stared at the image with Abuelita.  

“This is just amazing, right, Maria? Oh, Rio’s gonna be a mom!” she practically squealed, making everyone in the room wince. “Oh, Riocita, I will be the best, most incredible–”

“ –excessively loud–” Rio muttered, earning a pinch from her wife.  

“Tia ever!” Oriana finished, completely unphased. “I mean really, who knew this day would come, but of course only Agatha could tame and put up with this one–” she gestured to Rio who shot her glare “ –and now a new generation of Vidals.” 

“ ¿Una nueva generación de Vidals? ” Maria let out a dark chuckle “¿Es este feto siquiera de sangre Vidal?” (A new generation of Vidals? Is this fetus even Vidal blood?)

It was meant to be a blow on Rio, the fact that Rio was not carrying– something that might disappoint Abuelita . But the word ‘ feto’ delivered the true blow for Rio. In truth, sure, it was a fetus– an unborn child, but the way her sister said it– the dismissive, easy nonchalance made Rio’s blood boil. She gritted her teeth, her mouth about to open as Abuelita snapped her head up, intrigued now, but Agatha’s grip on her hand tightened, silencing Rio.

“Nuestro hijo sólo tiene sangre Harkness-Vidal. No hubo esperma de donante. Usamos la médula ósea de Río, ” her wife spoke calmly, but her eyes were ablaze on Maria. (Our child has only Harkness-Vidal blood. There was no donor sperm. We used Rio’s bone marrow.)

Rio could feel the anger radiating off her wife, but it all dissipated as Abuelita beamed at them. 

“Un niño hecho desde cero,” Abuelita’s voice was in awe. 

“‘ Cero’?” Agatha looked up at Rio, her eyebrows creasing. 

“Scratch– she means a child made from scratch,” Rio murmured softly before nodding at Abuelita. 

Abuelita pressed her hands over her heart as she looked between them, between Rio’s wife’s stomach and her proud, stubborn stance. 

“ Ay, mija, ” she whispered, stepping forward to take Agatha’s hands into her own. “ Eres una maravilla. Una bendición. ¿Cómo te sientes? ¿Te ha estado cuidando bien mi nieta o sigue siendo una terca insoportable ?” (You are a marvel. A blessing. How do you feel? Has my granddaughter been taking good care of you, or is she still an insufferable stubborn thing?) 

Agatha smiled, warm and knowing. “ Ella ha estado– ” (She’s been– )

“–cuido mucho a mi esposa, muchas gracias–” ( –I take great care of my wife, thank you very much– )

“ –sobreprotector, ” Agatha finished smoothly, earning a sharp look from Rio. (–overprotective.)

Abuelita huffed before ushering Agatha toward the dining table, completely dismissing Rio in favor of her now favorite grandchild-in-law. She could only shake her head as her sister came beside her, helping her take the food into the dining room. Rio carried in the lamb as her sister carried in the potatoes and gravy. Abuelita was already sitting at the head of the dinner table, Maria on her right and Agatha on her left. Jack sat next to Maria, fidgeting as his gaze shifted between her wife and Abuelita, looking completely lost. She could laugh at how stupid he looked among the Vidals– well, mainly how stupid Maria looked bringing a man around who didn’t speak Spanish. 

In truth, Agatha didn’t speak Spanish when they met, but after three years of dating, she was pretty fluent as Rio would barely give her a break some days, forcing her to drown or swim. So, when Agatha met Abuelita after those three years, Abuelita was immediately taken with her. She still counted it as a win, especially as her wife and Abuelita were talking away. 

Oriana came back with the last of their sides as the family all sat down, Rio next to her wife, forcing Oriana to face their grandmother at the head of the table. Across from her, she caught Jack staring at the wall full of pictures and more accurately, Rio’s favorite from the wedding. She braced herself for it as his gaze met hers, but shocked by how quiet and sincere his words were. 

“The wedding must have been beautiful, Rio,” he said, just loud enough for Rio to hear. “Was it here in Westview?” 

Rio shared a polite smile, “No, we got married in Salem. And you, any plans for your wedding?” 

Maria raised an eyebrow as Jack went quiet, “Oh, so suddenly interested, hermanita .” 

Rio’s lip curled, “Not really, although I am curious who could be attracted to your stellar personality.” 

They went quiet, both of them staring each other down as Oriana started a conversation with Jack, easing him out of the warzone while Agatha and Abuelita remained oblivious. Abuelita was stacking her wife’s plate with food, asking her questions so fast, she was surprised her wife could understand. But Rio was hardly paying attention. She was locked in battle against her eldest sister as they both leaned suddenly forward, waiting to see who would break. Neither did, and the jabs continued. 

“You know, I just never thought I’d see the day,” Maria mused, taking a slow sip of wine. “You, a mother? With how reckless you used to be?” 

Rio didn’t even flinch, “And yet, here we are.” 

Maria’s lips twitched, “Agatha must have the patience of a saint, especially while pregnant.” 

Rio smirked, “Or she just really likes me.” 

Maria scoffed, looking at Agatha as if searching for some hidden flaw she had yet to find. Her wife must have finally gone to check on Rio and noticed the tension between them. A hand landed on her thigh, stopping her bouncing knee as she rubbed her leg. 

Her wife raised an eyebrow, but clearly tried to add humor to cool down the tension, “She grows on you.” 

Maria laughed, but it wasn’t out of humor as her head cocked to the side, her eyes flicking back to Rio, “Like cordyceps I’m sure.” 

The table stilled, everyone’s forks pausing mid-air as Abuelita sat up straighter eyeing her granddaughters. Rio didn’t miss the way Abuelita eyed them, but she ultimately said nothing as Rio’s jaw clenched tighter, her grip on her own fork starting to bend the metal. Her wife kept her hand on her thigh, rubbing it up and down to sooth her, but Rio hardly noticed it as from the other side of her, Oriana groaned. 

“Here we go,” she muttered, setting her fork down and taking a long drink of wine.

Rio leaned forward more, her gaze refusing to leave Maria’s, “Careful, Maria” she mocked her “your green is showing.” 

Her sister’s smile was sharp and unforgiving, as she scoffed, “Jealous? You are saying that I am jealous? Of what?” 

Rio leaned back, a smirk spreading across her lips as her wife squeezed her thigh hard in warning, but she ignored it again, “That I have something that you don’t.” 

With that, she took Agatha’s hand and kissed the back of it, deliberate and taunting. Just as she predicted, Maria’s jaw clenched harder as her eye twitched. 

“Ah,” Rio hummed. “There it is.” 

Maria set her glass down hard, both hands on the table as she leaned closer, baring her teeth like a predator as her eyes grew impossibly darker, “You act like you’re so special, Rio, like you’ve done something so remarkable, but let’s not forget– you’ve always been a selfish, reckless, brat!” 

Something simmered deep inside Rio as her own jaw clenched, her hand leaving her wife’s as she sat up straighter, her shoulders squaring, her own voice coming out in a growl, “Selfish?” 

Her sister’s eyes shimmered with something she couldn’t name but everything in Rio dropped with Maria’s next words, “Do you remember that night?” 

Rio’s heart stopped for a moment, the hair raising on her arms as her breath quickened.

No, she wouldn’t , Rio thoughts scrambled. 

She couldn’t fathom this was where her sister was taking the conversation. Sure, they fought nastily, always with the jabs and aiming to tear each other down, but there was something else in her sister that Rio now saw. There wasn’t a mask of disgust or that look that told Rio that Maria was better than her. No, somehow this was worse. This was real anger combined with something the whole family shared– something they each dealt with privately, but Rio knew her sister’s grief led her to this. She couldn’t speak, couldn’t utter a word, couldn’t even signal Abuelita for help. Instead, Rio felt her mouth hang open, her eyes wide and fearful, her body tensing up like a deer in the headlights 

Maria’s voice was laced with venom and melancholy, “The night you snuck out? ¿Cuándo te escapaste como siempre lo hacías? Te marchaste de Río, los dejaste en el hotel y no habrían estado allí si no fuera por ti.” (When you snuck out like you always did? You left Rio, leaving them at the hotel and they wouldn’t have been there if it weren’t for you.) 

Rio could only hear her voice and the pounding in her ears as the weight of the words struck her like sharpened stones. Everything in Rio’s mind screamed as the faint smell of ash entered her senses. There was a flash of orange somewhere and loud yells from a firefighter telling someone to stop her. She blinked as tears welled up in her eyes and it was then, Rio realized why Maria was never close to her, why Maria always pushed her away, looked at her with disgust– it was because she blamed her for their parent’s death. 

I was eight. I was eight. I was eight, her mind chanted over and over again. 

“ ¿Y luego lo tienes todo? ¿Una esposa y ahora un maldito hijo?” Maria dug the dagger deeper into Rio. (And then you have it all? A wife and now a fucking kid?)

But the dagger didn’t stop. Instead, it tore into her organs, plunging into her soul where her deepest regret lay buried. At one point she had that constant thought for years, until she accepted she was only eight, playing on in her mind like a broken record. 

 “ Deberías haber muerto con ellos.”

(You should have died with them). 

 

Everything died in that moment. The air became nonexistent and she swore the plants in their home started wilting around her, shrivelling before vanishing into dust. Her vision blurred as she flinched back, everything in her about to spring up and run, or spring up and launch herself across the table, she wasn’t sure. Everything around her slowed as she watched everyone’s reactions like she was outside of herself– like she was in the third person. Jack had grown rigid, looking at her sister with disbelief and maybe disgust– like he understood the words that were spoken. Oriana’s mouth was wide open, tears dripping down her face as her gaze was focused on the oldest. Her wife was stoic, not an emotion to be read on her face as she seemed to be processing the rancor from Maria’s words but Abuelita? Abuelita had a look she had never seen before. 

Then, time started again and a coldness settled over Rio, as if she had been left outside in the middle of night during a snowstorm, and had been refused entry into the cabin. It was the final blow, the one she never had been ready for, the one she never imagined Maria would say aloud. 

I should have died with them. 

The words strangled her like a python squeezing the life out of its prey before delivering the final blow. Rio’s breath was ragged and then it hitched, a sharp inhale that barely made it through her lungs. Her fingers were trembling, her teeth starting to chatter. Now, she could truly hear it: the sirens, the screams, the explosion–

She still remembered the way the fire cackled at her, how the smoke forced her mouth open and filled her tiny lungs. There was the phantom feeling of large arms dragging her away from the heat that blistered her skin. Her own arms were flailing, screaming, fighting because she had to go back in– she had to get to them. The night had never left her, never truly left her despite so many times of shoving the memories into their retrospective boxes and burying them deep within her, and Maria had found it, shovelled it up with a few words. 

A sharp CRACK snapped Rio out of her chasm of thoughts, the sound echoing through the room. Abuelita’s hand was firm against the table as she stood, towering over Maria. 

“ ¡Cállate la maldita boca, Maria! ” (Shut your damn mouth, Maria!) 

The sheer fury in her grandmother’s voice was something none of the sisters had ever witnessed before, especially Rio for all of her years of being scolded or even doted on with tough love. This was rage in all of its glory, burying Maria in its hot ash like Pompeii had done to her victims. Her dark eyes burned further, turning Maria to stone, body frozen in time. 

“ ¡¿Cómo te atreves?! ” (How dare you?!) 

Maria flinched… she flinched. 

“ ¿Diciendo esas cosas a tu propia hermana? ” (Saying those things to your own sister?) Abuelita’s voice shook the room, her fury vibrating in every syllable. “ Eres una vergüenza. A tu familia, a tus padres, a mí . ” (You are a disgrace. To your family, to your parents, to me .) 

Maria’s lips parted as if to argue, but Abuelita had other plans and no one interrupted the reigning monarch of the Vidal family, “ ¿Quieres echarle la culpa a Rio? A una niña de ocho años, ¡cuando deberías estar mirando en un espejo, Maria! ” (You want to blame Rio? An eight-year-old child, when you should be looking in a mirror, Maria!) 

Maria tongued at her cheek before her head tilted her eyes creasing, “ Abuelita , I– ” 

BANG! 

The entire table shook, the plates rattled, a wine glass shattered, and everyone, including Abuelita, jumped. All heads snapped to Agatha whose hands had slammed down against the polish wood and Rio swore there were cracks underneath her palms. She radiated a power, even as her movements were slow, standing up, the chair screeching across the floor as she towered over everyone. If Agatha was a witch of fictional powers, Rio swore there would be lightning of purple crackling around her, a storm of a witch who reached her limit. 

When she finally spoke, her voice was calm, but there was something else, something that even Abuelita flinched back away from her. Her voice was a weapon itself, carrying into the room before hitting its target with devastating accuracy. 

“ Get. Out.”

Maria blinked as no one dared to move, but her wife didn’t waver, no– the woman didn’t blink, didn’t even glance at Rio whose heart was hammering so fast and her vision was blurring, she was sure she was going to pass out. 

But the sheer audacity of this bitch as she scoffed, “Oh, now you’re talking?” 

Agatha’s lip curled, “ You do not come into my home, insult my wife, and then play the fucking victim.” 

Maria stood slowly, towering over her wife, but the only movement from her was a simple hand to her stomach which made Rio’s heart crack, but she could not move. She could not sit her pregnant wife down, who should be resting, who shouldn’t be on her feet for too long, who shouldn’t be dealing with the stress of the Vidal family. 

Maria’s smile was evil as she leaned forward, “Oh, and now you’re defending her? After everything? How noble–”

“I said get out!” Agatha snarled, her voice vibrating through the room, reaching Rio’s very bones. 

And for the first time, Maria staggered, her façade dropping as even Rio had never seen her wife this angry. She was seething like a cornered dog, her chest heaving, her hand rubbing circles on her stomach as if it was the only thing keeping her from launching herself across the table. 

 Agatha’s lips curled, her tone venomous, controlled, deadly, “You will not speak to my wife that way. You will not bring your unresolved grief, your bitterness, your own self-hatred and use it against her just because you’re too much of a coward to face it yourself.”

Maria didn’t move. 

“I don’t care what grudge you carry. I don’t care what you think justice is in life, or what you think my wife deserves. You don’t speak of our unborn child like that! And you sure as fucking hell don’t get to sit at my table in my house and decide whether my wife should be alive or not.” 

Once more no one spoke. Everyone’s breaths were trapped as Maria stared at Agatha, lips now pressing in a thin line. Her shoulders heaved and Rio now saw how glossy her sister’s eyes were. Her wife’s eyes burned holes into her skull, daring her to say something, to fight back, or do something reckless just so Agatha could launch herself across the table, and… it was every bit of the woman Rio had fallen in love with… a woman whose love was quiet, unshakeable, and came in a storm when matters called for it. 

Maria’s gaze flicked to Rio’s, to Oriana’s, and then to Abuelita ’s who just sat down, refusing her gaze, and with one final glance at Rio, she turned on her heel, heading towards the front door. Jack was on her heels, but no one else moved, not until the front door closed with a slam. 

And it was only then did the breath return, but the first inhale was like inhaling the frigid air of the top of a mountain. Rio didn’t think, didn’t look, didn’t even realize her body was moving until she opened the door to their bedroom– until her legs gave out. She collapsed, her knees slamming into the carpet of their room as she buried her face into their quilt to muffle her screams as grief consumed her, as if she was there again, before the burning hotel, and instead of being that shocked eight-year-old girl, she was a thirty-three-year-old woman who had lost her parents.

 

 

Chapter End Notes

sorry....

I am really putting Rio through it, but like im emotional because I think I miss my wife... i just talked to her and I have to wait three more months until seeing her

(she's not my wife- she's straight and my bsf. #sobsinsingle)

#there is reason I am writing this story LOL

Chapter 17: There and Known

Chapter Summary

She looked up, finding tears in her wife’s eyes, as she laughed lightly, shaking her head, “Goddamnit, Rio, I’m supposed to be taking care of you.”
Rio pulled her underwear down before standing up before her wife, pulling her flushed against her, “We will take care of each other then.”
Agatha pushed her, shaking her head as tears poured faster down her cheeks, “Just get in the damn tub.”

OR:

Some more Vidal chaos and Agatha trying to take care of Rio, but Rio can’t help but to take care of her pregnant wife.

Chapter Notes

Sorry this took me so long. I’m currently posting this from an airport in Greece, and I had trouble writing some of the scenes. This hit a little too close to home as I lost my grandma back in 2022.

Please bare (bear? I’m sleep deprived) with me for formatting and any mistakes. I hate writing on my phone.

Anyway, I love you all. Thank you for comments and kudos.

Enjoy 💚💜

Love, RioAllAlong_719

See the end of the chapter for more notes

Ximena Vidal 

 

Ximena felt the breath leave her body as the words left her eldest grandchild’s mouth. She moved on instinct and in truth the rest was a blur until the sudden bang. She remembered her head turning, revealing her granddaughter’s wife in all her glory. A vein was throbbing from her forehead, her eyebrows pinched together painfully, and her fingers digging into the wood as her voice boomed through the house. She had sat down, feeling the power from the mother-to-be but moreso, the unconditional love the woman had for her granddaughter, but there was something more Ximena saw. She remembered their wedding well, how they had looked at each other– really looked at each. She remembered their vows, their first kiss as wives, and their first dance– the way they held each other… Ximena knew that Agatha Harkness was the love of her granddaughter’s life– and her dear Riocita, her wild, free-spirited, rebellious, and reckless granddaughter was the first to find her one. But the moment that had just passed, the power and love that radiated from Agatha made Ximena realize that this was more than unconditional love– this was two souls who were so entangled together, who were mirrors of each other, who were each other’s other half. 

Rio Vidal found her soulmate.

 

Ximena was glad that the eldest left, but some part of her broke. She always knew that the two sisters never got along, competing for her attention and approval and Ximena admitted that she wasn’t entirely fair with her granddaughters, but unlike Maria, Rio didn’t need coddling. Rio was the strongest of all of her grandchildren and Rio never ceased to amaze her, and now she was about to become a mother… but instead of celebrating, Maria had to ruin it and in that moment, Ximena felt like she lost a granddaughter. She had watched the woman she barely recognized leave before she heard the quick footsteps of her youngest running down the hall.

Maria struck Rio hard and Ximena knew even the strongest would crumble under what she had said. She stayed seated as Agatha turned, hurrying after her wife but the Oriana was also on the move. 

“Déjala, Ori,” she said softly, making the middle sister freeze in her tracks. (Leave her, Ori.)

Oriana stared after Agatha’s retreating form until they heard the door close softly behind, but before they heard the click of the door, they both winced hearing the youngest granddaughter’s muffled sobs. 

Oriana turned back to her, tears running down from her light brown eyes, “Pero ella es mi hermana.” (But she’s my sister.)

Ximena nodded her head slowly as her heart twisted at Oriana’s defeated voice– a voice that carried the weight of a sorrow that no words could truly untangle. Both of them felt the sudden change, the sudden unravelment of their family, and what was once strong, was now torn, but Maria? Maria made her choice and unless she came back crawling, Ximena did not have it in her to look at her. Oriana looked more torn, suffocated by pressure of being a twin and an older sister, but Ximena could see it– could see how Oriana was further from the twin she used to spend her every waking moment with. Maria’s words had not just struck Rio, but tore a wound so deep that even the strongest of hearts could not withstand– and Oriana, she truly was all heart.

Oriana’s eyes were clouded with unshed tears and she could only sigh, “Ya lo sabes, mi niña. Pero ni siquiera las hermanas pueden arreglarlo todo. Sabes lo fuerte y terca que es Rio, pero permítele el espacio para que finalmente se abra paso con la seguridad que se merece–”  (I know, my girl. But even sisters can not fix everything. You know how strong Rio is and how stubborn, but allow her the space to finally break in the safety she deserves–)

“Pero yo soy su hermana,” she repeated. “Soy su lugar seguro.”  (But I am her sister. I am her safe space–)

Ximena held up a hand to silence her, shaking her head. She allowed silence to fill between them as she studied the middle sister. Her lips quivered as her gaze narrowed back to the door where Agatha had disappeared, her fingers twitching at her sides. She followed her gaze as a soft smile graced her lips. 

“Los ves, Ori… ¿no? Ves la forma en que Rio mira a Agatha como si fuera la luna. Y Rio, claro que es un río, pero se convierte en un océano, sus olas se mueven únicamente para Agatha, se extienden hacia ella. Ves la suavidad en los ojos de Agatha cuando mira a Rio, la forma en que su luz solo brilla para su esposa, un amor como ningún otro. He visto la forma en que Rio ha cambiado por ella, cómo se ha suavizado, cómo ha encontrado la paz en su caos. Agatha es la luz que guía a Rio, su ancla, una presencia que le da estabilidad.”

 (You see them, Ori… don’t you? You see the way Rio looks at Agatha like she is the moon. And Rio, sure a river, but she becomes an ocean, her waves solely moving for Agatha, reaching out to her. You see the softness in Agatha’s eyes when she gazes at Rio, the way her light only shines for her wife– a love like no other. I’ve seen the way Rio has changed for her, how she’s softened, how she’s found peace in her chaos. Agatha is Rio’s guiding light, her anchor, a grounding presence.) 

“¿Y María?” she swallowed hard, her gaze dropping to the floor as they flinched again, hearing a sob echo from down the hall. (And Maria?)

“Maria,” she snapped, a little too harshly as Oriana winced but nevertheless held her gaze. “Ella ya tomó su decisión. Ahora, come un poco. Démosle espacio a tu hermana pequeña.” (She has made her choice. Now, eat a little. Let’s give your little sister some space.)

 

They ate in silence. Oriana pushed her food around before Ximena snapped telling her to just eat. Neither of them could stomach the food though and eventually without a word, they stood and started cleaning up together. Ximena wrapped both Agatha’s and Rio’s plates for later, placing them in the fridge until both Abuelita and granddaughter were going through the unfamiliar space, searching for containers, finding where the plates went and the silverware.

Once everything was clean, the pair found themselves in the living room, Oriana flipping through Netflix as Ximena stood, staring down at the door. It had been almost two hours and she couldn’t stop the worry from eating away at her. It wasn’t like she could leave. Oriana checked and found that Maria had fled using the car they all came here with, and she knew Maria wasn’t going to come back, especially as half way through their cleaning, Oriana told her that she received a text from Jack letting them know that he had decided not to marry Maria but thanked them for their kindness. Ximena had smiled, happy for the boy to actually go find someone who would treat him right. Other than his lack of Spanish, he was a good man– too good for Maria. 

The weight of anxiety gripped her chest, but without a word, she stepped down the hallway, her movements deliberate, sure– just as she had always been. She approached the door and, with the care she reserved for the most precious moments, turned the handle. The room inside was bathed in the soft glow of a single lamp, the space surprisingly immaculate. Rio, in her youth, would have never been so neat. 

The sight of it almost felt unfamiliar. In the center of the room was a large bed, its covers a gentle tapestry of green and lavender, the colors interwoven like a quiet promise. And there, nestled into the warmth of the sheets, was her granddaughter. Her heart stilled as she watched Riocita, curled on her side, her face pressed into the skin of her wife’s neck, seeking comfort in the familiar embrace. Her hand moved in slow, gentle circles across Agatha’s belly– an act of love, of care, of quiet reverence. Agatha, equally curled, held Rio close, pressing soft kisses into her hair, each breath a testament to the calm that had settled over them both. Whatever tears had once fallen had dried, leaving only the peace of shared intimacy. 

For a moment, Ximena felt like an intruder– a stranger in a room that should have felt like home. She had never seen Rio so small, so vulnerable. Even at eight, covered in soot, smelling of smoke and fire, Rio had always been a force of nature. Now, here she was– stripped of her walls, her strength laid bare, trusting someone else to hold the pieces of her together. Rio shifted, nuzzling deeper into Agatha, refusing to raise her head. Just as Ximena considered stepping away, Agatha’s eyes met hers, a soft, understanding smile spreading across her face. 

“Mi amor, Abuelita está aquí,” Agatha whispered, her voice a tender thread of connection. 

“Querida, ven a la sala. Tenemos mucho de qué hablar. Deberíamos estar celebrando,” Ximena said slowly, her words measured and calm. (Honey, come to the living room. We have a lot to talk about. We should be celebrating.)

Rio had spoken enough for the moment. It was time. Time to ease her granddaughter’s mind, to show her how proud she truly was. Rio had come so far– from a lost, soot-streaked child, to a rebellious teenager, to a reckless college student, to a wife– and now, to a mother-to-be. Ximena would ensure that this moment, this summit of Rio’s life, would not be clouded by doubts or shadows. She would hold it in her heart, and no one– no one– would take this joy from her granddaughter.

 

Rio

 

Her body twitched before her eyes flashed open, taking in her surroundings. She was in her room, the morning light just peaking through the blinds as her whole body felt heavy. Her eyes were strained, burning as she reached up, rubbing the crusts away before sitting up slightly. The moment she blinked again, the anger came rushing back, Maria’s words rattling in her brain and then came her wife’s soothing voice, her warmth wrapping around her as Rio cried for the first time over her parents’ deaths. She remembered the way her wife just held her without judgment, and only with a steady understanding, care, and love. When she finally managed to calm down, her wife reminded her of the cycle— how all things eventually lead to death and it was their time. 

Rio knew to anyone else it would anger them, but Agatha knew her— knew she took soul comfort in the understanding of the green and the sacred balance. And with that, Rio relaxed, her tears ceasing and she knew it wasn’t her fault— but it didn’t stop the anger that radiated through her being. After all these years, after all the bickering, the stupid fights she and Maria had, she never knew it stemmed from the mere fact Maria thought she should have died too. 

But in that moment, with her face tucked into her wife’s neck and her hands on her wife’s growing belly— all she could do was lean into it, revel in the love she had and life she had created. Agatha held her until Abuelita came in, inviting them back to the living room and the whole time, Abuelita looked at her with nothing but pride. They talked of the baby, possible names, the plans for the future, and then Abuelita’s approval of her wife’s choice of breastfeeding. They had laughed when Abuelita told Agatha to lift her shirt so she could check but Rio quickly stepped in when she realized Abuelita was serious about checking her wife’s breasts. 

Her wife’s privacy would already be compromised enough from their future checkups and finally the birth where she knew her wife will be pissed when a doctor reaches their hands up her vagina. The least Rio could give her was to stop her Abuelita from doing— whatever she wanted to check. After a few words back and forth, her Abuelita stepped back before asking about doing a home birth which Rio had groaned telling her that they felt safer going to the hospital. More-so, Rio had done enough research to the point it made her sick thinking of the pain her wife would go through when she goes into labor. The idea of a natural birth made her stomach twist, wanting nothing more than her wife to not be in pain. Of course, the pain would be inevitable but if there was medicine to stop the majority of it, Rio wanted that for her wife. 

In truth, that was all she remembered from last night— well except for when Agatha had to tell the story of her after anesthesia and how she had flirted non stop with Agatha. Oriana had gushed and Rio felt like her reputation was ruined by her own wife. Rio didn’t do soft, unless it was with Agatha Harkness. But after that, the rest was blurry and she guessed at that point, the exhaustion from her tears and anger won out and she must have fallen asleep in Agatha’s arms on the couch. 

Rio turned beside her, finding her wife circled up against her, her face serene, a tiny snore leaving her mouth, and her body pressed against Rio further. A smile graced Rio’s lips as her hand came down, running through the tangles and smoothing it out, but the smile faded into concern as she wondered how she got in bed. She knew Agatha could carry her but Abuelita would never allow her— she couldn’t have allowed her. Yet, here she was in bed, in a white tank top and her boxers.

A soft hum made her gaze back down to her wife, realizing she was dreaming. Rio smirked, moving her body down so they were face to face as she watched as her wife’s eyebrows scrunched together. Rio cocked her head, wondering if Agatha was having nightmares again. She had woken her wife up from plenty in the past and was usually good at telling when one was going on but there was a different expression on Agatha’s face, one that spoke that she was confused and anxious. 

Rio sighed, leaning forward and placing a soft kiss on her lips before moving to her cheeks, pepping her kisses there lightly to rouse her wife. For a moment, her wife protested before blinking open her eyes in a slight daze. Blue eyes blinked at her and Rio internally cringed. Perhaps it was not a nightmare and now her pregnant hormonal wife was going to kill her. She braced herself, but instead of her wife cussing her out for waking her up so early in the morning, a soft smile graced her lips. 

Her wife leaned forward, capturing Rio’s lips in a sweet kiss. She felt her eyes close, a hand placed on her cheek to draw her closer. Rio moved her body closer, pressing up against her wife, smiling as her belly stopped her from being flushed against her. Rio reached down, her hand against the smooth skin as her thumb brushed over top of hers. 

“I dreamt about you,” her wife murmured against her lips.

Rio smirked, knowing usually when she was in Agatha’s dreams, it usually led to slow morning sex. She pressed her nose against hers, getting blue eyes to open, finding her pupils blown. 

Biting her lip, she whispered, “Tell me about it?”

Both hands were on her, one cupping her check, the other on her chest as her wife avoided her gaze now, a shy blush creeping in on her. 

“We were in bed,” she started slowly. “I was on my back with you on top of me. We were kissing, your fingers inside me and mine inside you. It was perfect, you were so deep, so perfect. It didn’t take long and we came together. The moment I calmed down though, there was a cry. Not like you or I, but it came from down the hall. You smiled, pulling away and grabbing your robe. And you said simply that you would grab him and then someone woke me up.”

As she spoke Rio was pressing hot opened mouth kisses down her wife’s throat, but paused at the last sentence, peering up at her wife with wonder. Agatha smiled at her softly, caressing her cheek with tender hands. 

“Soon, mi amor,” she whispered before her lips latched to hers. 

A tongue licked into her mouth, hot and desperate, swirling with hers as she took in the perfect taste of her wife. She gasped but ultimately melted, moving over her until her wife was on her back. “Rio—”

Rio smirked, drinking in the sight of her— lips swollen, cheeks flushed, body writhing beneath her like she was made to be there. She leaned in, biting down on Agatha’s bottom lip before soothing it with her tongue. 

”¿Me sientes, cariño?” Rio’s voice was a low hum, her fingers curling up again. (Do you feel me, darling?) 

Agatha moaned, back arching, her hands gripping Rio’s shoulders tighter in reply. 

“Eso es, déjamelo todo a mí.” (That’s it, leave everything to me.) 

She quickened her pace, her fingers curling with each deep thrust as her palm rubbed her wife’s clit. She smirked as Agatha let out a sharp cry.
