“Strange, though,” she mused. “Because I distinctly remember Rio mentioning they had no contact with you for… reasons–” she trailed off before finishing, her tone sharpening. “Reasons not appropriate for this setting, Mrs. Harkness.” 

Evanora opened her mouth, realizing she was losing them, but Dr. Carvallo spoke first. 

“Mrs. Harkness,” she said evenly, “while our university prides itself on excellence– on perfection, even– on fostering those who thrive under pressure, we hold family as our highest value. If your company– if you – cannot do the same, then we do not believe this partnership serves the Salmite’s best interests. Thank you for your time.” 

Both professors rose swiftly, gathering their things. But before they could walk away, Dr. Dylan, the taller of the two,  paused. She turned back, her sharp gaze settling on Evanora. 

“I thought you’d like to know,” she said coolly, “that my wife and I” –she gestured toward Dr. Carvallo– “were the ones who paid for their rings. We don’t typically involve ourselves in student affairs, but Agatha and Rio? They’re brilliant. And they deserve to be happy.”

 

It was hard, like a thousand jellyfish stings to her cheek when they walked away, their fingers intertwining. She bit back the slur, huffing before contacting someone personal she hired. Within minutes she had the address to their new apartment, confronting her daughter and trying to drag her out, and then, voilà – a shiny heavy restraining order slapped on her desk the next day. 

 

Next thing she knew it was fifteen years since Agatha ran away and Evanora gave up.  Agatha was no longer her daughter. So, she failed that one– but Evanora managed to convince herself, it wasn’t her fault. It wasn’t. She was perfect. Agatha just had to be the balance– the sinful, disgusting, evil thing. 

 

And then the obsession began. 

Evanora was out one day in the city when she saw her daughter. She was with that woman, their hands entwined as the woman carried their shopping bags. Her daughter was older, still holding the unnerving beauty as the woman beside her was guiding her through the crowd with ease. 

So Evanora got curious. She hired the right person to help her hack Agatha’s and Rio’s Instagrams. She couldn’t risk somebody following them around, but this was enough for now. Their accounts were private, their names only “Rio.H-V” and “Agatha.H-V” and no profile pictures. It was hard to find them, but when she did, she found that Rio posted more often, a whopping seven posts, but her stories were always full of their life. Agatha had only three posts : one from a date of her and Rio, one of their engagement photoshoot, and one of their wedding… almost ten years ago. 

Rio posted everything on her story, spamming it from time to time with moments between her and Agatha. Everything she posted was either her garden or Agatha. Her fingers were always tightening as she tapped through Rio’s endless stream of stories, each one another glimpse into the life her daughter had built– one she had no place in. The stories were intimate, clearly only made to show for the few people that followed Rio :  close friends and family. 

Mornings in their home, sunlight spilling through sheer curtains, casting golden light over a kitchen cluttered with plants. A steaming cup of coffee on the windowsill, captioned: Mañana . A hand adorned with a large emerald ring reaching for it, disappearing out of frame. A sleepy voice in the background murmuring something unintelligible, Rio’s quiet laugh following. 

Then, the garden. Rows of plants, wild and thriving, vines curling around trellises, bursts of purple from flowers Evanora didn’t care to name. Some days, the posts were just close-ups of petals dusted with morning dew, others were videos of Rio’s hands carefully tending to them. One day, Rio had taken a picture of a brand new greenhouse, capturing it : sin embargo…Ella es mi regalo más grande. 

Whatever the fuck that meant. 

But that led to glimpses of Agatha in the greenhouse with Rio. She would be barefoot, a book in her lap, or lying on a blanket, eyes closed, utterly at peace. Sometimes there were pictures of them on the hill in what seemed to be their yard. Pictures of the stars, Agatha’s head resting on her shoulder and stupid a caption like : She says she loves the stories about the stars, but I know it’s just an excuse to listen to my voice.

And then there were the posts that made Evanora’s blood boil and evaporate. The ones where Agatha wasn’t just a shadow in the background but the shining star of Rio’s night sky. 

A video of Agatha cooking, stirring a pot as Rio zoomed in on her face. Mi chef favorita , the caption read, followed by a teasing, “Even if she burns things sometime s .” Agatha shot a playful glare at the camera, shaking her head before muttering, “You’re so annoying.” 

Another, Agatha sitting on their couch, glasses perched on her nose, deep in a book. The caption: She’s been like this for three hours. Send help . The next clip, Agatha looking up with an unimpressed expression, “I can hear you, Rio.” 

And then, the moments that felt like a slap to the face. 

A short, grainy video of Agatha asleep against Rio, her body on top of her, her face buried in her neck, and her breath slow and steady. Rio’s hand ran through her dark thick tangles, voice barely above a whisper as she said, “ Siempre tan hermosa .” 

A photo from an anniversary dinner, their hands entwined over candlelight. The caption: A decade and still the love of my life . 

A clip of Agatha throwing her head back laughing : Shining brighter than the stars . 

Someone else taking a video of them while they were out hiking : Agatha jumping on Rio’s back, complaining, “Carry me.” 

“ Mi amor–” Rio laughing and stumbling slightly before uprighting herself.

“Just until we get to the top, my love,” Agatha begging. 

The caption : Third wheeling with these idiots. Someone push me off a cliff .

 

Every post, every video, every little comment or song that Rio posted, mocked Evanora. She thought she hated her daughter, but Rio… Rio fucking Vidal… 

The obsession deepened. 

Evanora found herself checking at odd hours, waiting for the next update, watching as Agatha’s life continued without her. And then one day, it all stopped. Rio wasn’t as active on Instagram. The stories ceased. Just silence, eerie silence and Evanora got her hopes up. Maybe this was it. Maybe they finally ended the fantasy, but something told her to keep checking, because the posts and highlights were still there. The same “H-V” remained. She had tried checking their work, going on Westview University’s website and Instagram, but it was the same, and she knew they both went by their maiden name for their PhD’s. 

She checked their friends’ Instagrams, only finding one from someone named Kingo. He was a famous actor in Bollywood and tagged them in one photo during what they called “Yule.” Her lip had curled, realizing it was a mockery– a defiance against Christianity. But a large group of people sat on a couch and behind it was this Kingo, a few other people, some old hag beside Rio, and Agatha. Evanora had stared at her daughter, not understanding why she looked so different. The layer of bodies and the couch covered most of her, just her head really showing, but there was a weird softness that Evanora couldn’t recognize. Her hair was shinier, fuller– it had to be a filter. 

 

So every day, right before bed and once she woke up, sometimes during lunch, she checked Rio’s Instagram. Radio silence. Nothing. For six months, there was absolutely nothing. It didn’t make sense. 

It was a weekend when Evanora decided to check her phone in the middle of the day. And finally, finally, there was a glowing orange and purple ring around Rio’s profile. Evanora vibrated with excitement, her fingers tightening around the phone, biting her lip as she sat up on her couch. This was it. After six months, half a year of nothing, of waiting, of absolute silence– something.

Her thumb tapped the screen before she could think and it opened to a living room, a familiar living room from Kingo’s post. The colors were dark and floral of the home, but Evanora didn’t care for that, she was staring at her daughter lounging on the couch, glasses perched on her nose as she seemed to be grading papers, but her head was turned slightly downward, watching something with lazy amusement, a smirk gracing her lips. Evanora’s gaze moved to a fluff ball– a bunny sprawled across Agatha’s belly. A belly that was full… rounded… not with indulgence, not with a natural curve of woman’s shape, no… 

No… 

Pregnant. 

The realization slammed into her, sucking the air from her lungs. Agatha. Pregnant. Her body went rigid, fingers digging into the phone case. Her mind screamed at her to look away, to close the app, to do anything other than sit there, helpless, as the world reshaped itself before her eyes. But she couldn’t. Because the video continued. 

The rabbit twitched its nose, purring and nuzzling further into her daughter. Agatha released a soft chuckle, a finger scratching behind its ears. Then– a honeyed hand with an amethyst ring, ran over the curve and around the bunny. Evanora’s heart was beating in her skull, smacking against it like a baseball bat continuously, as if her brains were getting bashed out from the inside- out. 

“I think Señor Scratchy can feel the baby,” Agatha murmured.

Rio scoffed, a fake annoyance in her voice, “Amazing, my child has yet to grace me with a kick, but the rabbit gets to feel special.”

Her daughtered laughed, low and warm, tilting her head back against the couch cushions, “I think our child knows he is harmless.” 

The woman behind the camera let out a mock gasp, her palm pressing more firmly against her stomach, “Please, that demon has bitten more times than I can count ever since he chose you over me– and I rescued him.” 

The bunny twitched his nose, but didn’t move. 

Her daughter just laughed again, “You’ll feel our baby soon, my love. Have a little more patience.” 

Evanora looked down at the caption before the story closed out : Bested by a fucking bunny .

She sat frozen, her entire body locked in place like cement had hardened onto her ligaments and bones. Her breathing came out ragged as everything turned red, her grip so hard, part of her screen cracked, but she didn’t care– because her abomination of a daughter– her evil, wicked child was not only ‘married’ that woman, but was having her child. 

 

And you would be a fool to think Evanora was going to sit back and do nothing. 

 

Restraining order be damned.

 

 

Agatha

 

Her weight moved off the podium as she read from The Tempest, her voice carrying into the silence of the hall, with only the few scribbling of pens echoing underneath her. She needed to move, focusing all her attention on reading instead of the constant pushes and pronounced flutterings inside her belly. Her son was restless, for some reason not calming as she read. Normally, when she was reading out loud to the class, he would still, calm down, a few movements, but nothing like what was happening right now. He was aiming for her ribs, luckily not her bladder, but it was more than uncomfortable. She walked the length in front of the room, rubbing her stomach as she softened her voice, needing the troublemaker inside her to calm down. Twenty-four weeks pregnant and maybe Rio was right, maybe she should have taken the semester off too.

 

This island’s mine by Sycorax, my mother, 

Which thou tak’st from me. When thou cam’st first, 

Thou strok’st me and made much of me, wouldst give me 

Water with berries in ’t, and teach me how– (1.2. 396-400).

 

Agatha stopped mid-line, something nudging against her palm– quick, fleeting. Just the slightest press, barely there. She swallowed, clearing her throat. It didn’t happen again, so she kept going, her voice steady as it filled the room.

 

To name the bigger light and how the less, 

That burn by day and night. And then I loved thee, 

And showed thee all the qualities o’ th’ isle, 

The fresh springs, brine pits, barren place and fertile. 

Cursed be I that did so!... (1.2. 401-405).

 

Another nudge. 

Stronger this time. 

Agatha froze. 

Their son– he just kicked. Right in the middle of a damn lecture. And this time, she felt it against her palm. A second nudge nearly made her drop the massive book in her hand. She cleared her throat again, fingers gripping the cover tighter, and forced herself to move back toward the podium. Of course he decided to do this now. Not when she was home. Not when Rio spent hours rubbing her stomach, whispering in Spanish, practically begging him to move. Not when Rio had fallen asleep with her hand resting over where he grew, waiting– hoping– pleading for a single little kick. Not when he kept her up at night, rolling and stretching like he had a whole damn playground in there. No. He picked now. Right in the middle of class. In front of an entire hall of nosy college students. While she was reading Shakespeare.

Definitely Rio’s kid, she thought fondly, rubbing her belly more as silence filled the hall. 

She didn’t notice, no, she was somewhere else, feeling him press against the palm of her hand, announcing his presence. She tried moving her hand in circles, hoping he would settle, but he only responded with another kick, as if reveling in his ability to throw her off balance. She almost chuckled, but she stifled it with a soft hum, realizing she desperately needed to finish the reading. The students were staring at her now, expectant– but mostly confused, but for some reason, she couldn’t pull herself out of her shock. 

“Dr. Harkness?” a voice finally cut through to her. 

She snapped her head up, looking up at William. 

“Are you alright?” 

She nodded her head, “Yes, go ahead and finish the passage and then whenever you are ready, get into small groups and discuss the relationship between Caliban and Prospero. And then, go back through and highlight passages like this one that underlines the ideas of power and colonization.”

Agatha sat back in a chair she had recently brought in when she taught. Two hours of being continuously on her feet were killing her, and she was only forty-five minutes into this lecture– an hour and fifteen minutes. Okay, she could do this, and then she had to wait another thirty minutes in Rio’s office… so, an hour and forty-five minutes… 

 She flipped absentmindedly through the book, rubbing her stomach as her son continued to press against her palm. Tiny punches landed like she was training the next Muhammad Ali. It wasn’t enough to physically take her breath away, more the shock factor. Every twitch she could feel now, and fuck, this was definitely going to be a long three more months. Another push against her palm made her freeze, staring down at her stomach expectedly.

Easy, love. I hear you. You just have to wait to surprise Mami, she thought to him, in some way hoping he could understand her and would settle. 

He didn’t. He just turned in her stomach, maybe a hand pushing against her this time. She inhaled sharply, mostly out of annoyance of not being able to pull herself together as the minutes ticked by. She rubbed her stomach more firmly, needing him to settle, to still– go to sleep– but he simply jolted? Maybe it was a sneeze? A hiccup? She read somewhere that she would start being able to tell more of his movements now, but he was so restless, as if he constantly needed to move… like his mami . She would have to talk to Abuelita and see if she knew what Rio was like in the womb. She felt her eyebrows pinch together as he continued. She tried schooling it back, but she could not, especially now that she felt more eyes on her in the silence. 

The students noticed. They weren’t saying anything– oh, of course not. They got their freebee on the first day. Despite her current state, she was pleased to know that the students found her somehow almost scarier, but now? The air had shifted, thick with uncertainty. Their gazes darted towards her and away again, like nervous birds. 

She turned a page in her book without reading a single word. A few students exchanged wary glances. One of them– Cassandra, or maybe Carolina– tilted her head slightly, as if on the verge of speaking, but thought better of it. Smart girl. 

Another nudge made Agatha jump in surprise, her head snapping down to her stomach again, Not now, love, please. Mama needs to get through this.

Silence stretched in the room, awkward and expectant. The students weren’t discussing. Weren’t flipping through their books. They were watching. Agatha exhaled slowly, trying to regain some semblance of control. 

“Start the discussion,” she instructed, her voice thinner than she would’ve liked. “I don’t want to hear silence.” 

Chairs scraped against the floor as the students shuffled into small groups, the forced hum of conversation rising, but Agatha barely registered it. Her son wasn’t letting up. He kept rolling, pressing, twitching, maybe hiccuping. It wasn’t just movement– it was urgency. She placed both hands on her stomach this time, as if they alone could decipher the message. Was he just restless? Or– she swallowed hard, tears pooling in her eyes in a sudden wave– was something wrong? 

 

***

 

Students

 

Eva and Ireland exchanged glances as class came to an end. It was clear in their minds– there was something wrong with their professor. Dr. Harkness’ hands were constantly on her stomach during the period and for once, she wasn’t engaging– wasn’t going on with her elaborate lectures or even the normal condescending remarks on the characters. Instead, she looked human. Eva looked up, finding the professor leaning on the podium, her gaze down and brow furrowed. She shifted in her seat, a mix of concern and confusion spreading through her body as Harkness rubbed her stomach again, as if soothing the baby. Perhaps the baby was kicking. Eva couldn’t help but wonder what that was like, to have tiny clumps of cells slowly form into a tiny human and then carry that human with your organs rearranging. She shivered. 

She glanced at Ireland again, “You think she is okay? I have never seen her so human .” 

Ireland chuckled, looking up at the professor, “The baby must move a lot. I’m sure she is fine. My sister’s baby constantly pressed against her ribs.” 

Eva nodded slowly as Harkness straightened up, her eyes glancing around the hall before taking a slow measured breath, before she spoke softer than any of them had heard, “Go ahead and start looking into Ariel. Come in with highlighted evidence for themes of freedom between him and Caliban. I will see you all Friday. Class dismissed.”

Eva and Ireland packed up, collecting their pens and binders as most of the students left, leaving just them, a few stragglers, and Harkness. 

“You want to head to the lounge in the science hall until Vidal’s?” Ireland asked as she threw her coat on. 

Eva nodded, grabbing her bag as they headed out into the hall. They both froze for a moment, noticing Harkness in front of them, weaving through the crowd of students with surprising speed for a pregnant woman. They remained quiet as they stepped out into the sun, the snow gone from the side walks, the sun melting what was left in the grass. Eva smiled, soaking in the rays as they crossed into the center of the quad, still behind Harkness. 

“You think she is going to wait in her wife’s office?” Ireland whispered. 

Eva nodded, “No other reason to be going to the science department.”

They tried keeping their distance, feeling awkward to walk too close to the professor, but then Harkness froze, stopping all at once, before trying to take a step back. The two glanced at each other, but then the air changed, something cold set and for a moment, it was like the clouds covered the sun. Ireland tensed beside her, and when she followed Harkness’ frozen gaze, she understood. 

A woman was approaching quickly, her strides swift and furious. She carried herself with a cruel smirk twisting her lips and the air was shifting into something traitorously cold. She moved fast for an old woman, lacking the usual calm, cautious steps, but instead, her presence was commanding– not in the calm, cold collected way the Dean was nor the steady confidence of Dr. Harkness. No, this woman held all the poise of a viper, carrying a poison rooted deep within her. 

Eva really looked at the woman, noting the ice blue eyes– they weren’t as sharp nor as bright, but the cheekbones and long hair was undeniable. They had to be related. Ireland and Eva moved around them, sensing to stay back, but something made Ireland freeze a couple yards away. Dr. Harkness didn’t notice her though, didn’t tell her stop gawking or ask if she was practicing catching flies with her mouth, instead; Dr. Harkness was eyeing the woman, her gaze hardening into something equally cruel as her lip sneered, but the hands that were at her side were now protectively on her belly. 

“Come on–” Eva tried to pull her away but Ireland stood her ground just as the woman stopped inches away. 

“Agatha,” the woman cooed, her voice laced with saccharine venom. “This has gone on long enough.”

Dr. Harkness rolled her eyes, “Mother, didn’t realize you grew a pair of balls– breaking the restraining order and such.” 

Harkness flicked her fingers at her like she was nothing, but Ireland felt her heart pounding in her chest, Mother? Restraining order? 

She looked quickly to Eva, whispering, “That’s her mom?” 

Eva nodded her head slowly, nearly stunned to stone, “And she broke a restraining order.” 

Their gazes were locked on the scene, on Dr. Harkness, an unshakeable professor whose eyes were now flicking around, as her fingers twitched anxiously on her stomach. Harkness pursed her lips, feigning boredom, but it may as well have been spelled in bold, Times New Roman, eighteen– she was shaken . 

The woman, Harkness’ mother, took a step forward, pinning her daughter in place. Her voice dipped lower, but not low enough as a deep ichor reeked from the crone’s mouth itself, shrivelling Ireland’s and Eva’s ears. 

“You thought you could keep this from me?” a crooked finger pointed down to her stomach. “Carrying that woman’s child. Carrying something so unnatural ?” 

Evanora’s voice dripped with malice, her lip curling in repugnance as if someone had presented her a freshly ripped out esophagus instead of looking at her pregnant daughter. Ireland felt a bile rise to her throat as Eva’s fists clenched, and yet they couldn’t pull away, their eyes locked on the scene before them. The acrimony exuded ran deep, cutting into flesh, making everyone within the quad look up, their faces paling as the students realized who the woman was facing off with. 

Harkness, however, didn’t move, didn’t flinch. If anything, the sneer on her lips deepened. 

“Oh, I’m sorry,” she drawled, her voice cutting like glass. “Did my life somehow inconvenience you? I’d ask how you even found out, but I assume you’ve been hacking through my wife’s socials like the deranged hag you are.” 

Evanora’s eyes flashed dangerously, “You disgust me.” 

The professor just sighed, adjusting her stance ever so slightly– protectively, instinctively, her hands cradling the swell of her stomach, “Well, the feeling’s mutual.” 

Ireland barely breathed just as Eva tugged on her sleeve. They didn’t need words as they sprinted to the science building. Their hearts were thudding against their ribs, the need, the urgency– necessity to find her. They sprinted straight to the office, finding a graduate student sorting through papers annoyed at the desk. 

“Dr. Vidal?” Ireland breathed out urgency. 

“In a lecture. She will done soon–”
“Where?” Eva demanded, finally getting the man to look at them. 

He raised his head in defiance, “ I am not getting trouble for whatever you two idiots want to start with the dean. The semester just started and I don’t need Dean Vidal breathing down my neck after what you said to her.” 

Ireland felt her own lip curl, snapping as she stepped forward with a force that made the graduate student falter, “Her wife is in trouble– in the quad, right now, with a woman who broke a restraining order.” 

The man blinked, his lips parting– somehow still holding the fucking adacity– but Eva leaned in, sharpening her voice that ran chills through her best friend’s body, “ Where is Dr. Vidal?”

He swallowed hard, “257.” 

The pair bolted, ignoring the burning in their legs as they climbed the first flight of stairs to the second floor. They didn’t stop, their feet slapping loudly against the polished floor as they reached the end of the hall and to four sets of large brown heavy doors. Ireland was ahead, bursting into the doors, the sound ringing like a gunshot through the silence as the door slammed off the wall. 

Every student turned simultaneously, their eyes wide until collectively, hesitantly, turning back to Dr. Vidal. A few students shrunk in their seats, while others gave them looks of pity. When Ireland finally turned towards the professor, her mouth opened– it snapped shut. Dr. Vidal was in the front of the hall, poised at the podium, mid-sentence– yet now her head snapped up, cocking to the side as dark brown eyes grew black with anger. Ireland’s stomach dropped, the realization slamming into her like a freight train. She just interrupted Dr. Vidal’s lecture… a woman who already hated her… a woman who thrived on natural order without interruptions– a woman who was now looking at her like she had announced she murdered her wife to her face.  

“And what ,” she said, her voice dragging, “is the meaning of this?” 

Her voice was so low– so low, like the first rumble of Smaug before he unleashed his fury upon the town of Dale. Ireland flinched, preparing for the flames, but Eva– her dear Bard the Bowman, stood fast. 

“It’s your wife.” 

And in a flash, Smaug no longer stood before them, but rather the protective, fierce, and loyal Meleys of a completely different realm, “What?” 

“Your wife,” Ireland repeated, her voice rushing out in a single breath. “She’s in the quad– with her mother.” 

Vidal moved, and within two blinks the lecture hall doors were slamming behind her, leaving the students in utter confusion. Eva and Ireland ran after her, jogging to even see her running figure ahead. They both pushed, watching their usual calm, collected professor sprint down the stairs– faster than they would give her credit for. They could barely keep up, losing sight of her as the three of them were skipping the stairs as they spun down them until Vidal tore through glass doors, running into the quad. 

The girls didn’t think, they just followed watching in awe as Vidal only sprinted faster the moment she caught sight of her wife, having a full blown argument in the center of the quad. Students backed away from them, peeking out from corners, but refusing to make themselves known, nor step in and help as Ireland the Eva grew closer, just to hear Harkness’ mother–

“I ought to have killed you the moment you left my body, and I won’t allow another mistake like that to happen again.” 

Something blinded Eva for a moment, a reflection– a ray from the sun hitting off something metal– something silver… and for a moment, the world froze. Eva’s eyes widened, her strides weak as she just reacted, unsure how she was going to get there faster, but Eva wasn’t needed. Vidal pushed through, a blur of black and green, cutting through the weight and severity– and she lunged.

When the pair hit the ground,there was a sickening crack, bones meeting ground, ribs bending and taking the force and weight. A scream tore through the air– someone– Eva couldn’t tell who– but then someone else gasped sharply. Sirens echoed in the distance, but they weren’t close enough– not with Harkness frozen. Her hands hovered uselessly over her stomach, eyes locked onto the tangled bodies before her. 

There was a flash of silver and this time, the girls saw it clearly– a simple kitchen knife, meant to cut meat, was glimmering in the sunlight, shining in all of its glory in Evanora’s raised hand. It seemed to smile– a wicked smile as Evanora pulled her hand back, winding it back with her eyes locked on her daughter. 

Vidal moved. 

Not away. 

Into it. 

A hand reached out, grasping the older woman’s but in a flash, an agonized grunt echoed across the quad, over the sirens, and hitting the students. Before the old woman could move again Vidal somehow had the knife throwing it far away before her elbow slammed down onto the chest of the woman who dared to move again. This time, the crack was deeper, a crunch that rattled the students’ very bones as mouths fell open, others lurched, and few had actually stepped forward, in shock and admiration as the woman screamed out, clutching where Vidal had struck. 

“Stay the fuck away from my wife, ” the Dean spat in the old woman’s face, before backing off of her, her ass falling on the sidewalk as her balance was lost.

That’s when Ireland caught it, her gasp breaking the silence. A dark purple– a maroon stain was growing on Vidal’s green blouse, her body trembling from adrenaline. The dean looked down, her hand pressing against it as she winced before turning the palm to face her. Red stared back at her, greeting warmly as more students and professors filled the quad. 

Police were running, an EMT team on their heels, but Ireland and Eva were only paying attention to the Dean’s wife. Dr. Harkness dropped to her knees with the grace of a non-pregnant woman, pulling her wife into her arms. Her hand immediately went to her side, applying pleasure to the wound as Vidal winced. 

“You fucking idiot– what the fuck were you thinking?” Harkness’ eyes were glistening, her cheek rosy as tears kissed them. 

Vidal let out a strained chuckle, “Wasn’t thinking, mi vida.” 

Then her voice grew serious as she somehow managed to sit up, caressing her wife’s cheeks, thumbs brushing the tears away, “Did she hurt you? Did she put her hand–”

“I’m fine– our child is fine,” Harkness leaned into her touch, her eyes closing for a moment as more tears escaped. “But you’re not–”

“It’s just a flesh wound,” Vidal turned her voice to mock a British person, her voice nonchalant with a slight bit of defence. 

“I know, you are not quoting Monty Python to me right now.” 

The Dean laughed, but winced, clinging to her wife just as the EMTs pulled up beside them. Eva turned back to Ireland, finding William just approaching and freezing as two gurneies appeared,the cops on the strange woman who had threatened the Harkness-Vidals.

No one spoke. The student body remained frozen, professors watching with their mouths open as they were able to put Dr .Vidal onto a gurney, another EMT helping Dr. Harkness to her feet. William couldn’t keep his eyes off the pair, watching Vidal reach for her wife, Harkness snapping at another EMT as they were loaded into the truck. 

He swallowed hard, “Did Vidal just jump in front of a blade for Harkness?” 

Everyone nodded their heads slowly as other students began muttering, looking at each to confirm what they had just seen. 

Ryan stood at the other end, his eyes wide as his bag fell off his shoulder, watching the officers take the crazy mother away as she screamed and cursed. But his gaze narrowed to the redhead watching the scene as well. He was in her class when they heard the older woman screaming and all of them, students and Dr. Maximoff came out to see what was going on. Ryan had watched the moment Dr. Harkenss’ mother pulled the knife, her gaze on the professor’s stomach. For a moment, he felt like a spector, watching everyone. He watched as students froze, others taking steps back in fear, and some even ready to move in, getting ready to sprint and stop what was about to happen.

Yet Ryan’s gaze ultimately fell on Dr. Maximoff, the way she froze, her eyebrows creasing in confusion. They were closer. They could have easily made it before any of the other students who were moving, but Dr. Maximoff hesitated. Her body flinched, unable to decide if she wanted to throw herself in or if the fear was too consuming. 

For once… the obsession didn’t win. Dr. Maximoff hesitated too long. The moment she did move, a loud crack had echoed through the crowd, a pair of bodies slamming into the frozen earth. 

Dr. Vidal did not hesitate. 

There was no second guessing, no fear as the woman pulled a blade– no, she threw herself in front of it, blocking the path to her wife and unborn child. Ryan couldn’t help but feel his own heart flutter– their love was real. It was so fucking real– not some ‘kissy kissy’ show or some insane romantic shit you could only find on AO3. He gazed at them again, his heart clenching, fluttering, and pounding all at once– he felt like one of those lesbihahns gnawing at the metal bars of a cage, yearning for that type of love. 

He turned back to Maximoff and she looked defeated, but for once that obsessive adoration wasn’t there, just a quiet understanding that was flashing across her features. Her obsession was strong, but nothing would ever be stronger than the love Dr. Vidal had for her wife. 

 

***

 

Rio

 

Pain scorched through her side as she blinked through the blinding lights of the ambulance. She knew it was serious, but she was right when she told her wife that it was a flesh wound, that nothing major was hit, but it still didn’t stop her from groaning like a little bitch as the EMTs started pumping her full of pain killers, or maybe that was the adrenalin. The only other thing she could focus on was a warm hand in hers, fingers brushing through her hair, caressing her face. The angelic voice was back– the exact same one she had heard when she woke up from anesthesia. Goddess above, she loved that voice. She turned her head towards it, feeling butterfly wings grazing her cheek, making her smile, and allowing herself to slip into darkness. 

Rio wasn’t out for long, she snapped her eyes open hearing her wife’s angry voice, “I am her wife . I have to be with her!” 

“Mrs. Harkness-Vidal, please, we just need a little bit of room. Once we have her stitched up, we will bring you right to her,” a doctor tried to reassure her wife. 

A female voice came in next, a little softer but laced with firmness, “Let’s check on baby, shall we? It’s a lot of stress for both of you, but we can reassure you that your wife will be fine.” 

And then Rio was rolled away, into a room where interns were on her immediately, cleaning and stitching up her wound. She felt dazed, her gaze on the door, watching patients be wheeled past, doctors running, nurses carrying charts, and a few random screams echoing through the hospital. She just nodded as the interns went through her blood work, doing the standard procedures until finally one of them addressed her for more than just the random check up questions. 

“How far along is your wife, Mrs. Harkness-Vidal?” a nurse asked her, standing at the foot of her bed as she signed through the paper to get her checked out. 

Rio smiled, “Twenty-four weeks. She is due in May.”

The nurse smiled warmly, “First little one?” 

“Yeah, and he will be the first great-grandchild.” 

“Ah, so he is extra special,” she nodded, as she set the chart down pulling up a chair beside her bed, but she didn’t sit, she simply leveled her with a look. “Alright, Mrs. Harkness-Vidal– Rio. Let’s talk about healing. The cut wasn’t deep, but it’s not great. Your tattoo isn’t perfect anymore and ribs move with every breath, so there will be discomfort and pain. You have six dissolvable stitches. But, you need to keep it clean and dry for seventy-two hours. No showers until tomorrow. No baths with the wife, no soaking. And you are off from work for the rest of the week. These first three days involve nothing but rest.” 

“And lifting things?” Rio started, already thinking of the laundry she had been doing to stop her wife from walking the stairs. 

She thought of the damn bunny, Señor Scratchy, her wife’s now-familiar– lifting him up from the floor when he or her wife wanted to cuddle. She thought of her garden, the mobile they had just ordered, even cooking– packing their lunches, putting groceries away. 

The nurse gave her a knowing smile, “No lifting anything over ten pounds for two weeks. You can have sex after, but it is preferred to wait after four weeks– don’t give me that look. I can tell you’re a good wife, aside from jumping into a blade– and your wife adores you. I don’t think I ever heard somehow who could make these doctors cower until she opened her mouth. Anyway, no laundry, no grocery bags, if you still have nursery stuff– nothing. It’s your job to heal so you can be ready when your son is born.”

Rio nodded, guilt flashing her features for a moment. She didn’t exactly see the knife when she tackled Evanora. All she knew was that the woman threatened her wife… again, but this time raised her arm. She didn’t even know the knife was there until she felt it across her skin, ruining her favorite blouse. 

The nurse continued, handing her a pamphlet for wound care, prescribing her Tylenol and telling her to watch for swelling or fever. The nurse finally left with the final word that her wife was finishing up fetal monitoring. She had relaxed back until a police officer walked in, his head bowed sheepishly, his hat off. Rio raised an eyebrow, trying to hide her panic. Fuck, she hated talking to the police and for a moment she wondered if all the times she got lucky when she started bar fights was about to catch up with her. 

Before the officer could open his mouth, she spoke coldly, “Do I need my lawyer present, officer?” 

He shook his head, “No, ma’am. No charges are being leveled against you.”

“And Evanora?” Rio’s gaze hardened, her jaw clenched as her mind went back to the shine of the blade, the rancor in Evanora’s gaze– the apathetic look in her eyes as she savored her wife’s fear. 

The officer took a breath, fidgeting where he stood, “We did not realize the extent of her injuries. We had taken her straight to the station for violating the restraining order and attacking your wife and unborn child. When you slammed into her, you cracked a few false ribs on impact. What we assume from the struggle on the ground and disarming her, you shattered her sternum along with the fourth, fifth, and sixth ribs. There was internal bleeding.” 

“And?” Rio arched an eyebrow.

“Evanora Harkness is dead.”



***

 

Rio winced as Lilia and Alice led them into their own home, carrying their bags. Lilia already had made food for the week, refusing to take no for an answer and giving it to them. Alice had come with their bags and laptops from their offices, helping them take it inside. 

Rio went straight for the couch, slowly easing herself down and closing her eyes. She hated this already, guilt consuming her being as she barely registered her wife and their friends talking in the kitchen. Rio wasn’t supposed to be the one resting. She was supposed to be the one taking care of her wife, easing her stress, her pains, and keeping her wife and unborn son healthy. She wasn’t supposed to be the patient.

Before she could move to get up, the couch dipped beside her, a hand caressing her cheek. She didn’t bother opening her eyes, leaning into the caress as if the touch could soothe the tidal of thoughts into gentle lapping waves. 

“My love,” came the voice, velvet-dark and perfectly sweet. 

The hand slid into her hair, brushing away the short pieces that framed her face and for a moment it was silent, her wife just studying her before lips were placed at the corner of her mouth. 

“You’re not allowed to feel guilty,” her wife whispered, voice warm and welcoming as the first fire of Yule. “We wanted her dead for a long–”

“It’s not that,” she interrupted quietly. “Trust me, you know how much I have fantasized killing her. But, I should have seen the knife, and avoided it. I am supposed to take care of you– not the other way around.” 

Her wife only gave her that look, the one that said ‘you are speaking such bullshit right now, but I love you. So I will listen and I will judge.’

So she continued, “I am supposed to protect you– make you comfortable as you carry our son. That was the plan.”
Agatha rolled her eyes fondly, “You should know by now that life doesn’t conform to your plans. If it did, we wouldn’t be married, love. But you realize that you always protect me and today, my love, you protected not only me, but our son too.” 

Rio sighed, the image of the blade being raised to throw at her wife flashing through her mind. She felt tears prick at the corner of her eyes as she took a deep shuddering breath only to wince from it. Her wife was there though, wiping her tears and holding her steady. Lips pressed to her cheeks continuously before she turned, capturing them with a simple tenderness between them. 

A throat cleared, breaking them apart, “Lilia and I labelled your food for the first week. Agatha, you will be the one putting them in the microwave. Me or Lilia will be back to do the laundry, neither of you are to touch it. If you need help with the nursery, call us, also when did you guys get a fucking rabbit?” 

Rio laughed at her best friend, before wincing again, muttering a curse. 

“Long story, but she rescued him,” her wife hummed.  

Lilia nodded slowly, before turning sharply to Rio “Just don’t let him on the stitches. You tear those stitches, Vidal, and you won’t be holding your son right away. I’ll handle the next two days at the university and Jen will step in for your classes. Monday, when you return, you will have an assistant to run and make copies and what not. We will do damage control of the narrative.” 

Rio nodded, “Thanks, Lilia.” 

“When you are all healed, we are celebrating that cunt’s death,” Alice added, grabbing her coat. “To the Mother, I was so happy when you texted she was dead.” 

Agatha sat up, a hand on her stomach, “We will celebrate once this one makes it through not tearing her stitches.”

She rolled her eyes at that, but felt herself fall further into the cushions, her eyelids heavy. She couldn’t stop the guilt from capturing her, and no, it was not over her mother-in-law’s death– part of it was regret, not being able to enjoy the moment she snuffed out the worthless life and not realizing how hard her elbow had landed. If she could go back, she would enjoy killing the cunt. But for now, she took the win that she was dead, the cops trying to sweep it under the rug that they messed up by not providing medical care immediately, and really, her and her wife couldn’t give two fucking shits. All she knew was that Evanora was dead. She couldn’t hurt her wife anymore and she would never get the chance to hurt their son. Their son would never know the cruelty from Evanora, nor the cruelty from her own sister. 

A hand cupping her cheek made her jump awake, not realizing she drifted off into her own world, “Do you want lunch before you take a nap, my love?” 

Rio shook her head, refusing to open her eyes as lips pressed to the spot in between her eyes, “Bed or couch?” 

“Couch, I don’t wanna get up,” she replied groggily. 

Her wife helped her slowly turn, laying her back on the couch before propping her neck up better and pulling the blanket down around her. She smiled as fingers ran through her hair, quickly soothing her. 

“Before you fall asleep, my love. I need to show you something.” 

Rio’s eyes snapped open at that, staring up into her wife’s eyes. They were shining down at her with restrained excitement, her gaze flashing towards the wound before her wife took her hand, bringing it up to her lips, kissing it softly. 

“When I was in the quad, I was actually going to wait for you in your office,” she smiled softly before lowering her hand to the large curve of her stomach. “Someone wants to meet you, my love.”

 

 

 

Chapter End Notes

so cameos : House of the Dragon, The Hobbit, Monty Python and the Holy Grail, Taylor Swift (Carolina and Cassandra) Amy Poelher, and guess what-- you, my dear lesbihahns. (lmk if I missed anything, currently only running on two cups of coffee, three hours of sleep, and trying to be discrete while I post this at this café.)

ANYWAY

Hope you enjoyed this, hopefully you are happy with what happened to Evanora

Chapter 23: I Do Not Do This For Myself ~ Hymn to Virgil Part 1

Chapter Summary

Señor Scratchy VS Rio Harkness-Vidal

Rio cries... a lot....

well... i fell like the song itself explains what happens

have tissues... you'll know when to pull them out.

Rioallalong_719 💚💜

See the end of the chapter for more notes

 

I f I held in my hands everything gold could buy

I’d still not have a thing worth giving you

You tell me the sun is shining in paradise

And I have to watch your lips turn blue

 

Hymn to Virgil ~ Hozier

 

Rio 

 

“Someone wants to meet you, my love.” 

 

Her breath caught as Agatha laid a gentle hand on the curve of her belly. Then came the soft press of her wife’s fingers atop her own, guiding, grounding. The silence was like a slow fog creeping over a valley. Rio didn’t dare breathe, too scared her breath would contrast with the life around her. Her eyes were wide, her body aching to rise– to pull her wife close. But she couldn’t risk the stitches, not when she needed to be healed and whole to hold their son when he was born. 

They waited. 

She swore the sweeper hand was morphing into the minute hand and then into the hour hand, everything practically screaming with impatience. 

Her brow furrowed, the confusion creasing her eyebrows painfully together. Her wife slid her hand, coaxing her to press lightly into her skin and another circle. Then–

She felt it. 

The faintest flutter beneath skin, a ripple of life, uncanny in its quiet power. 

Rio gasped and tried to sit up, but Agatha’s hand held her steady, firm against her chest. She froze. And then– another nudge, delicate and defiant– brushed against her palm. Tears streamed freely before she even knew she was crying. Her hand moved instinctively, searching, cradling, as her son danced against her wife’s skin– kicking, reaching, maybe trying to meet her hand from within– to press their palms together. Her heart thundered in her chest. The world narrowed to the sacred rhythm of movement and stillness, mother and son.

Nothing else existed. Not the stitches, pulling with every breath. Not the rising tide of pain as the medicine ebbed. Not the death of her mother-in-law. Not the life she took with her own hands–

Oh fucking well. She’d do it again . 

And again 

And again– as many times possibly. 

Without flinching. 

Bitch had it coming.

There was no other feeling that could compare, even when she heard his heartbeat for the first time. Goddess, it was like when she first realized she fell in love with her wife, when she first kissed Agatha as her wife, the time Agatha showed her the positive pregnancy test– all in one but somehow not more profound. They were all such important, emotionally overwhelming moments, that she couldn’t possibly explain. There were no words in English or Spanish to put the name to how she felt– to how her heart was fluttering– to how happiness was consuming her– to how much she loved this woman who was carrying their child. 

She felt herself start to tremble, her hand rubbing soothing circles as he continued to show off his strength. She swallowed hard, allowing the tears to slip from her eyes as the weight of everything crashed into her. Her son was alive, kicking her, greeting her– and…

 

she could have lost both of them today. 

 

Rio couldn’t stop the sob that bubbled up in her throat, tears pouring down her face. Evanora could have taken everything away from her in one swipe if it wasn’t for Eva and Ireland. Evanora could have killed her wife and her son if it wasn’t for her getting there in time– images of her wife bleeding out– stab wounds in her stomach like that one lady during the Red Wedding flashed through her mind. She felt like Caitlin Stark in that moment in her mind, on her knees begging Evanora to let them go– 

Rio blinked out of the image, her free hand covering her face as the sobs pulled at her stitches, making her cry more. She winced, groaning, and choking on them as the sobs came heavy. Her wife was there though, leaning over her quickly, one hand rubbing circles on her chest over her heart, the other interlocking their fingers. 

“Hey, hey, easy, my love. Gentle breaths,” her wife soothed. 

Rio refused to remove her hand that covered her face, trying to ease her breathing as her wife pulled away for a moment. She didn’t question it when her wife stood, moving to the kitchen before coming back. The sobs hurt, pulling tightly at the stitches with each inhale. The couch dipped again and she only peeked when she heard the squeak of scissors cutting through the fabric of her chemise. She didn’t protest, knowing the blood already ruined it along with the large tear from the knife. Her wife simply cut straight up before cutting at the sleeves, Rio barely moving to help her take the thing off her. The scissors were set on the coffee table with a light ping before a hand was back on her chest, rubbing in slow circles between her breasts, right on her chest plate. 

Rio slowly realised her wife was doing what she usually did to help her wife’s anxiety (when her wife didn’t want a hug or to be held), a grounding technique that stimulated the parasympathetic nervous system. The circles didn’t stop, a rhythmic pattern calming her racing heart, easing and slowing each breath she took. Rio didn’t remove her hand from her face as her breathing regulated, but then something warm and wet dropped on top of her hand, slowly rolling down her wrist. 

She removed her hand then, looking up into tear-filled eyes and the sight broke her. She tried sitting up again, needing to pull her wife into her embrace– everything in her screaming to take care of her– to be the strong one of the two. But the hand on her chest, forced her down again, forcing her still as her wife raised an eyebrow. 

“ Stop fucking moving , Rio Atropos Harkness-Vidal ,” her wife seethed through her teeth, tears still streaming.

Rio froze, her eyes widening at the mention of her middle name. For whatever reason, her parents had decided to name her and her sisters’ middle names after the Moirai. Maria Klotho Vidal, Oriana Lachesis Vidal, and finally– in the cruel hand of irony– she was named after the eldest Fate and after the one who cut the thread of life… damn, she really was living up to that name after killing someone today.

When her wife would yell at her, it was always Rio Harkness-Vidal– and sure it was serious, but when Atropos– The Cutter of Threads– was used… it was only when Agatha was serious, furious, or trembling on the edge of losing herself to fear.

 “To the mother, you’re going to fucking tear your stitches not even after a half-day of fucking having them.” 

Her own tears betrayed her next. They slipped down in silence, caught in the hollow curve of her cheekbones, kissed the corners of her lips. Agatha– her Agatha– was radiant even when furious, especially when furious. The magic in her eyes wasn’t the kind that cracked open worlds. It was the kind that stitched broken things together. 

“I’m fine,” Rio whispered, even though they both knew it wasn’t true. 

“No, you’re not,” Agatha snapped, voice hoarse, the hand on her chest trembling while the others that threaded their fingers together gripped tighter. 

Rio blinked, and the ceiling above them blurred. Agatha sniffled, dragging the back of her hand across her face. 

“You– you ran into someone wielding a knife– my mother holding a knife,” she choked. “You started bleeding out in the middle of the quad and now, you’re acting like you still have to be the unbreakable one.” 

“I am the unbreakable one,” Rio replied softly, voice sanded down by emotion. 

“Then why,” Agatha murmured, voice cracking, “did I feel the world start to come apart the moment the blood started to form a puddle on the sidewalk– when they separated me from you and told me if they weren’t there sooner– you would still be in the hospital from how much blood you lost.” 

Silence.

Rio’s hand moved slowly, caressing the side of her stomach feeling her son press against her, anchoring her. Before she could respond, her wife let out a heavy sigh. 

“You don’t always have to be my protector, my love,” her wife whispered. “You gave me a son, you almost gave me your life– it’s my turn to protect you.” 

 Rio stared at the love of her life– feeling herself falling in love all over again. Her heart thudded in her chest, fluttering at her words and chanting its own mantra : 

 

She’s mine. She’s mine. She’s mine. She’s mine. 

(Lub-dub.) (Lub-dub.) ( Lub-dub .) ( Lub-dub .) 

 

Rio nodded, just once. A surrender and promise mixed all into one. Agatha exhaled shakily, her hand moving to where her heart was beating underneath, pausing for a moment, as if her heart’s mantra was the only thing keeping her wife together. 

“Still beating,” her voice was barely a croaked whisper. 

“And black,” Rio remembered the story as old as time between them, back to their college days when they were arguing over a piece of literature and Agatha had said her heart was black. “For you. Always for you.” 




Rio must have fallen asleep, maybe pulled under by the way her wife continued to rub her chest, easing her breathing or maybe when Agatha shoved her fingers down her throat to make sure she took her pain killers and basically waterboarded her. Rio hated pills, medicine, anything to deal with some science grade medicine– she may be a science professor, but her specialty was plants– the natural elixirs they produced– not whatever buck-fuck tiny ass pill that was through how many chemicals in a lab instead of something hand growned, picked, and ground up. 

Okay, so it was coming back to her. 

 

Her wife had gotten up in the pretenses of having to use the bathroom. Rio had closed her eyes, trying to act like every twitch wasn’t killing her. Then a hand was on her jaw, forcing it open before she could even process it. Fingers were shoved down her throat, causing her to gag, her stitches pulling slightly before a pill was placed in her mouth and a water bottle hovered her. Rio went to fight, to turn, to try to choke the pill back up, but the pain weakened her and so did the water that was now filling her mouth. 

She swallowed hard the moment the fingers left her mouth, her eyes watering as her wife stared down at her with a smug smirk. She had merely shrugged, not a word needed to know that Rio would not have taken them if it wasn’t shoved down her throat. 

“Goddess, take a girl out before shoving your fingers down her throat.”

Her wife had only scoffed, patting her cheek condescendingly, “Don’t act like you didn’t like it.” 

And Rio was out.

 

She groaned as she managed to sit up, blinking as she took in her surroundings. The house was plunged in darkness, the only light glowing from the kitchen. She could barely see what was in front of her as her hand reached for the table searching for a phone to check the time. She quickly found one, her eyes shutting as the screen scorched her face. Blinking through tears, she turned down the brightness and found her wife’s phone in her hand. She raised an eyebrow at her wife’s background. It was the selfie Rio had begged her to take with her– the one that Agatha had grumbled and refused until Rio was honest to goddess whining. It was the one lazy evening where they were cuddling in the window seat, Agatha reading to her and their son– well, really for her as it was Hekate: Her Sacred Fires by Sorita d’Este. Not exactly a book read to a baby. 

Rio wanted a picture of them together with Agatha’s baby bump and well, Rio wasn’t going to get it without a fight– but she won. Staring down at the lockscreen her heart soared, staring at their fingers interlocked on Agatha’s stomach, her back against Rio’s front, but their bodies turned so their lips could perfectly lock. Her wife told her that she didn’t care to have it in her phone, but seeing it now, she knew her wife went on her phone when she wasn’t looking and sent it to herself. 

She shook out of her heart eyes, staring to find it was only six in the evening, enough time to plan and cook dinner. Rio stood, struggling slightly before turning on the lamp next to her. She froze. There, on the other side of the couch, was her wife, pillows tucked behind her back, under her belly, and between her knees as she slept– the almost finished baby blanket slowly falling to the floor, and something white and fluffy sitting on her chest. Rio shook her head, tonguing at her cheek to stop the smile as she turned on the lamp next to Agatha. 

Her wife didn’t even stir, the gentle rise and fall of her breathing moving the bunny with her. She reached down, her fingers grazing the white and light brown fur when it moved, faster than she could react. The nip was sharp, fleeting– and unfortunately, expected. Rio barely flinched back, watching Scratchy’s nose twitch at her as he was ready to thump at her, but pausing as if realizing he was still on her wife. Rio reached forward again, her hand pulling back in time as the bunny’s mouth opened. She was starting to regret ever introducing her wife to Scratchy. Rio tilted her head, trying her best to intimidate the rodent, but it merely twitched its ears before settling closer into her wife. 

Rio let out a small groan of frustration, before hissing at the thing, her teeth bared at him like a predator. Señor Scratchy flinched. A squeak left his mouth as he fell off her wife, behind her into the space between her and the couch. 

That woke her wife.  

Bright blue eyes snapped open, Agatha blinking up at Rio before her hand searched for the missing bunny on her chest. Then, Scratchy must have pressed against her back, because her wife huffed, rolling her eyes.

“What did you do to him?” 

Rio gasped, betrayal moving across her features, “What did I do to him? He bit me. ”

“He’s a baby, my love. So you’re telling me you’re going to bite our son when he bites you?”

“The fuck you mean ‘when,’ our son wil be an angel unlike that demon,” Rio huffed back. 

Agatha chuckled low and amused, “Babies teethe, love. They drool, slobber, pull at your hair and jewelry, and I have no doubt, as your son, he will bite.” 

“Why is he my son when he bothers you or you think he will do something, but our son when he is fine?”

Her wife just smirked at her, refusing to reply as she sat up, slowly, slapping Rio’s hands as she moved to help her, “You’re supposed to be resting.”

“So are you,” she fired back. 

Agatha rolled her eyes but softened as the damn rodent jumped into her lap, pressing up against her stomach and purring lightly. It was Rio’s turn to roll her eyes as she stepped forward, going to lean down and kiss her lips, when a sharp pull stopped her, making her stand up straight. She turned away, knowing her wife was looking at her worried. 

“I’ll order Chinese for tonight,” she muttered, grabbing her wife’s phone. 

 

***



Rio knew healing wasn’t going to be easy, but she didn’t think it was be this fucking annoying. They ordered takeout that night and instead of falling into their normal position on the couch watching a movie– Agatha lying completely on top of Rio– Agatha moved their coffee table and triggered the footrests from the couch. They sat like that side by side, and worse, the fucking bunny had a problem with Rio running her hand on her wife’s stomach. He would push her away, nip at her when neither of them were looking, and kick his leg at her when Agatha’s attention was fully on the movie. Little shit . 

 

Sleeping was somehow worse. Her wife refused to cuddle her, not wanting to hurt her and Rio couldn’t sleep on her side as well. Even when she wanted to pull her wife close, her wife was fast asleep, cocooned in the pregnancy pillow Rio had secretly bought a week ago– just in case. And now, she wished she hadn’t– so her wife would move in her sleep and cuddle her instead of acting like she was this fragile thing. But no, her wife was clinging to the U-shaped pillow, her face buried underneath the mess of hair, and snoring… loudly. 

Her wife never snored when she laid on Rio… well, she told herself that, but that was because she was usually asleep before her. Rio had just laid there, staring up at the ceiling, listening to Scratchy, pawing at the bed for her to lift him up. She ignored him. If she couldn’t cuddle her wife, then he wasn’t getting the attention either. 

 

Thursday consisted of her and her wife helping each other dress in lazy clothes. They moved slowly that day, Rio hating to admit, but she did want nothing more than to lie down– most specifically with her wife. She got part of her wish. They had gone to the couch, Rio’s head in her wife’s lap as she happily felt their son push against her hand. For those hours of sitting there while her wife read, Rio would like to say she was playing with her son, moving her hands on either side of her stomach, waiting to feel the kicks against her palm. To her surprise, her wife didn’t shrug her off, just allowed her to feel him until the movements slowed down and she guessed he fell asleep. She had kissed Agatha’s stomach, lying her head back down and closing her eyes. 

She hummed, her breathing slow and she willed the pain to eb away and wished for sleep. Yeah, Rio apparently was asking for too much, because a soft chewing noise was filling her hearing. She felt herself twitch, sleep leaving her as annoyance filled her very being and when she turned her head to look off the couch, black beady eyes were staring into her soul as he chewed and licked at her hair. She snatched it back, finding it a matted mess as her wife chuckled above her. 

“He was just grooming you, love,” her wife hummed, her own fingers running through Rio’s hair. 

Rio shook her head, “No, he was being annoying while I was trying to sleep.” 

Agatha laughed softly, but moved Scratchy away from her, allowing her to drift off. 

 

Unfortunately, that wasn’t the last of Scratchy’s attempts, and by attempts– she actually thought he was trying to get her killed by her own wife. She swore the bunny fucking imprinted  on her wife. He was so in tune with her, always by her side, always nuzzling into her stomach– she hoped, if their son really was her son… when he got the strength, that he would kick that stupid ass bunny someday soon. She didn’t understand it. He was constantly getting on her nerves, eating holes into her socks, hopping in front of her to trip her… Goddess, Rio actually thought her life was about to be ended the first few times it happened around her wife. 

 

Rio stood up slowly, her wife placing a hand on her arm to help balance her as she groaned. It was later in the day, her phone was already blowing with emails from work– the student newspaper, Pepper Stark, their friends, fuck– even Dr. Carvallo and Dr. Dylan was reaching out, but that was the least of her worries. Apparently, killing one of the world’s richest CEOs meant news outlets were on campus, they were filling both her and Agatha’s emails. At one point during the afternoon, someone had even knocked on their door. Rio was tempted to get up, but her wife held her down, both of them going quiet until the knocking stopped. For Agatha, her mother’s COO reached out asking if she would be taking over and without another word, Agatha signed over the company, refusing to even have a relationship with Harkness Inc, ever again. 

The money came next, despite the rancour and abuse her wife received from her mother, Evanora didn’t take her out of the will. Within the twenty-four hour timespan of her death… Well, Rio couldn’t believe the amount of zeros that landed in their bank account. Everything else on the will was on hold, from the houses to so many assets neither Rio nor Agatha bothered to remember– a lawyer stepped in, but agreed to wait until after their son was born and for Agatha to be on her feet. Nice lawyer. 

 

“Do you want anything to eat, mi amor ?” she asked as she slowly made her way to the kitchen, her hand over her side, her feet dragging. 

“No, we will be eating dinner soon. I will get up in a second to throw it in the microwave!” her wife called out. 

Rio stepped into the kitchen, reaching for her glass of water by the sink, before spinning in a circle, looking for her medication. She wasn’t going to have her wife shove her fingers back in her mouth… no matter how Rio did like it. Rio turned towards the center island, her eyes sweeping for the tiny white pill bottle before making her way down the hall towards their room. Just as she passed the living room, a white flash moved in front of her feet. Stumbling, Rio clutched her side, trying to avoid whatever it was until a loud squeak echoed into the stillness of the house. 

“Rio!” 

She flinched, pain spidering up her side as she took a few deep breaths, but she should have kept moving when rushed footsteps made their way towards her. For a moment, she guessed her wife was checking in on her, but to her horror, her wife’s eyes were on the ground where Señor Scratchy laid on his side as if a bus had hit him instead of a light graze from Rio’s foot. 

“What did you do?” her wife reached forward, seeming to forget about the belly on her as she scooped the rabbit up in her arms, checking over his legs and sides. 

Rio blinked slowly, staring at the scene in front of her. Her mouth opened and closed, her eyebrows creasing as the rodent nuzzled into her wife, acting if he was trying to get away from her. The rodent chittered as Agatha soothed him, whispering softly to him. 

“Rio?” her wife snapped her gaze to hers, her blue eyes piercing angrily into her. 

She instinctively took a step back, before gesturing out her hand, “I didn’t do anything– I didn’t even touch him!” 

Her wife tilted her head in the way that told her that she didn’t believe her. 

Rio’s lip curled, “He hopped in front of me, and he’s fine!” 

“He’s a baby, Rio!” 

She groaned, running her hand down her face, “He’s a rodent. I am the one with stitches in my side.” 

Her wife simply raised an eyebrow, but said something more, setting the rabbit down. He quickly hopped back into the living room as Agatha wrapped her arms around her neck. She melted into her wife’s embrace, her belly hitting her first. Rio smirked, a hand coming down to rub over the smooth bump. Her smirk softened as a limb pushed against her, making her wife slightly wince. Soothing over the kick with a careful hand, her lips pressed to her wife’s cheeks. 

“And how’s our baby doing, mi vida ?” she asked softly.

“He’s fine, just super active and doesn’t know how to sit still like his mami ,” her wife quipped.

Rio only raised an eyebrow and before her wife could stop her, her tongue stretched out, licking up slowly against her cheek. The skin was smooth underneath, soft and tasting of lavender, something sweet, and something distinctly her wife. From the force of her tongue, the skin moved up slightly, but her wife didn’t pull away, no. She only sighed in defeat as the spit made her cheek gleam in the low lighting. 

“Fifteen years, and one would think you would grow out of licking me.”

“You knew what you signed up for, mi amor ,” she countered. “And you like me licking you.”

Her wife rolled her eyes, “Yes, my love. Your tongue is very talented. Now, go take your pills before I shove my fingers down your throat again– and don’t even start, Rio!”

 

It had happened two more times, Rio barely catching herself on the final time, bracing herself against the wall. Her wife had only sighed, drawing the bunny into her arms. 

“You two need to get along,” her wife grumbled.

Rio’s eye twitched but she eventually said nothing. 

Instead, the bunny and the Green Witch stared each other down before Rio broke away with a huff. 

 

***

 

It was late when Rio’s phone rang. A loud trill waking them both up from their slumber on the couch. Rio sat up too quickly, her skin burning at the stretch as she was quick to lay back down, holding her side. She watched through blurred tears as her wife sat up, reaching for her phone, a hand absentmindedly on her belly as she moved. Rio blinked away the tears trying to appear fine as her wife answered the phone whilst getting up and walking over to her. A soft hand caressed her jaw, easing the pain softly– a minor distraction, a symbole of comfort. 

 “Hello?” her wife answered the phone, putting it on speaker. 

“Agatha, that you?” Pepper’s voice was shrill, slightly panicked but loud. 

Rio winced, her eyes closing as she breathed heavily through her nose. 

“Yes, yes. My wife is resting.”

Pepper hummed for a moment, seeming to contemplate whatever she was about to say. There was a deep breath in, as if Pepper was standing at the top of a cliff, about to dive into the unknown raging waters below. 

“I hate to cut right to business,” she started slowly. “But I need one of you to come for a short time– I hate to ask either of you, but Agatha–”

Rio’s heart rate spiked, her eyes darkening at the thought of either of them walking onto that campus where the flashing mob of hyenas lurked, ready to pounce with questions, students gawking at both of them, more whispered rumors among their colleagues. Her fists clenched and before her wife could respond, Rio was weakly sitting up. 

“No.”

Agatha narrowed her eyes to her, tilting her head with that unreadable expression.

“Rio, please– I need a statement from one of you. The students have been dodging reporters left and right, more conservative reporters are spinning the truth. I need, preferably your wife, to make a statement.”

“No, absolutely not.”

“Rio–”

“No! You’re six months pregnant and were almost stabbed. And now they’re reaching for a story that isn’t even there– to impeach into our lives when everything we have done is to keep our lives ours ,” she argued back, but her body slumped as her wife laid her back down. 

Her wife only hummed, realizing the roles were reversing. Rio was no longer calmed, collected, in control– no, she was frantic, grasping at the eroding edges, mud pulling into her own despair as she imagined her wife being surrounded by reporters. She could see Agatha panic, freezing as questions fired about her abusive mother– about her wife, about Rio , killing her mother– 

Gods, Rio wanted to slam her head into something. How could she not see what eternal struggle her wife must be having? She killed her mother– Rio killed her mother, and sure, an abusive, homophobic cunt– there had to be something– some type of emotion, and yet, she hadn’t seen anything. Not relief. Not anger. Not happiness. Just concern for Rio. She didn’t know how to handle it– she didn’t know how to handle the fact that fuck , she killed someone.

Would Agatha trust me around our child? What if she thinks that I’m heartless? What if she doesn’t see it as protection– I just wanted to protect. I just wanted to keep her safe. I–

Lips pressed against hers, soft and sweet, ceasing her spiralling thoughts in her tracks. It was slow, petals pressing against her lips before it deepened, the flower blossoming– a teasing tongue moving into her mouth before pulling away all together. 

“Pepper, we will call you back later and give you an answer around eight.”

With that she hung up, turning to cradle her face in her hands. Rio melted, leaning into the safety. She didn’t know why the simple gesture always made her feel like Agatha was wrapping her in eternal protection. Her gaze was soft and understanding, thumbs wiping tears away. 

“You need a nap, my love,” she spoke softly. 

Rio scoffed, “I’ve been sleeping all day.”

Silence filled the room, but it wasn’t unbearable. It was a moment to build up their strength, to breathe in an air of calm before diving into what both of them knew would be an argument. Rio turned her face slightly, kissing the palm of her wife’s hand as she rubbed her stomach, feeling a sharp kick against her palm. 

“You woke him up,” Agatha muttered, but there wasn’t any malice, just a quiet understanding of the comfort it brought Rio. 

“I don’t want you going in tomorrow.”

Agatha chuckled darkly, “Well, you’re still on bed rest, so it has to be me.”

Rio tilted her head at her wife, subtly telling her to drop the bullshit. Agatha sighed, helping her sit up so they could talk. Fingers ran through her hair, trucking the stay pieces away from her eyes. 

“What has you so worried, my love?”

Rio’s mouth opened, her hand back on her wife’s baby bump.

“And don’t say the baby,” she cut her off, a knowing glint flashing in those ocean eyes. 

“Agatha, I killed your mother.”

Her wife tilted her head, chuckling lightly, “Yes, you did. Once you’re all healed up, I plan to thank you properly.”

Her voice dropped lowly, a hand moving up her thigh. Rio sighed, naming it easily : deflection. 

“Agatha.”

Her wife groaned, looking the other way, “Must we do this?”

“You can’t fuck your way out of this one, mi amor ,” she hummed, brining a small chuckle to her wife’s lips. 

Her wife breathed in, her eyes closing as her hand came to the side of her stomach, comforting their son who must be tossing and turning within her. Rio placed a hand along with her, not in an attempt to rush her, but just to let her know she would wait– she would sit for as long as Agatha needed. 

She chewed her cheek nervously when tears started to pool in the corners, eyelids opening to reveal eyes as crystal blue as any glacier, “What does it make me if I am so happy, so relieved that she is dead?”

“And what does it make me if I am the one who killed her?” Rio asked just as quietly. 

A weak smile pulled at the corner of Agatha’s lips, “Lucky.”

“Agatha.”

“A saint.”

Rio tilted her head, shaking it slightly bewildered.

“My hero.”

A beat of silence.

“His hero.”

Rio’s breath caught. Her gaze snapped to her wife–  who couldn’t meet it, who stared instead at the gentle swell beneath her sweatshirt. And just then, as if stirred by the words or by fate itself, a limb pressed outward, distorting the smooth curve of Agatha’s belly. She traced it with her fingers, feather light touch, one that would tickle, watching it flinch away from her touch. A soft, startled laugh escaped her– then stilled. Her eyes drifted into memory, into mourning.

She leaned back against the couch, her head tilting toward the ceiling.

“She had a knife,” she said. “And her eyes were locked on my stomach.” 

Rio’s entire body tensed. Whatever guilt had been stirring in her was gone in an instant, evaporated like fog under flame. She would do it again. A thousand times again. Agatha turned then, cupped her wife’s face with a hand that had always held love and war in equal parts.

“And just like all the times before– long before you even knew– you were there. Saving me. Shielding me. Yes, my mother is dead” a  breath “thank the goddess for that. But you?” Her thumb brushed Rio’s cheek. “You’re no killer, my love. Technically…” she added, with a hint of dry irony, “the police let her die when they didn’t act fast enough.”

Rio’s voice was barely a thread, unraveling, “I still snapped her ribs.”

And then Agatha whispered, her voice a ghost of old bruises and buried screams, “And she shattered so many of mine long before you ever laid a hand on her.”

Rio froze as her wife pulled away from her face, her legs curling underneath herself as her hands went back to their son, soothing him beneath the skin. But Rio couldn’t react. She was shocked, her body rigid as her wife’s words spun continuously around her head, pinging off her meninges and cerebrum. 

Her wife didn’t really talk of her past, but she knew well enough how some days her wife tensed when someone snuck up behind her, when a cabinet was slammed too loudly, or how she froze when people started screaming. She would hide her panic around her brilliance in sarcasm, quick retorts that sounded as if they had been rehearsed but in reality, her wife was always good at insulting someone. 

She knew Evanora hurt her wife, knew she was homophobic, but never realized how deeply her wife’s scars were, and in that moment, her own scar formed, a painful lingering thing– a deep reminder that she couldn’t have done anything to stop it, that she didn’t have the power of cosmos to go back in time and kill Evanora before she even had a chance to hurt her wife. All she could do was be here for her now. 

Rio took a deep breath, knowing not to push. It was rare for her wife to open up about anything in her past and she learned quickly that asking questions drove her further away. Instead, she laced her fingers with her wife’s over their son, unable to pull her in or lean down and kiss her belly. 

“I would burn the entire world for you, mi vida, ” she whispered softly. 

There was a moment of silence between them as she caught sight of a brief smile, but she knew better than to expect a loving or poetic answer in return. Those were the really rare moments, like Rio telling her stories underneath the stars, sometimes moments after making love, or if they got into a fight and were making up. 

This was way too raw, way too close to that certain thread she knew Agatha was too scared to pull all at once, even for her. This was where Agatha’s armour held tight, even when her soul was peeking through. 

So, she braced herself. 

“Gods, Rio,” she shook her head, trying to hide the blush in her cheeks. “You’re so pathetic.” 

 

 



Rio inevitably lost the fight of Agatha going to the school to make a statement. She wasn’t going to teach. Just an hour meeting before giving a statement to get the news reporters away from the university. But that didn’t mean Rio was going to let her go alone. No, she texted the coven separately, telling them to stand by her wife, asking for them to intervene if necessary. Lilia would be the closest as she was the temporary Dean while Rio was out and already taking over her duties for next semester. But it didn’t stop the nerves from eating at her. She didn’t know how many people knew of Evanora’s death, but she was for certain the whole business world did and it scared her to think what ties they had with the cunt. 

So Rio didn’t sleep that night again. She laid on her back, staring up at the ceiling, listening to Scratchy thump at her from the floor and the soft snores from her wife beside her. She got up around four in the morning, not able to stand being so useless. 

She went to their bathroom, cleaning the wound and staring at the stitches. They weren’t kidding, unfortunately saying the blade messed up her tattoo. The scar would be hideous on her tattooed bones, distorting the perfection and forever imprinting the memory of what she had done to protect her unborn son and her wife. She grumbled, quietly heading into the kitchen and grabbing the misting bottle for her plants. She moved around the ivies, refilling the tiny bottle continuously, but she couldn’t lift her watering can for the other plants. She moved to the palm tree in the dining room, knowing she would have to move it to the greenhouse soon. 

When she turned to the monstera by the reading nook, she froze. Soil was everywhere, the leaves shredded but luckily not eaten. Monstera was toxic to bunnies, and she had been nervous to leave Scratchy out of his cage but animals were smart– Señor Scratchy was too smart apparently as her heart cracked as she stared at the exposed mangled roots.
