They were chewed up and spit out. Her beautiful white and green leaves were already browning around the edges. She turned back to the floor, finding bunny sized paw prints leading underneath the dining table in circles as he seemed to kick off the dirt before going to his pen where she knew the bastard was sleeping soundly without a care in the world. 

She would have to wait to run the sweeper, but she didn’t even think she could with the stitches. She didn’t want her wife cleaning this up either as tears pricked at her eyes. The monstera was so expensive, a plant she didn’t think she could have or wisely buy even with her and Agatha’s salaries. Her wife wouldn’t be happy if Rio bought it but everytime they were in a plant store together, Rio would always freeze when passing by a monstera, caressing the large leaves in hopes of one day it would be a sensible purchase. But her wife had been watching and the day she became Dean of faculty, she came home to find the plant sitting on their kitchen counter. 

She froze, caressing the tiny leaves in shock before arms wrapped around her waist, kisses being pressed to the back of her neck. Next thing Rio knew, she was holding onto the counter her other arm around her wife’s neck, relying on Agatha to hold her up as fingers thrusted roughly inside her. 

But that plant meant everything to her. It was the fact that her wife saw her– really saw her. She noticed Rio hesitating about buying the plant and did it herself. And now, dirt ruined their hardwood floors, the plant in pieces scattered around. Gods, as if they didn’t have enough going on. 

Rio moved slowly, allowing the tears to run down her cheeks as she used the arm on the side that wasn’t cut, weakly sweeping the dirt and leaves into a pile with a broom before petting her monstera softly, whispering apologies for bringing the rodent into their sanctuary. 

A thump made her grow stiff. She turned slowly, finding the culprit twitching its nose at her before hopping away down the hall to where her wife lay sleeping. She couldn’t find the strength to pick up the dirt, but it was out of the way to pick up later. She wiped the tears away, moving on to the rest of her plants through the house before moving to the living room. 

And Rio wanted to scream. 

The lab report she assigned… the reports she was about to grade… the hand written reports she forced the students to do… were all in pieces scattered across the living room floor. They sat in wet little piles, rabbit teeth marks signs of complete obliteration. Not a paper was left intact. Never in all of her years had she had something like this happen. She was known for slapping down graded reports almost immediately, enjoying the silent tears down some students’ faces. And now, her reputation was ruined by a fucking rodent with floppy ears and a fluffy tail. 

She slowly lowered herself to the floor, grunting in pain as she gathered the paper, throwing the pieces into a pile on the coffee table, where they originally were to be dealt with later. She had to worry about her wife first. 

It took too long to stand for Rio’s comfort. She stumbled, her side pulling as white hot pain flared, but nothing tore. That’s what mattered anyway. She couldn’t tear them. All of this was for her son. 

She got to work in the kitchen, pulling out her wife’s snacks, the ones she could reach without pain and the ones that weren’t heavy. A panic gasped made her turn too sharply, her feet sprinting back to their room before she could even react to the pain. Her wife was sitting up in bed, frantically looking around before immediately relaxing once blues set their sights on her. 

“ Mi amor? ” she questioned softly, approaching her quickly.

Rio sat on her side of the bed, getting her wife to lay back down on the pillow, her hand smoothing over her wife’s belly. Nothing kicked at her hand, their little one still asleep. 

“What’s wrong, mi amor ?” she whispered, trying to ease her wife back into sleep. 

Agatha grabbed her hand tightly, refusing to let go as her eyes fluttered closed, “You weren’t here.”

Rio felt herself sigh softly, moving her hand to lace their fingers together over their son. She held herself back from leaning down and kissing her lips, instead lifting her other hand, moving tangles of chestnut away from her wife’s face. 

“I’ll always be here for you and our son,” she promised. 

Agatha didn’t even crack an eye open, “Then why weren’t you in bed for the last three hours?”

Rio blinked over at the digital clock, glowing dimly– 7:03 AM. Her heart quickened. Her wife was leaving at 10:30. The hours felt too thin, like paper drenched in ink, crumpling under the weight of what was to come.

“Just thinking and cleaning up the fact that your rabbit ate my students’ lab reports.”

Blue eyes shot open at that, “He what ?”

“Paper was everywhere. I told–”

She didn’t even get through the sentence before the laugh came- small at first, then rolling, then full. That rare, bright kind of laugh that turned into soft snorts when Agatha really let go. Rio froze like she always did when Agatha laughed like that, watching her with awe, letting the sound wrap around her like warmth. And no, she decided firmly– she would not ruin that music with the truth about the monstera. 

“You’re telling me,” Agatha snorted between giggles, “that Dean Vidal has to go in on Monday and explain that a rabbit ate the lab reports?” 

“I’ll blame you and your pregnancy brain,” Rio deadpanned, slowly leaning back against the pillow behind her and groaning slightly as her skin pulled. 

Agatha simply pushed herself up on her elbow, one brow arching with sharp suspicion, “You dare, and I’ll tell the board you fabricated your entire dissertation.” 

“You don’t even know where my dissertation is.” 

“I do. Bottom drawer. Labeled ‘For when Rio Thinks her Dick is Bigger than Mine.’” 

Rio huffed at her, “You sure about that?” 

Agatha smirked, sinking into her maternity pillow like a queen atop of a throne of smugness. 

“Check the sex drawer, dear,” she sneered. “I can guarantee you I purposely bought mine to be bigger than yours. Plus, you’re a size-queen when you’re a brat.”  

Rio looked down, holding back from chuckling as her tongue poked at her cheek. Her wife had her there. But her mind drifted to her precious monstera and the amusement fell.

She muttered under her breath, “I still hate that bunny.” 

That got Agatha’s attention, “You what?” 

“I hate him, Agatha.” 

Rio leaned forward, careful not to jostle her side too much, her voice rising in exasperation, “He chews everything. He’s eaten three pairs of my socks. He peed in my shoes. He has bitten enough and not to mention last night he tried to trip me– not once but three fucking times. And now, he destroyed my students’ reports. That was vengeance. That was premeditated.” 

“He’s teething,” Agatha said calmly, a hand rubbing over her belly as if a rodent could be equivalent to their child. “And he’s a prey animal. He senses your loathing.” 

“Because he earned it.” 

“He’s traumatized.” 

Rio gaped at her, “From what? Sleeping as if he pays the bills? The free roam he is allowed? His whole entire bunny kingdom in our dining room?” 

Agatha’s lips twitched, but she didn’t break, “He’s sensitive. Like me.” 

“Oh, hell no,” Rio hissed, pressing a hand to her side as she sat up straighter. “Do not compare yourself to that fluffy demon. You don’t chew drywall.” 

Agatha shrugged, far too at ease, “You haven’t seen me in a third-trimester rage.” 

“Don’t tempt me.” 

A soft quiet fell between them then, the absurdity of their conversation wrapping around them like a familiar quilt. Rio leaned her head back, the ache in her side reminding her of her limits. She reached out again, her fingers brushing Agatha’s hand, which was still resting over the slow swell of their son. 

“I don’t trust him near the baby.” 

Agatha blinked, the tiniest pause, “He’s a rabbit, Rio. Not a dog or a cat.” 

“I’d trust a dog or a cat over him,” she quipped back. 

“He’s a baby too–”

Rio sat up, pretending like it didn’t hurt, “Why are you defending him? He destroyed my monstera– the monstera you bought me.”

Her wife stared at her, not saying a word as her eyes were wide with surprise. She sat up slowly on the pillow as Rio blinked and then she realized she was crying. Her vision was blurry, hot tears running down her cheeks. Her wife moved slowly, easing her back down in the bed and kissing her lips softly. 

“It’s okay–”

“It’s not!” Rio protested, but Agatha was cradling her face, already calming her. 

“It is, my love. I’ll clean it up and we will look into getting three more of them if you would like.”

She groaned, more tears running down her face, “With what money?” 

Her wife tilted her head, and it hit Rio. Right. Evanora Harkness and the amount of zeros now at the end of their shared account. Thumbs caressed her cheeks again, wiping away the tears as her wife was careful not to lean on her, even with her belly.  

“You didn’t sleep last night, my love,” her wife didn’t even bother forming it as a question. 

Rio shook her head though in agreement, “How could I when I can’t even hold you?” 

Her wife sighed, “I’m right here, Rio. Rest. I’ll wake you before I leave and then you will stay in bed the rest of day.” 

She nodded weakly, feeling the hands leave her face. Agatha moved, angling so her body was horizontal and so her belly wouldn’t be resting on Rio. Instead, her head was resting atop of Rio’s breast, easing her aching heart only slightly.



Rio stirred from her slumber, the softness against her lips drawing her from the haze of sleep. A smile tugged at her lips as she let her hand wander gently across the curve of her wife’s cheek, holding her there, savoring the connection. Their lips parted briefly, just enough for a shared breath, before they were drawn together again, the kiss slow and tender, unraveling the last traces of drowsiness in Rio’s body. Her heart fluttered, each beat echoing the rhythm of her overwhelming love for her wife and the little life growing within her. 

When Agatha finally pulled away, Rio’s eyes shot open, her breath caught in her throat. She went to sit up quickly, but the movement was halted by a wince, and she eased herself into a more deliberate motion, taking in the radiant vision before her. Agatha stood there, a painting in a dark purple overall dress that hung loosely around her frame, allowing her to breathe. The fabric molded comfortably around her swollen belly, and beneath it, a soft white sweater hugged her arms, the Triple Goddess pendant resting against her chest like a sacred token. And then, the emerald wedding band, glinting on her finger, a finger that finally lost its swelling. 

With a careful motion, Rio swung her legs over the edge of the bed, her hands instinctively reaching for Agatha’s hips, drawing her closer. The smile that spread across her face was almost painful in its intensity as her palms gently brushed against the curve of Agatha’s belly, feeling their son shift beneath her touch. Her heart soared as the baby greeted her with a little nudge against her hand. Agatha chuckled softly at the look of awe on her wife’s face, the tenderness in her gaze making Rio’s heart swell. 

“You really are obsessed with me, aren’t you?” Agatha hummed, her voice laced with affectionate amusement.

Rio nodded her head, whispering in soft Spanish to their son who happily pushed back against her cheek the moment she rested her head there. Fingers smoothed out her sleep tousled hair as Rio kissed her stomach more before standing up. Agatha smiled brightly into her as her lips happily caught hers. 

“You are radiant, mi amor, ” she whispered, before singing softly. “ Mi hermosa, mi cielito, mi sol, mi corazón, mi alma– la madre de mijo.” 

Her wife laughed softly, both of them imagining Rio spinning her around if she had the strength to because– Gods, she really would do anything for her wife. Anything to make her smile, to make her wife laugh– to make her realize how beautiful she is and especially radiant carrying their son.

“You’re going to make me late, love,” her wife tried to pull away, but Rio pressed her lips to hers again.

She moved her lips up to her ear and whispered, “ Dioses , the things I want to do to you, cariño. ”

Her wife pushed her away, gently , tutting at her, “Nope, nope. Two weeks, Rio, and we can fuck to your heart’s content but for now, you don’t even get to tease me.” 

Rio tongue pushed against the side of her cheek, still refusing to release her, “So, just in time for my birthday?”

Her wife’s smirk was vicious, “Yes, my love, but unfortunately, not in time for Valentine’s Day. So, be good and don’t tear your stitches, so I can fuck my older woman properly to make up for that loss time and to celebrate you.” 

Rio shook her head, “Agatha, I am only tens months older than you.” 

“Still old, love. And now look at you, a milf.” 

She barked out a laugh before her hand went to her own side, holding over her stitches, “If anything that title belongs to you, cariño. ” 

“For you only,” her wife nodded, kissing her lips softly before pulling away. “Walk me out?” 

Rio followed her wife through the house, finding the monstera picked up, and in the living room, she caught a glimpse of the coffee table, where all of their papers were missing from it. She helped her wife with her coat before peering outside and finding the street empty. Good. 

“Text me as soon as you are done, mi amor. And if you get uncomfortable with a question, Lilia will be right there. And–” 

A hand was placed on her chest, “Breathe, Rio. It’s a little meeting and a quick Q&A. If anything, Pepper and Lilia already have a statement drafted. I’ll be home in at most four hours.” 

Rio did not breathe. She only stared at her wife unimpressed with a slight pout to her lips that her wife tried kissing away. 

“Four hours, and then I’ll be home and we can play whatever you want– Minecraft, those Italian brothers, or the last of whatever– that one weird fungi zombie game.” 

She lit up at that, kissing her wife again quickly before opening the door for her, handing her purse.  

“And Rio,” her wife turned the moment she stepped on the porch. “Please, don’t do something that tears your stitches.” 



***



Don’t tear her stitches. 

Easy. 

Or so she thought. 

 

The moment she watched Agatha’s car leave the driveway, Rio found herself pacing. What the fuck was she supposed to do? She couldn’t sit down. She was too scared to try to start cleaning anything or helping around the house. She couldn’t risk it. So, for the first hour, she had paced in the living room, ignoring angry black beady eyes staring at her. She ignored his thumps, his demand for attention. He was a rodent. He was fine. 

 

Then came the sound of chewing, of paper tearing. She stormed into the dining room, finding Scratchy had somehow opened one of her old science books from her days at Salemite’s. She sighed, dragging the heavy book from the shelf and into the center of the floor. 

“If I let you chew this, Señor, can we call it a truce?” 

The caramel spotted bunny ripped another page, chewing on it and letting the pieces fall to the floor. 

“Good enough, Señor ,” she sighed, turning towards the basement. 

The plan was simple. 

Four hours at most.

Do something that wouldn’t jostle her. Something where she wouldn’t be tempted to move too much. So Dead by Daylight was out, so was Red Redemption. She could play Hogwarts Legacy, but she also got too upset when she reached the spiders and had to fight them. The babies didn’t deserve it, and she knew she would end up moving wrong. Resident Evil was definitely out and so was Assassin’s Creed. So The Sims it was. 

She stepped down slowly, exhaling into the cool air. She went straight to the worn couch draped in old green blankets and in front of her was the tv where her PlayStation sat like a faithful dog beneath the screen. She lowered herself slowly, each movement precise, controlled. Her stitches ached, but dull. Bearable. She set the controller beside her, reached for a pillow to tuck beneath her side, and carefully reclined. The moment her back met the cushions, she felt the tiniest bit relaxed. 

She set her music up on her phone, turning on Unloved as The Sims logo flickered onto the screen. Rio smirked, knowing she was about to make something so dumb and beautiful. She started from scratch. The first Sim was a tall blonde, sharp features, and shoulder length hair. The blue suit was up next before giving her sim the perfect characteristics: “hot headed, psychotic, romantic but selfish.” Villanelle. 

Next came the soon-to-be-lover. She had crazy dark curls, permanently inquisitive eyebrows, and a trench coat. She made her a genius, a perfectionist, and made her a secret agent. Eve. 

Then, just to add a bit of fun, she dropped them in a suburban home with one bed. 

“Now,” she grinned to herself, tapping her finger against her lips, “let the longing begin.” 

She set them to “enemies with chemistry.” Their first interaction was a flirty introduction. The screen flashed red hearts and furious speech bubbles in tandem. Eve slapped Villanelle. Villanelle flirted back. She also cooked ramen and set the stove on fire. She smirked to herself, remembering when Hela did that to her ramen in the microwave in college. 

While their digital lives unfolded, she decorated their house. She added a large garden, a shit ton of books, something suspiciously hers and Agatha’s aesthetic. Except in this land, she could put a knife display in the kitchen, line the fireplace with human skulls, and ominous weapons littered the house. She puppeteered their lives as if she were a fanfic writer, controlling each and every moment, every tension filled scene, leading them closer and closer to romance. 

Time started to slip. She didn’t even realize how long she had been sitting there, escaping her stress for her wife as the music even trickled on. First, she barely reacted, hearing a few thumps of a certain creature making his way down the stairs. In her mind, Scratchy was still having fun with her book. It was a giant biology book on plants that she hardly touched since university. She didn’t think he would make his way downstairs to see what she was up to. 

Then, she heard it. Thump . A blur of caramel and white fur shot across the floor, straight towards her. She didn’t have a chance to move before the bunny jumped up, barely making it on her and that was the problem. Señor Scratchy jumped right on her stitches and white hot searing pain ripped through Rio. She dropped the controller as Señor Scratchy slipped from her side, to the couch, and onto the floor with a dull thud before he bounded away like nothing happened.

At first, she couldn’t move. The pain bloomed so suddenly, so violently, that it stole the air from her lungs. Her hand clutched her side, her breath caught in her throat as her other hand scrabbled at the cushion beside her, searching for purchase, grounding, anything. She blinked rapidly, her vision swimming, her body locking in place as the sting evolved into something sharper– wet, burning, wrong. 

Blood. Her fingers came away red. 

“Shit– shit– carajo ,” she hissed, folding forward slightly but quickly straightening when the movement nearly dropped her to the floor. 

Her pulse roared in her ears. She looked down and saw it. It was a little bit of blood, nothing to make her woozy or something too serious. If anything the bunny just pulled at the stitches and irritated her skin or maybe snapped a single stitch. She would be fine, but panic was creeping up her spine. 

This couldn’t be happening. If she didn’t heal, she wouldn’t be able to hold him, I won’t be able to hold my son. 

Rio forced herself up, teeth clenched as she powered through. She made it to the steps, keeping the pressure on her side as she slowly climbed the stairs, scared to risk truly tearing something or causing more damage. 

Calling her wife was out of the question. 

Please don’t do something that tears your stitches, her last words before she left.    

In her defense, she wasn’t doing anything but making a digital serial killer and digital MI6 agent fall in love. Gods forbid a woman had hobbies. 

It was the bunny, but he didn’t mean it. He had no conception of what was going on. Just that Agatha was the one that loved him most and Rio was extra human there. But she couldn’t worry her wife. She could just get to a clinic. Get it stitched again. 

Easy. 

Clean. 

Quick. 

She made it to the top of the stairs, finding her slides in the hallway that led to the front door, and just as she grabbed her keys there was an unmistakable sound of a metallic click. Rio’s heart stopped as her body refused to move. She figured she could just turn away, slip out the back maybe, text her she went for a drive. But Rio didn’t have her phone. It was still in the basement where The Sims were probably making out by now and her truck was parked next to Agatha’s in the driveway. 

The door creaked announcing judgment day and the slam was the final toll of death. 

Agatha stepped through the doorway, her presence unmistakable– heavy, electric, already tense. She had barely crossed the threshold when her eyes locked on the scene before her. Shoes half-on. Car keys clenched. A widening patch of red blooming through gray fabric at her wife’s side. Her whole body stopped. 

“Are you fucking kidding me?” the words sliced through the quiet like a blade. 

Rio flinched, instinctively straightening even though the movement wrenched her wound and made her wince. Her body screamed but it was nothing compared to the blaze in Agatha’s voice. 

“I said four hours. Four . And you couldn’t sit still for those goddamn hours?” 

Rio opened her mouth to speak, to explain, to plead– but Agatha was already moving. Not with tenderness. With precision. With fury. 

“You’re bleeding,” she hissed, eyes narrowing as she stalked forward, each boot clicking like a gun cocking. “You’re fucking bleeding and I can see it from the door. What the hell happened?” 

“It was just the rabbit–” 

“Oh, was it now?” Agatha cut her off. 

Her arms folded across her chest, one hand resting protectively over the curve of her belly. Rage radiated off her, but beneath it– panic, raw and desperate. 

“I’m sure it was the fucking bunny. Or was it the greenhouse this time? Or the bookshelf in the nursery that I told you to fucking wait on? Always some shit you ‘just had to do.’ Gods forbid you fucking rest and let someone else take care of you!” 

“Agatha–” 

“ No,” her voice trembled. “You always have to prove how strong you are, don’t you! As if jumping in front of a knife, wasn’t enough! As if me standing in front of a group of journalists telling how you saved mine and our son’s lives, wasn’t enough! Do you even–” 

She faltered– then pushed the words out like they were poison in her mouth, “ Do you even fucking care to hold him, Rio? ” 

Silence. 

The Earth wasn’t rotating. 

Oxygen didn’t work.

The air was nothing but carbon monoxide. 

And something in Rio snapped…

perhaps a heartstring. 

Agatha was mean– she had a beautiful snark and vicious streak– her mean wife to her gold retriever wife. It was one of the many reasons she fell in love with the woman. But never had it ever been used on her like this. 

“You– you– that’s not fair, Agatha, and you know it,” her voice cracked as she refused to allow the tears to run that gathered in the corners of her eyes.  

This was where Agatha expected her to fight back. To yell at her that she didn’t mean that– for Rio to scream how everything she had been doing had not been for herself, but for her loves. 

Because if anyone knew Rio, it was Agatha. She knew how Rio didn’t love lightly– how once she gave her heart, it was to the bone. How she threw herself into love with a kind of terrifying surrender, a devotion so absolute it didn’t matter if it burned her. Agatha had seen it. Felt it. Built her world around it. 

She knew Rio would do anything for her. Which is why it broke something deep and primal when Rio didn’t fight. Didn’t yell. She just stood there– silent– as the tears began to fall. Not from physical pain. Not from fear. But from heartbreak. Because Agatha hadn’t just struck a nerve– she had torn out the black thing. And instead of holding it, trembling and sorry, she had crushed it beneath her heel.

Agatha stared at her, and Rio saw the surprise and regret blooming too late in sea blue irises, “Rio–” 

“No.” 

She turned, grabbing her keys with blood still darkening the side of her hoodie. 

“Where the fuck are you going?” Agatha demanded, panic crawling into her voice now. 

“To breathe,” Rio bit out as she walked around her wife. “To not say something I can’t take back. Because you already did.” 

Rio slammed the door behind her, tears falling freely from her eyes as she flew to her truck. 

And when she blinked again, she was pulling into the emergency section of the hospital… 

still crying… 

still trembling… 

still digesting the venom that threatened to end her completely. 



I do not do this for myself

I’d walk through hell on living feet for you 


Hymn to Virgil ~ Hozier

 

 

 

Chapter End Notes

well.... *claps hands together*

until next time (which hopefully this weekend) where Agatha actually works on her emotions... (you should know by now that I will never do my girls dirty... so trust the process)

see you soon

gonna go read safeword fic now

💚💜💚💜💚💜

Chapter 24: I Can Only Do My Best ~ Hymn to Virgil Part 2

Chapter Summary

“We didn’t leave or break, mi amor. Just evolved like how caterpillars transform. Sometimes we need a dormant stage not only to heal, but to grow into something more beautiful.”

OR :

 

Agatha realizes what she did, they talk, and well... come see for yourself

Chapter Notes

‼️TW‼️ : panic attacks, talks of past abuse (physical and emotional) from Evanora Harkness

You all can thank @paybacksawitchh on twitter for the idea with Nicky. (he is fine yall)

also, to the girl i've been flirting with on Twitter, I KNOW YOU'RE THERE. Don't be shy 😘

To quote myself on twitter : I hope this chapter rips your heart apart, digging into the four chambers of your hearts, and then comes with a soothing balm, stitching you back together with a thread made of diamonds.

bare with me for mistakes, I can barely keep my eyes atm

love,

rioallalong_719 💚💜

See the end of the chapter for more notes

You are the reason I went through it, oh 

The only meaning as I knew it, my-yeah 

I can only do my best

 

Hymn to Virgil  ~ Hozier



Agatha

 

“ Do you even fucking care to hold him, Rio? ” 

 

“ Do you even fucking care to hold him, Rio? ” 

 

“ Do you even fucking care to hold him, Rio?"





She couldn’t move. 

She was frozen with her own words that were thrown echoing in her head as tears ran down her face. Everything in her screamed to go after her. But her body couldn’t. Instead, her mind replayed the way Rio’s face fell over and over again– the moment the words left Agatha’s mouth– her wife went pale. She stilled for a moment, her expression blank before those soil eyes became wet with tears. Her wife’s proud and broad shoulders slumped as her lip twitched as if she was holding back a sob and Agatha swore she heard the audible crack of her black heart. 

Agatha shook out of it, because it couldn’t have been the damn rabbit. Rio was being reckless like always. She found herself in the nursery before she even knew what she was doing, her heart pounding in her chest. The box for the unbuilt bookshelf sat in the same place in the corner, untouched as Agatha’s breathing quickened. She swallowed hard, her hand on her stomach as she made her way to the greenhouse, ignoring how the cold bit at her face as the door swung open. Inside, it was all the same. No new projects, the herbs all in the same place, the flowers the same perfect purple, the azaleas now in bloom. Agatha froze by them, her cheeks wet as her hand caressed an azalea’s velvet petal. Her hand shook as she plucked one, bringing it to her nose as her eyes squeezed shut, taking her back in time.  

 

 

“You know what they mean, right?” the raveness beside her asked as they both stared at the vase full of azaleas next to their bed. 

The morning golden light was just spilling in from the window, making the dark soil of Rio’s irises a liquefied amber, revealing hints of hazel and swirls of dark chocolate. Her attention moved to Rio’s back, the muscles subtly flexing– the lines of her shoulders down to the curve of her spine. Heat entered her cheeks as she thought of the other night– when she pushed Rio onto the bed and grabbed her by her hips to flip her over. Rio didn’t need any more directions, going on her knees arching her back as her chest rested on a pillow Agatha placed under her at the last second. She smirked, remembering her own hands tracing the outline of muscles, her thumbs moving in circles over them as Rio purposely flexed them for her. She half wondered if Rio would get a back tattoo the moment they did have the money. Goddess, it would be the end of her life if Rio did. 

She snapped her attention back to her girlfriend, when she moved, leaning over to pluck one the flowers from the vase. The golden morning sun highlighted more of her muscles in her arms, the bicep Agatha wanted to dig her teeth into–

“Cariño, are you listening?” Rio was now facing her, the covers pulled over her naked body. “You’re staring.” 

Agatha rolled her eyes, going to quip back when strong hands were on her. She yelped as her girlfriend manhandled her, laying her on top of Rio, their breasts squished together, their legs entangling, and their noses gently brushing. Rio was cold like always, but Agatha ran hot so the cool body was perfect, and yet… it warmed something deep within her as her eyes fluttered closed, butterfly wings caressing her nose. She loved mornings like this: Rio holding her, placing kisses to the nose she used to be so insecure of and yet, her girlfriend was obsessed with it. The way her hands and fingers would run through her hair, soothing her and telling her without words that Rio loved her. It was months ago, during the summer underneath the stars that Agatha had surprised Rio and said it first, and yet it was still delicate, still horrifying, and still something Agatha could barely wrap her head around. 

And yet, Rio somehow understood. They didn’t say often, just moments where it truly was warranted. Moments like last night, when something triggered Agatha– she wasn’t sure what, but her childhood came all rushing back– the beatings, the bruises and broken bones she hid, the one time her mother shaved her head in her sleep to “teach her a lesson.” Hence, why Agatha always kept her hair long. But nothing compared to the words, the way such small things embedded themselves into Agatha’s very being– she was a monster, she destroyed everything she touched, didn’t deserve love– that she was too hard to love. And here was Rio, loving her like it was as simple as breathing air. 

She remembered not being able to breathe, trying to pull away from Rio before she destroyed it, but Rio wouldn’t let her. No, Rio held her. Let her cry, scream, and sob without a single look of judgement, only a deep understanding, and Gods, it broke Agatha even more. Her girlfriend held her in bed, whispering over and over again, that she was not unloveable, that she didn’t destroy everything she touched, that she deserved to be loved– and Gods, for the first time in her life she actually believed it. Agatha was never good with spoken words, despite being a literature nerd, and in the moment, she replied in kind the only way she knew how. Rio and her made love deep into the night and as always, Rio was there, promising the world to her. 

A calloused but tender thumb caressed her cheek, her eyes fluttering open to stare down at the woman. She followed the slope of nose, to the column just above her lips that led to her nose, and then to her lips where a line ran down the center of her bottom lip. Her own thumb brushed over it, as her eyes took in her jawline, her cheeks, those puppy eyes that could make her fold with a single look when Rio wanted something. 

“Agatha,” Rio started slowly, her voice still scratchy from sleep, but holding something that was reverent, deep. “Do you know what azaleas symbolise?”

She shook her head, not trusting her voice as she sat up slightly on Rio’s chest, looking at the single azalea Rio twirled between her fingers.

“The colours of course have meanings and red is normally love, purple for royalty–” 

‘And here she goes,’ Agatha thought fondly, as Rio started yapping about flowers instead of going straight to the point. 

“The language with both colours and flowers goes so far back, but purple because it’s your favorite, and the flower itself? They promise new beginnings– the cycle of life, death, and rebirth. But when given, worldly– they symbolise ‘love’ and ‘gentleness,’” a faint pink entered Rio’s cheeks as her eyes flicked back to Agatha. 

A hand came forward, fingers brushing through her wild mane but with a tenderness that Agatha never knew existed, the flower was placed behind her ear as Rio smiled, that cute little gap between her teeth revealing. 

Rio’s voice turned to a quiet but certain whisper, “I give you these to say– I will never stop loving you with all the gentleness you deserve.”  

 

 

Rio never strayed from her words– she kept her word like it was a blood oath…

But Rio was wrong. 

Agatha did destroy everything she touched. 

 

She was frantic, tears bursting from her eyes, not even realizing she was in the basement, still clutching the azalea as she stared at the TV, TheSims still playing with what she assumed was Villanelle and Eve. She would have laughed– she would have shook her head, call her wife “pathetic” because if Rio had the writing skills she would be one of those pathetic losers who wrote fan-fiction, hyper obsessing over lesbians who didn’t exist– over the stereotypical lesbian couple where one of them dies in the end and the other just has to live with it.

Instead, her eyes narrowed to the couch where a deep maroon stained a green blanket and something on their carpet looked suspiciously like a tiny paw print of blood. Rio was telling the truth. 

Of course she was– you just had to ruin it– destroying everything you touch, a voice in her head sneered at her. 

Her hand shook violently as she reached for the phone on the couch, pausing the music. She felt her body go numb as she realized her wife left her phone, no way of contacting her unless she called the hospital– but it was too late…

 

"To not say something I can’t take back. Because you already did.”

 

Because she already did. 

 

Agatha found herself upstairs on their couch, her hands on her stomach, but no movement came. There wasn’t a push against her palm, not even a shift inside her. He was utterly still. She rubbed a little harder, pressing her hand more firmly. She needed his movement, needed something to ground her. Scratchy was nowhere to be found. Her feet hurt from pacing and standing at the conference and she knew she was going to lose it if now her son was ignoring her. 

And he did.

Not a single shift or hiccup. 

Just the eerie stillness she realized she caused.

A pain sobbed escaped her throat as her mind plagued with her mother’s words. They clawed at her throat, tearing into her very flesh as every loving kiss from her wife, every tender embrace– was washed away. She destroyed everything, and somehow she held onto this delusion for fifteen years that she could have Rio forever. But no, love was not meant for Agatha Hakrness. 

Go ahead cry– you think your life is hard? Evanora’s voice screamed at her. Try giving birth to a fucking monster like you. You ruin everything you fucking touch. 

Rio was going to leave her– come back and slap divorce papers and pack. And then, Rio would take their son away from her– claim she was unfit in court with her history of being abused by her own mother. 

Another sob surged from her body, trembling her very being as her heart felt as if it was tearing in two. Her body was weak as her fingers almost clawed at her stomach– another part of her begging for their little one to move. He was always active this time of day, right after lunch, and his mami happily fussed over him, allowing him to kick her hands as she whispered soft Spanish to him that seemed to make him so excited, rolling and pressing against her stomach as if begging to be in Rio’s arms. 

He deserved a mother who would love him so much that he felt like he was the moon, stars, and sea– and Agatha… she didn’t know if she could be that mother. But Rio could– she would be– she would spoil the shit out of him, dress him up in costumes, buy matching clothes, hold him close when he got scared, chase him in their backyard, teach him about nature… and Agatha? What would she even do? 

“Please,” her voice broke through the eerie silence. “Please, my purest love, move.” 

He didn’t. 

Oh. 

Oh. 

He knows. 

Another sob broke from her. Their son already knew the kind of monster she was– he already knew how she would snuff out any candle, any light. He knew what a terrible mother she was already becoming and she had barely begun. 

“I didn’t mean it,” she whispered, voice raw and shaking. “I didn’t mean it. I didn’t mean it. Please, please– oh!” 

She broke off into a sob hugging her belly tighter to herself as Rio’s face flashed in her mind– the tears, the betrayal, the heartbreak– 

“I-I didn’t– didn’t mean it,” she hiccuped even louder. “I-I was scared, my purest love, so scared that-that she-she would– that something would-would hap-happen to her.” 

Another sharp wail.

“I-I can’t do this without her. I can’t– I can’t– please, I can be good. Pl-please, don’t go quiet on me. Don’t leave me too.” 

Her fingers pressed harder against the curve of her belly, not in pain, but in desperation, like she could force herself back into worthiness by sheer will alone. 

“Please– please, little one. I can be good . I can be good . Just one little kick, one roll– please!” 

The stillness echoed like a grave. 

And that was when the panic tipped into collapse. 

Her body folded in on itself, curling sideways onto the couch, arms around her belly as if shielding him from herself. Her face buried into the couch, muffling her sobs– the soba were loud wails, ones that came from the very depths of her soul– a soul that felt as if it was being left behind by death herself.

 

***

 

She didn’t know how long passed, not until the light spilling into the house was dim with the setting sun. And not once did their son move. Her soul continued to grieve the loss– the loss of her the love of her life, the loss that she caused– because she was unloveable. Because it was inevitable that she would be her own demise. 

Her cries consumed her to the point she didn’t hear the faint groan of the door creaking open. Didn’t hear the slow shuffle or the grunt of pain from the foyer. Didn’t notice the lights flipping on nor the sound of her own name being called once, twice– soft and worried– like she already sensed something was wrong beyond the fight from hours prior. 

Only when she felt a fingers push her hair out of her face did Agatha look up. Rio stood over her, still in the gray hoodie, stained with blood. Her face was blotchy, her eyes tired and red rimmed from her own tears. She looked wrecked and yet, there was only immediate worry as Rio’s lips moved, but Agatha couldn’t hear her. Instead, the raging sob came, spilling from her as she realized her wife was back. Rio didn’t leave her. There wasn’t hate in her eyes. Sure, hurt, but Rio returned. 

Her wife was trying to talk to her, but Agatha couldn’t hear it. She sat up, ignoring how she struggled with the added weight before standing. Before she could stop herself, to even think that Rio would need space, she launched herself, angling her body away from Rio’s freshly stitched side as she nuzzled into her neck. 

She almost gasped when arms wrapped around her, fingers combing through her hair as Rio hushed her softly, pressing kisses to the side of her head. But it wasn’t enough. She still said that to Rio– to the one person who loves her unconditionally. To the one person who saw right through her walls without even trying– the woman who constantly grounded her, protected her, worshipped the fucking ground she walked on– the woman who had been there the moment she revealed the stick to her, cherishing, always a step ahead of what she needed… a woman who worked for weeks carving a crib from scratch just so their son would feel extra loved. 

Agatha Harkness was a monster. She destroyed everything she touched, and worse, she hurt the woman that she loved with every cell in her body… and their son growing inside her was still, refusing to move… sensing the evilness of his mother. 

“I’m sorry, I’m sorry, oh Rio–” she lobbed louder into her wife’s skin. “I’m sorry– I didn’t mean it. Pl-please Rio.” 

“ Cariño,” her wife’s voice was gentle, but it cracked with its own raw pain. 

“Pl-please! I-I’m s-sorry. I’m sorry,” she couldn’t stop the wave as her voice was visceral. “I can be good. I can be good! Please, please! Please, my love!” 

Fingers were continuous through her hair, the other hand cradling the back of her head as she cried into the crook of her neck. Her wife pressed soft kisses to her head as she continued to cry, unable to stop begging and apologizing and part of it was for the son who refused to move. 

“You are good, cariño–”

“No,” she croaked, refusing to pull away from her. “No-no-no, not after what I-I said– oh, Rio. Please, please, pleasepleaseplease.” 

“My love” and oh how Agatha’s heart leapt at those words  “you need to take a few breaths. You’re having a panic attack.” 

But Agatha couldn’t. 

She couldn’t stop trembling. 

She couldn’t stop sweating. 

Her heart was pounding. 

Her breath was restricted, and the pain in her chest wouldn’t stop. 

“The breathing exercises, remember them?” Rio’s voice was a calm melody, guiding her to the eye of the storm. “In through your nose, love. Out through your mouth. In. Out.” 

With a mix of Rio’s delicate beating heart and lulling voice, Agatha’s cries ceased. Her breathing came back, matching her wife’s as more soft, lingering kisses pressed to the side of her head. She didn’t know how long they stood in each other’s embrace, more kisses pressed to her head as they swayed carefully, both aware of Rio’s stitches. They swayed until Agatha noticed the ache in her lower back, the uneasiness she felt being on her feet for this long. Without a word exchanged, Rio pulled away, their eyes locking for a breath and when Agatha saw her wife’s soil eyes, she looked away, unable to fathom the pain in them. Rio didn’t push, just merely took her hand and guided them to the bedroom. 

Both hurt in their own ways, they still moved in sync, in a tender but aching quietness as Rio helped her out of her shirt before Agatha was fully undressing Rio– her wife unable to bend down. The bandaged now wrapped around her torso, the blood gone, but each breath shuddered with pain as Agatha struggled to bend down as she pulled Rio’s joggers off of her. At this point, she would have teased her for her boxers filled with cartoon bones. Her wife would have swept her into her arms, maybe made love to her, or just cuddled her and they would talk of their future. Instead, Agatha turned to their dresser where a half glass of water remained alongside Rio’s pill bottle. Her wife took the pill without a fight before laying down on Agatha’s side instead. She blinked but Rio weakly held her arms out. 

“Just…don’t put too much weight on me.”

Agatha climbed in beside her, her head resting on Rio’s chest, her belly resting on her good side. They didn’t speak. They just laid there in their hurt until Agatha’s tired, pregnant body couldn’t take it anymore, easing her into a dreamless sleep with her wife still by her side. 



***



Agatha awoke to her whole body being sore. Every movement hurt in its own type of way and yet her eyes were the worst. They were heavy, tired– dry with too many tears shed and somehow not enough as she lifted her head, finding she hadn’t moved from her position at all. Her head was on Rio’s chest, her belly on her, away from the bandage that wrapped around her. Rio was breathing loudly– she never exactly snored, but her breath was loud, as if she were greedy for air. She half-wondered if it was Rio vaping for almost ten years straight before finally quitting for their wedding. 

She sat up slightly, looking at the arm wrapped around her back, the hand resting near her stomach. She rubbed her stomach, wondering if he would finally move. He didn’t, and Gods, the images, the pain, the broken look on her wife’s face all came rushing back. She could cry again, sob, dive into Rio’s arms– beg her for forgiveness, but she knew she didn’t deserve it. Her eyes flicked over her wife’s features, finding the pinch of her eyebrows finally relaxed, that haunted expression gone, and her whole body fully rooted into their bed. She was out cold, probably thanks to the amount of medicine in her system… and well, thanks to her after breaking her love’s heart. 

Agatha slowly got up, scared to jostle her as she went to the bathroom, barely brushing her hair, but cleaning her teeth as she walked out into the kitchen. She felt robotic, her body drained from emotions, but her heart was burning alive, begging her to do something– make it up, somehow, but she didn’t know how. What do you say to the love of your life as you break their very soul? 

She ended up sitting at the kitchen counter, not having the energy to make tea or even have toast… she took a few deep breaths, her hands on her stomach, rubbing, searching– but he did not move. Her body was a big fat liar when it came to the stiffness of her eyes, and the tears were back as she searched for him. 

“Please, please, my purest love, move for me,” she begged. “Just a flutter. Mami está aquí . She’s home, so please, por favor, mijo.” 

Nothing. 

She placed her forehead on the cool countertop, watching her tears drip onto the dark marble until she heard hesitant footsteps padding from their room. She perked up, but the steps faded into the living room, the couch cushion making the softest noise before the television clicked on. She swallowed hard, standing up from the counter and starting tea as she already heard the strange inspirational music of a nature documentary filling the home. 

Okay, semi normal. Maybe, we just move forward, she thought to herself as the water finished and she quickly made Rio’s favorite green tea with Dragonwell, before making hers a simple herbal with honey. She made her way slowly into the living room, finding Rio laying on her back stiffly on the couch. Her eyes were trained to the clicking of whales and immediately she recognized one of Rio’s comfort series : Secrets of Whales . 

“They’re known as killer whales, but their real name is orca,” Sigourney Weaver started before the orcas whistled and clicked on the screen. “They’re the ocean’s top predator.” An orca floated closer, its body tilted as it sang. “But their tough reputation is because they put family first.” 

Ouch. 

Rio flinched as well, but hers was deeper, because Rio constantly put them first, and stupid fucking Agatha– 

Agatha placed the mug of tea down next to her wife before crossing in front of her, sitting on the other side where Rio’s feet were. They didn’t speak, tension crackling between them as Sigourney droned on. She couldn’t count how many times her wife had watched this series and didn’t understand how she didn’t grow bored. Instead, Agatha folded her legs underneath her, rubbing her stomach and trying to coax to their son to move. He still wouldn’t as if he could sense the tension between his mothers– there wasn’t a lack of love between them, just something hurt and she swore their little one was picking up on. She swallowed back the tears again, but her mind was running rampant. Did she do something physically? Did she hurt him when she was spiralling over her wife? What if her panic attack caused something– maybe he didn’t get enough air and… she killed him…? 

She bit her lip, shaking it out. It was a moment of weakness. Just one panic attack. Nothing that could have caused any permanent harm. Sometimes babies went still and perhaps he was moving, she just couldn’t feel it between the overwhelming emotions draining her and her wife the past three days. 

Agatha moved her hands, her body moving on muscle memory as she shifted closer to her wife, placing her feet in her lap as her thumbs worked the knots out in her calves. She let the motion of her thumb ground her, digging into a knot as Rio laid her head back, but she wasn’t ready for the tone to leave her mouth. 

“What are you doing?” Rio’s voice was rough, raspy, but an underlying anger was there.

She froze, her hands still wrapped around her wife’s leg as she stared at the orcas on the screen instead of meeting the intensity of her Rio’s gaze. She knew if she looked, she would sob, cry, and beg– but she already did that all last night. What more was she to do? 

“I-I just thought– um,” she trailed off, not really having an answer. 

“Agatha,” the way her name was spoken sounded like a death sentence. “We are not going to just sit here and pretend like you didn’t say that to me last night.” 

She felt herself bristle on the spot. The switch was flipped and her defense built in an instant as she turned her head slowly towards her wife. 

“I am not doing that,” she spoke darkly, but before she could even snap– say something else she knew she would regret, Rio’s own gaze darkened into something she had only seen directed at someone else. 

“ No,” Rio growled, her voice carrying an ancient power behind it. “ You don’t get to talk to me like that after what you said. Do you even realize how badly you hurt me, huh? It was more than what any knife could do.” 

Agatha swallowed, the switch flipped back, “I didn’t mean it–” 

“No, but you still said it–” 

“I was scared!”

“You were cruel,” her wife’s words were final, cutting deep into her and taking the air from her lungs, but Rio didn’t stop there. “You were cruel.”
 She said it again, quieter this time, but no less sharp. Her voice was shaking, raw, ragged at the edges that pierced Agatha’s very soul. She went to open her mouth, but there was nothing. No apology would ever be enough. How could she apologize for becoming the monster she was always destined to become? Nothing could patch how Agatha tore open Rio’s heart and destroyed each of her four chambers. 

Rio shifted, pulling her legs back from Agatha’s lap like she was reclaiming something– her space, her boundaries, maybe even her sense of self. 

“I know you were scared,” Rio continued, her voice breaking at the edges. “But that doesn’t make it okay. You looked at me, and you made me feel like I didn’t matter. Like I didn’t love you enough. Like the last six months– fuck, like the last fifteen years didn’t even matter… like I was just some dog on a leash following you around because I didn’t know anything else.”

“That’s not true, Rio,” Agatha whispered, eyes wide as the tears made her blue eyes look like glass. 

Rio shook her head, her hand clenched into fists in her lap as she replied, “It doesn’t change how it felt– how I felt. You know what it did to me to walk out that door with blood soaking through my side, and the only thing I could think was that you– ” her voice cracked, and she bit it back. “That maybe you didn’t want me anymore.” 

Something snapped hard in Agatha as she partially lurched, “No– no, Rio, don’t say that. Please– you know that is not true.”

 “I’m saying it because it felt real,” she snapped, breathing hard now, tears collecting at the corners of her eyes. “You basically told me that I didn’t care to hold my son. Our son. Do you know how many nights I’ve poured over those stupid ass baby sites– watched every video on pregnancy, on baby’s health, on parenting– how many fucking times I talk to him when you’re asleep? How many times do I actively have to stop daydreaming in the middle of a lecture– imaging you with our son– whether he will have my eyes or yours? If he will have your chin or my nose? Do you even realize how long I spent on the crib? How long I toiled between taking care of you, keeping up with work, and making sure it was perfect?”

That did it. 

Agatha’s eyes filled instantly, her breath hitching. 

“I know I messed up,” she whispered, her voice strangled. “But I never meant to say that. I didn’t mean to make you feel that way– I see you all that you do! please– te veo, Rio– ” 

She couldn’t finish. A sob cracked from her throat, raw and wrenching, and she doubled over slightly, hands cradling her belly as her breath started to catch. It was worse than last night. Her whole body refused to hold her up as pain ruptured through her very being. One hand clawed at her throat as she choked out the sobs, grief and guilt and fear, pinning her down and having their way with her. The sobs slowly turned into something that was a mixing of hyperventilating and choking. Her face was burning and worse, their son still wasn’t moving. 

Rio’s face changed in an instant. 

“Agatha?” she asked, suddenly stricken. “Agatha, breathe.” 

“I c-can’t–” she gasped, trying to sit upright. “I– he’s not– he’s still not moving– Rio, I think– I think something’s wrong– ” 

All of the hurt, the fury, the sharp edges Rio had carried with her melted into ash as she moved from her spot, a mix of carefulness and franticness as she sat beside her wife. 

“No, hey, hey, look at me, mi amor. We’re going to the hospital–”

“NO!” she choked out, turning and burying her face into Rio’s shoulder. 

Goddess, she was horrible at this. Rio was the one hurting and yet, she couldn’t stop her own self from hurting as well. Yet, that was always the way with them, even when they first started dating. If one of them was hurting, the other would feel it too– not as strong, but dull and aching as if their very souls were connected and it was moments like these that Agatha believed their souls were– also the hormones pumping through her very being. 

Rio nodded against her, kissing her head. 

 

Okay, yeah, so there was a fear of hospitals added in whatever mass of problems she definitely needed to see a therapist about. She swallowed it down when her wife was stabbed, but that still didn’t stop her from yelling at every nurse until she was allowed at Rio’s side. But for her, it was worse. Agatha kicked and flailed, even if her own bones were broken she refused. 

Rio hated medicine. Agatha hated hospitals. What a wonderful duo. 

She was going to be such a stubborn cunt when it came time for their son to join them. 

 

Her wife knew, she always knew. If anything her mind carried her back to the first time Rio dragged Agatha kicking and screaming to the hospital during college. Agatha cussed out any nurse that tried to make Rio leave, but to her own surprise, Rio did stay. Her hand never left hers– in fact Alice and Lilia actively had to bring them food because Rio would refuse to leave her side. It was that same day, Rio had learned the amount of times Agatha had gone to the hospital as a teenager and was left there alone because of her mother. 

“Okay, okay,” her wife murmured. “Just breathe with me.” 

Agatha obeyed, syncing her breaths with Rio’s the way they had practiced in those sporadic online prenatal classes– classes Rio dragged her to, though they both knew they should attend more. But fear made a coward of her. When it crept in, Agatha’s instinct was always the same: run, or push it away before it could push first. Now, trembling in the quiet aftermath, she pressed her face beneath the shelter of Rio’s chin. Rio breathed deeply, exaggeratedly, within the limits of her stitches– offering rhythm, offering anchor.

When Agatha’s breaths soothed, when the pain of breathing faded, lips were placed to her head, the voice was just under a whisper and it trembled : “I love you, Agatha. I think more than either of us will understand.”

And the tears were back, but they stilled the moment Rio’s hand rested on her stomach, because the moment it did, there was an obvious nudge, a hard push inside her that seeked Rio’s touch.  

Agatha sat up straighter, her hand coming next to Rio’s. The nudge was stronger, a steady press against both of their hands. Agatha let out a choked out laugh in surprise. Beside her, her wife pressed a gentle kiss to her cheek. 

“He’s alright, mi amor, ” she whispered softly.

Agatha swallowed hard, “And what about us?” 

She expected Rio to pull away, to flinch back, but she didn’t; she kept right at Agatha’s side, kissing her head, her hand searching for their son who responded. 

“We will be.” 

Agatha nodded. It wasn’t what she wanted to hear, but it was better than the alternative. It was better than Rio and her screaming at each other. She closed her eyes, breathing through her nose, wishing she could go back in time and take it all back– to be the one rushing her to the hospital instead of spouting venom. She sounded so much like her mother. 

“You’re not your mother,” Rio startled her. 

She always wondered if Rio had magic and was able to read her thoughts, “How did you know I was thinking that?” 

Her wife kissed her head again, “Because of how scared you are to become of her. Because you actively acknowledge it– because I know you.”

Agatha shook her head, “That still doesn’t change that was something she would have said.” 

“Then why did you say it?” 

Rio’s voice wasn’t raised. It wasn’t angry. It was only calm, curious. It was Rio wanting to understand her more– and perhaps Rio already knew… Rio definitely already knew. She just wanted Agatha to say why– for her to realize why. 

“Because I am too scared to lose you, Rio,” her mouth was moving before she realized it. “I am so fucking terrified because I can’t do this without you. I saw the blood and all I thought was rushing you to the hospital– of all the monitors– of something going wrong– of the fucking impossible where you don’t take another breath. ” 

Tears sprang from her eyes, but a hand was cupping her cheek, tilting her face up for Rio’s lips to press against them– kissing them away. Her heart was pounding, its veins unravelling from the muscle and reaching out towards the woman. 

“So, I did what was easier. I lashed out instead of falling apart… because if I fall apart and you’re not there… who is left to pick up the pieces?” 



Agatha replayed what she said to her wife over and over again and she realized…

 

They both loved each other more than either of them could or would understand. 

 

The couple sat in silence, Agatha’s words echoing in both of them. Neither knew how else to respond… so they sat there in their hurt, in their love…so grand, so profound, written deep in the fabric of the cosmos itself… 

 

 



They moved slowly– awkwardly. The hurt didn’t vanish from Rio, but her love didn’t either. Agatha took whatever she could from Rio who willingly held her, kissed her head, and moved slowly with her in the kitchen as they prepared leftovers between lunch and dinner. They remained close the whole day despite needing space, but they found their space in the silence– in the silence of pages turning of books, in the silence of pens scribbling for grading, or crackling of keys as Rio answered emails. Even in the background of noise between nature documentaries or Agatha pulling up live videos of Fleetwood Mac on the television. 

Through it all, there was a peace in the house that unsettled them both, not in the way that they weren’t really talking, but more in the way of something that partially started the fight… Rio was the first to notice it, looking around the floor. 

“Where is he?” Rio asked softly, snapping Agatha’s attention from the baby blanket she was doing the finishing touches on. 

“Who?” 

Rio tilted her head, “Señor Scratchy.” 

Agatha looked around herself, knowing the bunny usually pressed up against her stomach when she was crocheting, but the caramel spotted fuzz ball was nowhere to be seen. 

Rio turned to her, “You didn’t get rid of him, did you?”
She raised an eyebrow, wondering if she was hoping she did, “No, and he wasn’t around last night either.”

Her wife went to stand, but Agatha snapped her fingers down, “Keep still, my love.” 

“You should be resting too,” Rio bit back. 

Agatha ignored the tone as she wandered the house, checking under tables or any space she could see without bending over. Her hand remained on her round stomach. Their son decided to continuously turn and toss, pressing against her stomach since Rio coaxed him to move. He was being annoying– dramatic. 

So you go from dead silence to not knowing how to sit still, my purest love? she thought to him.

Also as if reading her thoughts, he delivered a decent kick to her ribs.

“Enough of that. Your mami is okay,” she muttered to him. 

It only earned her another kick. She rolled her eyes, keeping her hand on her side as he seemed to take interest in her hand, pressing against, jabbing at her palm, between her fingers. She hoped he had Rio’s endless curiosity. 

She went into their room, finding nothing. Their bathroom? Nothing. The extra bathroom that would be for their son? Also nothing. Then, she moved into their nursery and there he was, sitting on her rocking chair like a mighty prince upon a throne that was not yet for him, but in his mouth was the azalea she must have left in the basement. Her heart dropped. Azaleas were toxic to most animals, especially bunnies and this little thing had it in his mouth. She moved, ready to yell at the stupid rodent, but he bounded away before she could blink. 

Agatha followed him out, her steps slow, a hand on her back that ached. When she made it to the living room, Señor Scratchy was sitting on Rio’s chest and in between Rio’s fingers… was the azalea. 

“I give you these to say– I will never stop loving you with all the gentleness you deserve,” Rio whispered, repeating her own words from so long ago. 

But Agatha heard her, and for, who knows how many times she cried that day, tears ran down her face again. So, in the panic to hold her dignity– whatever was left of it… definitely wasn’t much… Agatha deflected with a joke. 

“Does that mean you have accepted Señor Scratchy’s apology?” 

Rio snorted softly, rolling her eyes before softening, “No, definitely not, but I do accept yours.” 

Oh. 

Oh. 

There goes whatever was left of her dignity.

Rio pushed the bunny off of her gently, and he avoided her stitches, bounding away to hide again before Rio pulled her into another embrace, her lips soft, sealing their love with a gentle promise to her cheek. 

Sunday came like the slow rise of the moon. It was still awkward, still a little painful, still a little tense. Yet, Agatha expected it. Part of her still couldn’t believe Rio allowed them to touch, allowed Agatha to lean into her embrace when this should be the time Rio would hate it. But Rio didn’t. She didn’t flinch, she didn’t push Agatha away… but she didn’t really speak. Nothing but time would heal the wounds Agatha inflicted… but what truly mattered to Agatha was that Rio was still there. Still loving her in silence, even when she knew there were moments Rio wished not to. Rio Harkness-Vidal would never stop loving Agatha Harkness, even if she was skinned to the bone and became Death herself. 

 

 

 

Agatha sat in the window nook, blankets around her frame as she read Sappho and her history, holding the book on her stomach as she ate strawberry gelato. She was lost, losing herself in the rhythm, between Sappho’s own words and the island of lesbos itself. It was comforting, something that she remembered reading while hiding it from her mother as a teenager. 

Rio was in the basement, playing TheSims and actually relaxing. They were slowly getting back to normalcy, slowly healing, and that was enough for Agatha… for now. She knew her body, her very soul was crying for her wife, but Agatha knew she had to give her space. Her presence couldn’t heal everything. 

She jumped when she heard the basement door creak open. Looking up, Rio was holding her laptop– her black rimmed glasses on the tip of her nose, her hair pulled messily into a claw clip, only in black sweatpants handing low on her hips… 

And she’s not wearing boxers for once, Agatha realized as her eyes trained on the V-line running into those sweatpants before her eyes were wandering up her abs, to her bandaged side, and to her green sports bra. 

Agatha bit her lip hard, realizing now was not the time to be looking at her wife like she was. Rio was in no shape for what Agatha wanted to do to her… possibly emotionally, but especially physically. Yet, it didn’t stop the vague image of her grinding against those abs– sue her, her wife was hot and she was nonetheless hers.

“ Mi amor, ” Rio’s voice had more spark to it than it had since she was stabbed and Agatha’s heart soared. “Did you see the students posted what happened on The Spider’s Web ?” 

 

Right, Westview University’s mascot was a fucking tarantula which was partially why Rio wanted them work there. They had the mascot in the science center with the zoologist professor– the professor Agatha avoided like the plague. There was no way in hell she would ever hold that thing– and yet, Rio always wanted one of them. She had to talk Rio out of it constantly. And well, recently it worked with the fact they were going to have baby and Rio only thought of the fucking leg hairs bothering their son’s skin– not the fangs, not the webs, not the beady eyes– just the fact that tarantula’s rubbed their legs together to release their hairs.

 

“They quoted you too,” Rio added, handing her the laptop before climbing next to her.  

Rio’s one hand rested on her stomach as she scrolled to the top of the page to stare at the title : 

 

Dean, Mother, and Protectrice

 

Agatha snorted, “That’s a stupid ass title.”

Rio rolled her eyes, chuckling lightly as her wife’s critique before she read : 

 

 

In a harrowing event that unfolded this past Wednesday, students witnessed a confrontation in the central quad between Dr. Agatha Harkness and CEO of Harkness, Inc, Evanora Harkness. The pair were spotted by students speaking harshly to one another until in a flash, a knife was pulled on Dr. Harkness. She was heroically saved by Dean Vidal, yet, the confrontation has brought up questions both for campus safety, protection, and justice, as well as put Dr. Rio Vidal, Dean of Faculty, and Head of the Biological Science Department in the spotlight of controversy. 

 

Dr. Rio Vidal was in the middle of a lecture when two students barged into her classroom, reporting that her wife, who is carrying their child, was being threatened. Eyewitnesses report Dr. Vidal responded without another thought, sprinting out of the hall and into the quad where the pair were now yelling at each other. 

 

“Well, we heard the yelling and screaming– even our professor was concerned and we stepped out of class without another word. Dr. Harkness was there and then next thing I knew there was a knife. Dude, it happened so fast, I don’t even know if anyone had the chance to either run away or run to help her when we see this blur of green. The crack was sickening, but nothing compared to hearing Dr. Vidal yell in pain as she managed to get the hag onto the ground, throwing the knife aside,” a sophomore literature major told us on Friday after the attack. 

 

The blur of green was Dr. Vidal physically subduing Evanora Harkness, who had raised a knife against Dr. Harkness, in an effort to protect her wife and unborn child. Police reported that the impact of the body slam broke multiple ribs on the CEO’s side, and though Dr. Vidal herself sustained a deep laceration on her ribs, she did not retaliate further. 

 

“Bro, Dr. Vidal literally threw herself at the crazy b*tch. There wasn’t a single flicker of hesitation. And then once the hag was staying down, Vidal moved off of her and Dr. Harkness was there holding her. I want a love that strong,” a freshman Biology student commented, still in awe of the heroism of the Dean of Faculty. 

 

Dr. Vidal and Dr. Harkness were then rushed to the hospital where Dr. Harkness confirmed her wife received six stitches and is healing at home for the meantime. However, friends of the CEO of Harkness Inc, have stepped forward, questioning the death of Evanora Harkness and claiming that our dean did this premeditated. But, police put out a statement that Evanora Harkness denied immediate medical care and succumbed to the intensity of her injuries of her own doing. Evanora was about to be charged for not only breaking the restraining order, but also for attempted murder. Evanora’s followers did not listen. So, this recent Friday, Dr. Harkness herself came and spoke on behalf of her wife : 

 

“Evanora Harkness had never been kind to me, even as a child. I found myself hiding my identity, scared to make a single wrong move in fear of being hurt by my own mother. At the age of eighteen, I had run away from home and went to Harvard where I met my wife. She has taught me how to be myself, how to love myself, and how to accept the love she gave me. Rio has been my protector for as long as I can remember. 

 

When Evanora broke into our apartment during our college years, we had put a restraining order against her… a restraining order that she had broken twice. On the second time, it wasn’t only my life threatened. It was our child’s life. The knife was aimed straight for my stomach, and I had no time to react until Rio was just there, protecting me like she always has done– but again, it wasn’t just me. Rio protected our child, putting her life in danger to make sure we weren’t harmed. 

 

With this acknowledgement, I extend my deepest gratitude to Miss Eva Bracken and Miss Ireland McGrath, two bright freshmen students in both mine and my wife’s classes. Without your quick thinking, I may not be standing here today.

 

To the students also struggling with their identities: you are valid exactly as you are. Don’t let the world convince you to be anything else but yourself. The right people will love you back just as you are without change or asking you to be less of yourself. And when you find that kind of love, you’ll see how beautiful it truly is. Love can heal what feels broken. It can make you stronger in ways you never saw coming. But this wisdom isn’t mine alone. It comes from the woman who is stronger than anyone I know– the one who protects me, who holds me through every storm, who loves me with a fire and a depth that never wavers.

 

And many of you know her as ‘The Green Witch of the Science Department.’”

 

 

Agatha watched as those brown eyes softened, staring at her like she hung the moon and stars, but like always, they resorted to their teasing, “You just ruined my reputation at work.” 

“Well, I just figured the Dean of Faculty needed to be humbled. It was about to time the school knew what a hopeless lovergirl you– mmph–” 

Lips interrupted words, soft and certain, pressing into hers with the heat of something tender and a little desperate. Rio’s palm pressed against her stomach– not hard, not possessive, just there, steady, like an anchor in the middle of a storm. As if grounding herself. As if reminding them both they were still here. Still whole. Still expecting a little one together. Agatha exhaled a shaky breath against her mouth, the last of their shared tension unraveling. The kiss bled warmth into the hollow of her chest, melted the ache she had carried since those words left her mouth. 

Their lips met again– slow, familiar, sure. Agatha reached up, cupping Rio’s cheek with one hand, her thumb pushing lightly against the subtle swell of her cheek. Her wife’s tongue slid languidly against hers. It was a quiet conversion between them, but not one of lust, rather a vow sealed in breath, lips, and tongue. She hummed into the kiss as she felt their love that once felt fractured, forged into something as hard as diamonds. 

Rio kissed her like it was forgiveness, as if to tell her that she completely forgave her– that Rio was no longer hurt. That there was no other place Rio would rather be than with Agatha at her side, her hand on her swollen belly, and their son happily kicking at Mami’s palm.

When they pulled away, their foreheads rested against each other’s. 

“Did you really mean it?” Rio whispered in awe as the lower half of her glasses fogged up from their shared breath.  

Agatha nodded against her, “Every since word, my love. You are the reason I am strong– that I am even here building this life. It has always been you, Rio.” 

Lips pressed against hers again until Rio chuckled against her. 

“And what is so funny?” Agatha asked softly, seeing the smirk on her wife’s face. 

Rio chuckled softly, “You say I am the lovergirl, but Dr. Agatha Harkness, the Purple Witch of the Literature Department, just spoke a love confession to a group of journalists and now the whole university can see it.” 

Agatha rolled her eyes, “Don’t make me take it back.” 

Her wife placed another kiss to the corner of her lips, “You wouldn’t. You love me too much.” 

She didn’t deny it, just smiled like a love stricken full at her wife, who smiled back at her. Their lips met again, pressing together like puzzle pieces as their noses brushed tentatively. A hand cupped her cheek tilting their heads slightly when teeth grazed her bottom lip, playfully nipping at her and making Agatha pull away with a gasp. 

“Rio,” she breathed, already feeling her skin start to heat up as her wife rubbed her belly more. “We can’t. You need to heal that wound.” 

Her wife raised an eyebrow, “You were just eye-fucking me before I sat down.” 

“I was not eye-fucking you. I was admiring what is mine.” 

“Same thing.” 

“Rio, I’m pregnant and hormonal, and your sweatpants were hanging onto your hips for dear life.” 

Her wife only wiggled her eyebrows at her, making her laugh as she rested her head against her wife’s shoulder. A comfortable silence filled between them as her wife’s hand never strayed, feeling their son push against her.

She moved the laptop off of Rio’s legs, cuddling closer without disturbing her side.

“I missed us,” Agatha murmured barely above a breath. 

Rio hummed, “We didn’t leave or break, mi amor. Just evolved like how caterpillars transform. Sometimes we need a dormant stage not only to heal, but to grow into something more beautiful.” 

Agatha held back her tears again. Damn Rio and her weird times she was good at words. 

“He’s so lucky to have you,” she said instead of responding. 

Her wife kissed her head, her hand brushing the hairs from her face before resting back on her stomach, “He’s lucky to have you, mi amor. ” 

“But–” 

“But nothing, mi amor. We are only human, not perfect cosmic beings and even then I think they mess up too– we can only do our best, and that’s what will matter to him in the end.” 



 

Outside, the night crept in like a sigh, wrapping its velvet arms around the quiet house. The Harkness-Vidals moved through it with their usual, sacred ease. In the kitchen, they cooked together– slowly as Agatha sat when her feet ached but traded places with her wife the moment something needed lifted. Their bodies moved like twin moons in orbit, mirroring each other with ease as their hands brushed, their smiles only blooming for the other, and kisses were exchanged between moments of rice gently steaming on the stove. Rio lit candles in the dining room, their golden glow pooling across the table like sunlight trapped in wax. She arranged azaleas in a vase, their petals flushed with memory and meaning. 

Later, when the dishes had been cleared and the world had grown quieter, they met under the cascading warmth of the shower. Steam curled around them, its own tender embrace as Agatha guided them so the water wouldn’t hit right on Rio’s stitches. Yet, her wife pressed up against her back, skin on skin– Rio’s hand over the life they created together. And it was there they acknowledged how they would burn the world for each other if it brought a moment of comfort for the other. 

 

 

 

 

I can only do my best, I do not do this for myself 

I’d walk through hell on living feet for you 

 

I would burn the world to bring some heat to you 

I would burn the world to bring some heat to you 

I would burn the world to bring some heat


Hymn to Virgil ~ Hozier

 

 

 

Chapter End Notes

See, it shouldn't have destroyed you. they're fine. The baby is fine.

just a bumpy ride.

next up : will Wanda get her karma ? How will Rio's birthday go two weeks later (the writing demon will come to life so yes, that means smut)

💚💜💚💜

Chapter 25: Her Protectrice

Chapter Summary

Did someone order a bus for Wanda Maximoff ?

Chapter Notes

okay... so i don't like this chapter, but like Visadero said, post it and then go to bed LOL

sorry, no smut, but next chapter, smut galore
